The SemideпђБnite Case

If ќ£ is positive deпђБnite, an induction argument veriпђБes that the quantity
inside the square root in (2.31) is strictly positive so the Aii are nonzero. This
ensures that (2.30) does not entail division by zero and that the algorithm in
Figure 2.16 runs to completion.
    If, however, ќ£ is merely positive semideпђБnite, then it is rank deпђБcient. It
follows that any matrix A satisfying AA = ќ£ must also be rank deпђБcient; for
if A had full rank, then ќ£ would too. If A is lower triangular and rank deпђБcient,
at least one element of the diagonal of A must be zero. (The determinant of a
triangular matrix is the product of its diagonal elements, and the determinant
of A is zero if A is singular.) Thus, for semideпђБnite ќ£, any attempt at Cholesky
factorization must produce some Ajj = 0 and thus an error in (2.31) and the
algorithm in Figure 2.16.
    From a purely mathematical perspective, the problem is easily solved by
making the jth column of A identically zero if Ajj = 0. This can be deduced
from the system of equations (2.29): the пђБrst element of the jth column of A
encountered in this sequence of equations is the diagonal entry; if Ajj = 0, all
subsequent equations for the jth column of ќ£ may be solved with Aij = 0. In
the factorization algorithm of Figure 2.16, this is accomplished by inserting
                                                вИЪ
вАЬif vj > 0вАЭ before the statement вАЬAij вЖР vi / vj .вАЭ Thus, if vj = 0, the entry
Aij is left at its initial value of zero.
    In practice, this solution may be problematic because it involves checking
whether an intermediate calculation (vj ) is exactly zero, making the modiпђБed
algorithm extremely sensitive to round-oпђА error.
    Rather than blindly subjecting a singular covariance matrix to Cholesky
factorization, it is therefore preferable to use the structure of the covariance
matrix to reduce the problem to one of full rank. If X вИЉ N (0, ќ£) and the d √Ч d
matrix ќ£ has rank k < d, it is possible to express all d components of X as
linear combinations of just k of the components, these k components having a
covariance matrix of rank k. In other words, it is possible to пђБnd a subvector
XћГ = (Xi1 , . . . , Xik ) and a d √Ч k matrix D such that DXћГ вИЉ N (0, ќ£) and for
which the covariance matrix ќ£ћГ of XћГ has full rank k. Cholesky factorization
can then be applied to ќ£ћГ to пђБnd AћГ satisfying AћГAћГ = ќ£ћГ. The full vector X
can be sampled by setting X = DAћГZ, Z вИЉ N (0, I).
    Singular covariance matrices often arise from factor models in which a
vector of length d is determined by k < d sources of uncertainty (factors).
In this case, the prescription above reduces to using knowledge of the factor
structure to generate X.

Eigenvector Factorization and Principal Components

The equation AA = ќ£ can also be solved by diagonalizing ќ£. As a symmetric
d √Ч d matrix, ќ£ has d real eigenvalues ќї1 , . . . , ќїd , and because ќ£ must be
                                   2.3 Normal Random Variables and Vectors          75

positive deпђБnite or semideпђБnite the ќїi are nonnegative. Furthermore, ќ£ has an
associated orthonormal set of eigenvectors {v1 , . . . , vd }; i.e., vectors satisfying

                 vi vi = 1,   vi vj = 0,   j = i,    i, j = 1, . . . , d,

and
                                      ќ£vi = ќїi vi .
It follows that ќ£ = V ќЫV , where V is the orthogonal matrix (V V  = I)
                               

with columns v1 , . . . , vd and ќЫ is the diagonal matrix with diagonal entries
ќї1 , . . . , ќїd . Hence, if we choose
                                        п£ЂвИЪ                  п£ґ
                                          ќї1 вИЪ
                                        п£ђ      ќї2           п£Ј
                                        п£ђ                   п£Ј
                         A = V ќЫ1/2 = V п£ђ         .         п£Ј,           (2.32)
                                        п£≠           ..      п£Є
                                                       вИЪ
                                                         ќїd

then
                                   AA = V ќЫV  = ќ£.
Methods for calculating V and ќЫ are included in many mathematical software
libraries and discussed in detail in Golub and Van Loan [162].
    Unlike the Cholesky factor, the matrix A in (2.32) has no particular struc-
ture providing a computational advantage in evaluating AZ, nor is this matrix
faster to compute than the Cholesky factorization. The eigenvectors and eigen-
values of a covariance matrix do however have a statistical interpretation that
is occasionally useful. We discuss this interpretation next.
    If X вИЉ N (0, ќ£) and Z вИЉ N (0, I), then generating X as AZ for any choice
of A means setting

                          X = a1 Z 1 + a2 Z 2 + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + ad Z d

where aj is the jth column of A. We may interpret the Zj as independent
factors driving the components of X, with Aij the вАЬfactor loadingвАЭ of Zj on
Xi . If ќ£ has rank 1, then X may be represented as a1 Z1 for some vector a1 ,
and in this case a single factor suпђГces to represent X. If ќ£ has rank k, then
k factors Z1 , . . . , Zk suпђГce.
    If ќ£ has full rank and AA = ќ£, then A must have full rank and X = AZ
implies Z = BX with B = AвИТ1 . Thus, the factors Zj are themselves linear
combinations of the Xi . In the special case of A given in (2.32), we have

                                    AвИТ1 = ќЫвИТ1/2 V                              (2.33)

because V  V = I (V is orthogonal). It follows that Zj is proportional to
vj X, where vj is the jth column of V and thus an eigenvector of ќ£.
    The factors Zj constructed proportional to the vj X are optimal in a
precise sense. Suppose we want to пђБnd the best single-factor approximation
76     2 Generating Random Numbers and Random Variables

to X; i.e., the linear combination w X that best captures the variability of
the components of X. A standard notion of optimality chooses w to maximize
the variance of w X, which is given by w ќ£w. Since this variance can be
made arbitrarily large by multiplying any w by a constant, it makes sense to
impose a normalization through a constraint of the form w w = 1. We are
thus led to the problem
                                 max w ќ£w.
                                w:w  w=1

If the eigenvalues of ќ£ are ordered so that

                              ќї1 вЙ• ќї2 вЙ• ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј вЙ• ќїd ,

then this optimization problem is solved by v1 , as is easily veriпђБed by ap-
pending the constraint with a Lagrange multiplier and diпђАerentiating. (This
optimality property of eigenvectors is sometimes called RayleighвАЩs principle.)
The problem of пђБnding the next best factor orthogonal to the пђБrst reduces to
solving
                                max        w ќ£w.
                            w:w  w=1,w  v1 =0

This optimization problem is solved by v2 . More generally, the best k-factor
approximation chooses factors proportional to v1 X, v2 X, . . . , vk X. Since

                                   vj ќ£vj = ќїj ,

normalizing the vj X to construct unit-variance factors yields

                                         1 
                                Zj =        vj X,
                                         ќїj

which coincides with (2.33). The transformation X = AZ recovering X from
the Zj is precisely the A in (2.32).
    The optimality of this representation can be recast in the following way.
Suppose that we are given X and that we want to пђБnd vectors a1 , . . . , ak in
d and unit-variance random variables Z1 , . . . , Zk in order to approximate X
by a1 Z1 + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + ak Zk . For any k = 1, . . . , d, the mean square approximation
error                                       
                               k
                     E X вИТ        ai Z i  ,
                                           2
                                                     (x2 = x x)
                             i=1

is minimized вИЪ by taking the ai to be the columns of A in (2.32) and setting
Zi = vi X/ ќїi .
    In the statistical literature, the linear combinations vj X are called the
principal components of X (see, e.g., Seber [325]). We may thus say that the
principal components provide an optimal lower-dimensional approximation to
a random vector. The variance explained by the пђБrst k principal components
is the ratio
                              2.3 Normal Random Variables and Vectors       77

                               ќї1 + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + ќїk
                                                       ;                (2.34)
                          ќї1 + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + ќїk + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + ќїd
in particular, the пђБrst principal component is chosen to explain as much vari-
ance as possible. In simulation applications, generating X from its principal
components (i.e., using (2.32)) is sometimes useful in designing variance re-
duction techniques. In some cases, the principal components interpretation
suggests that variance reduction should focus пђБrst on Z1 , then on Z2 , and so
on. We will see examples of this in Chapter 4 and related ideas in Section 5.5.
3
Generating Sample Paths




This chapter develops methods for simulating paths of a variety of stochastic
processes important in пђБnancial engineering. The emphasis in this chapter is
on methods for exact simulation of continuous-time processes at a discrete
set of dates. The methods are exact in the sense that the joint distribution of
the simulated values coincides with the joint distribution of the continuous-
time process on the simulation time grid. Exact methods rely on special fea-
tures of a model and are generally available only for models that oпђАer some
tractability. More complex models must ordinarily be simulated through, e.g.,
discretization of stochastic diпђАerential equations, as discussed in Chapter 6.
    The examples covered in this chapter are arranged roughly in increasing or-
der of complexity. We begin with methods for simulating Brownian motion in
one dimension or multiple dimensions and extend these to geometric Brownian
motion. We then consider Gaussian interest rate models. Our пђБrst real break
from Gaussian processes comes in Section 3.4, where we treat square-root dif-
fusions. Section 3.5 considers processes with jumps as models of asset prices.
Sections 3.6 and 3.7 treat substantially more complex models than the rest of
the chapter; these are interest rate models that describe the term structure
through a curve or vector of forward rates. Exact simulation of these models is
generally infeasible; we have included them here because of their importance
in пђБnancial engineering and because they illustrate some of the complexities
of the use of simulation for derivatives pricing.


3.1 Brownian Motion
3.1.1 One Dimension

By a standard one-dimensional Brownian motion on [0, T ], we a mean a sto-
chastic process {W (t), 0 вЙ§ t вЙ§ T } with the following properties:
(i)   W (0) = 0;
80      3 Generating Sample Paths

(ii) the mapping t вЖТ W (t) is, with probability 1, a continuous function on
      [0, T ];
(iii) the increments {W (t1 ) вИТ W (t0 ), W (t2 ) вИТ W (t1 ), . . . , W (tk ) вИТ W (tkвИТ1 )}
      are independent for any k and any 0 вЙ§ t0 < t1 < ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј < tk вЙ§ T ;
(iv) W (t) вИТ W (s) вИЉ N (0, t вИТ s) for any 0 вЙ§ s < t вЙ§ T .
In (iv) it would suпђГce to require that W (t) вИТ W (s) have mean 0 and variance
t вИТ s; that its distribution is in fact normal follows from the continuity of
sample paths in (ii) and the independent increments property (iii). We include
the condition of normality in (iv) because it is central to our discussion. A
consequence of (i) and (iv) is that
                                   W (t) вИЉ N (0, t),                              (3.1)
for 0 < t вЙ§ T .
    For constants ¬µ and ѕГ > 0, we call a process X(t) a Brownian motion with
drift ¬µ and diпђАusion coeпђГcient ѕГ 2 (abbreviated X вИЉ BM(¬µ, ѕГ 2 )) if
                                X(t) вИТ ¬µt
                                   ѕГ
is a standard Brownian motion. Thus, we may construct X from a standard
Brownian motion W by setting
                                X(t) = ¬µt + ѕГW (t).
It follows that X(t) вИЉ N (¬µt, ѕГ 2 t). Moreover, X solves the stochastic diпђАeren-
tial equation (SDE)
                           dX(t) = ¬µ dt + ѕГ dW (t).
The assumption that X(0) = 0 is a natural normalization, but we may con-
struct a Brownian motion with parameters ¬µ and ѕГ 2 and initial value x by
simply adding x to each X(t).
    For deterministic but time-varying ¬µ(t) and ѕГ(t) > 0, we may deпђБne a

Random Walk Construction

In discussing the simulation of Brownian motion, we mostly focus on simulat-
ing values (W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn )) or (X(t1 ), . . . , X(tn )) at a пђБxed set of points
0 < t1 < ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј < tn . Because Brownian motion has independent normally dis-
tributed increments, simulating the W (ti ) or X(ti ) from their increments is
straightforward. Let Z1 , . . . , Zn be independent standard normal random vari-
ables, generated using any of the methods in Section 2.3.2, for example. For
a standard Brownian motion set t0 = 0 and W (0) = 0. Subsequent values can
be generated as follows:

             W (ti+1 ) = W (ti ) +     ti+1 вИТ ti Zi+1 ,    i = 0, . . . , n вИТ 1.        (3.2)

For X вИЉ BM(¬µ, ѕГ 2 ) with constant ¬µ and ѕГ and given X(0), set

 X(ti+1 ) = X(ti ) + ¬µ(ti+1 вИТ ti ) + ѕГ      ti+1 вИТ ti Zi+1 ,     i = 0, . . . , n вИТ 1. (3.3)

With time-dependent coeпђГcients, the recursion becomes
                                +
                         ti+1                 ti+1
X(ti+1 ) = X(ti ) +             ¬µ(s) ds +            ѕГ 2 (u) duZi+1 ,   i = 0, . . . , n вИТ 1.
                        ti                   ti
                                                                                    (3.4)
      The methods in (3.2)вАУ(3.4) are exact in the sense that the joint distri-
bution of the simulated values (W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn )) or (X(t1 ), . . . , X(tn )) co-
incides with the joint distribution of the corresponding Brownian motion at
t1 , . . . , tn . Of course, this says nothing about what happens between the ti .
One may extend the simulated values to other time points through, e.g., piece-
wise linear interpolation; but no deterministic interpolation method will give
the extended vector the correct joint distribution. The methods in (3.2)вАУ(3.4)
are exact at the time points t1 , . . . , tn but subject to discretization error, com-
pared to a true Brownian motion, if deterministically interpolated to other
time points. Replacing (3.4) with the Euler approximation

X(ti+1 ) = X(ti ) + ¬µ(ti )(ti+1 вИТ ti ) + ѕГ(ti )      ti+1 вИТ ti Zi+1 ,    i = 0, . . . , n вИТ 1,

will in general introduce discretization error even at t1 , . . . , tn , because the
increments will no longer have exactly the right mean and variance. We return
to the topic of discretization error in Chapter 6.
    The vector (W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn )) is a linear transformation of the the vec-
tor of increments (W (t1 ), W (t2 ) вИТ W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn ) вИТ W (tnвИТ1 )). Since these
increments are independent and normally distributed, it follows from the Lin-
ear Transformation Property (2.23) that (W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn )) has a multivari-
ate normal distribution. Simulating (W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn )) is thus a special case
of the general problem, treated in Section 2.3.3, of generating multivariate
normal vectors. While the random walk construction suпђГces for most appli-
cations, it is interesting and sometimes useful to consider alternative sampling
methods.
82      3 Generating Sample Paths

   To apply any of the methods considered in Section 2.3.3, we пђБrst need
to пђБnd the mean vector and covariance matrix of (W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn )). For a
standard Brownian motion, we know from (3.1) that E[W (ti )] = 0, so the
mean vector is identically 0. For the covariance matrix, consider пђБrst any
0 < s < t < T ; using the independence of the increments we пђБnd that

       Cov[W (s), W (t)] = Cov[W (s), W (s) + (W (t) вИТ W (s))]
                          = Cov[W (s), W (s)] + Cov[W (s), W (t) вИТ W (s)]
                          = s + 0 = s.                                    (3.5)

Letting C denote the covariance matrix of (W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn )), we thus have

                                 Cij = min(ti , tj ).                            (3.6)

Cholesky Factorization

Having noted that the vector (W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn )) has the distribution N (0, C),
with C as in (3.6), we may simulate this vector as AZ, where Z = (Z1 , . . . , Zn )
вИЉ N (0, I) and A satisпђБes AA = C. The Cholesky method discussed in Sec-
tion 2.3.3 takes A to be lower triangular. For C in (3.6), the Cholesky factor
is given by              п£ЂвИЪ                                   п£ґ
                           вИЪt1 вИЪ 0           ¬Ј¬Ј¬Ј       0

(W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn )) as AZ is simply a matrix-vector representation of the

lower triangularity of A, evaluation of AZ is an O(n2 ) operation; the random
walk construction reduces this to O(n) by implicitly exploiting the fact that
the nonzero entries of each column of A are identical.

is ѕГA and we once again пђБnd that the Cholesky method coincides with the
increment recursion (3.3).

Brownian Bridge Construction

The recursion (3.2) generates the vector (W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn )) from left to right.
We may however generate the W (ti ) in any order we choose, provided that
at each step we sample from the correct conditional distribution given the
values already generated. For example, we may пђБrst generate the пђБnal value
W (tn ), then sample W (tn/2 ) conditional on the value of W (tn ), and proceed
                                                                 3.1 Brownian Motion     83

by progressively пђБlling in intermediate values. This пђВexibility can be useful in
implementing variance reduction techniques and low-discrepancy methods. It
follows from the Conditioning Formula (2.24) that the conditional distribu-
tion needed at each step is itself normal and this makes conditional sampling
feasible.
    Conditioning a Brownian motion on its endpoints produces a Brownian
bridge. Once we determine W (tn ), пђБlling in intermediate values amounts to
simulating a Brownian bridge from 0 = W (0) to W (tn ). If we next sample
W (tn/2 ), then пђБlling in values between times tn/2 and tn amounts to sim-
ulating a Brownian bridge from W (tn/2 ) to W (tn ). This approach is thus
referred to as a Brownian bridge construction.
    As a пђБrst step in developing this construction, suppose 0 < u < s < t
and consider the problem of generating W (s) conditional on W (u) = x and
W (t) = y. We use the Conditioning Formula (2.24) to пђБnd the conditional
distribution of W (s). We know from (3.5) that the unconditional distribution
is given by             п£Ђ        п£ґ      п£Ђ п£Ђ         п£ґп£ґ
                           W (u)              uuu
                        п£≠ W (s) п£Є вИЉ N п£≠0, п£≠ u s s п£Єп£Є .
                           W (t)              us t
The Conditioning Formula (2.24) gives the distribution of the second compo-
nent of a partitioned vector conditional on a value of the пђБrst component. We
want to apply this formula to пђБnd the distribution of W (s) conditional on the
value of (W (u), W (t)). We therefore пђБrst permute the entries of the vector to
get                    п£Ђ        п£ґ      п£Ђ п£Ђ         п£ґп£ґ
                          W (s)               sus
                       п£≠ W (u) п£Є вИЉ N п£≠0, п£≠ u u u п£Єп£Є .
                          W (t)               su t
We now пђБnd from the Conditioning Formula that, given (W (u) = x, W (t) =

               E[W (s)|W (u) = x, W (t) = y] =

In particular, the conditional mean (3.7) is obtained by linearly interpolating

   Suppose, more generally, that the values W (s1 ) = x1 , W (s2 ) = x2 , . . . ,
W (sk ) = xk of the Brownian path have been determined at the times s1 <
s2 < ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј < sk and that we wish to sample W (s) conditional on these values.
Suppose that si < s < si+1 . Then

     (W (s)|W (sj ) = xj , j = 1, . . . , k) = (W (s)|W (si ) = xi , W (si+1 ) = xi+1 ),

in the sense that the two conditional distributions are the same. This can again
be derived from the Conditioning Formula (2.24) but is more immediate from
the Markov property of Brownian motion (a consequence of the independent
increments property): given W (si ), W (s) is independent of all W (t) with
t < si , and given W (si+1 ) it is independent of all W (t) with t > si+1 . Thus,
conditioning on all W (sj ) is equivalent to conditioning on the values of the
Brownian path at the two times si and si+1 closest to s. Combining these
observations with (3.7) and (3.8), we пђБnd that

               (W (s)|W (s1 ) = x1 , W (s2 ) = x2 , . . . , W (sk ) = xk ) =
                     (si+1 вИТ s)xi + (s вИТ si )xi+1 (si+1 вИТ s)(s вИТ si )
                N                                ,                             .
                             (si+1 вИТ si )             (si+1 вИТ si )

This is illustrated in Figure 3.1. The conditional mean of W (s) lies on the
line segment connecting (si , xi ) and (si+1 , xi+1 ); the actual value of W (s) is
normally distributed about this mean with a variance that depends on (sвИТ si )
and (si+1 вИТ s). To sample from this conditional distribution, we may set
                                                  +
                  (si+1 вИТ s)xi + (s вИТ si )xi+1        (si+1 вИТ s)(s вИТ si )
        W (s) =                                 +                         Z,
                          (si+1 вИТ si )                    (si+1 вИТ si )

with Z вИЉ N (0, 1) independent of all W (s1 ), . . . , W (sk ).
    By repeatedly using these observations, we may indeed sample the com-
ponents of the vector (W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn )) in any order. In particular, we may
start by sampling W (tn ) from N (0, tn ) and proceed by conditionally sampling
intermediate values, at each step conditioning on the two closest time points
already sampled (possibly including W (0) = 0).
    If n is a power of 2, the construction can be arranged so that each W (ti ),
i < n, is generated conditional on the values W (t ) and W (tr ) with the
property that i is midway between  and r. Figure 3.2 details this case. If, for
example, n = 16, the algorithm starts by sampling W (t16 ); the пђБrst loop over
j samples W (t8 ); the second samples W (t4 ) and W (t12 ); the third samples
W (t2 ), W (t6 ), W (t10 ), and W (t14 ); and the пђБnal loop пђБlls in all W (ti ) with
odd i. If n is not a power of 2, the algorithm could still be applied to a subset
of 2m < n of the ti , with the remaining points пђБlled in at the end.
    Our discussion of the Brownian bridge construction (and Figure 3.2 in
particular) has considered only the case of a standard Brownian motion. How
would the construction be modiпђБed for a Brownian motion with drift ¬µ? Only

the пђБrst step вАФ sampling of the rightmost point вАФ would change. Instead
of sampling W (tm ) from N (0, tm ), we would sample it from N (¬µtm , tm ). The
conditional distribution of W (t1 ), . . . , W (tnвИТ1 ) given W (tm ) is the same for
all values of ¬µ. Put slightly diпђАerently, a Brownian bridge constructed from
a Brownian motion with drift has the same law as one constructed from a
standard Brownian motion. (For any пђБnite set of points t1 , . . . , tnвИТ1 this can
be established from the Conditioning Formula (2.24).) Hence, to include a
drift ¬µ in the algorithm of Figure 3.2, it suпђГces to change just the third
line, adding ¬µth to wh . For a Brownian motion with diпђАusion coeпђГcient ѕГ 2 ,
the conditional mean (3.7) is unchanged but the conditional variance (3.8)
is multiplied by ѕГ 2 . This could be implemented
                                             вИЪ         in Figure 3.2 by multiplying
each b by ѕГ (and setting wh вЖР ¬µth + ѕГ th Zh in the third line); alternatively,
the пђБnal vector (w1 , . . . , w2m ) could simply be multiplied by ѕГ.
    Why use a Brownian bridge construction? The algorithm in Figure 3.2 has
no computational advantage over the simple random walk recursion (3.2). Nor
does the output of the algorithm have any statistical feature not shared by
the output of (3.2); indeed, the Brownian bridge construction is valid precisely
because the distribution of the (W (t1 ), . . . , W (tm )) it produces coincides with
that resulting from (3.2). The potential advantage of the Brownian bridge
construction arises when it is used with certain variance reduction techniques
and low-discrepancy methods. We will return to this point in Section 4.3 and
Chapter 5. BrieпђВy, the Brownian bridge construction gives us greater control
over the coarse structure of the simulated Brownian path. For example, it
uses a single normal random variable to determine the endpoint of a path,
which may be the most important feature of the path; in contrast, the end-
point obtained from (3.2) is the combined result of n independent normal
random variables. The standard recursion (3.2) proceeds by evolving the path
forward through time; in contrast, the Brownian bridge construction proceeds
by adding increasingly пђБne detail to the path at each step, as illustrated in
Figure 3.3. This can be useful in focusing variance reduction techniques on
вАЬimportantвАЭ features of Brownian paths.

Principal Components Construction

As just noted, under the Brownian bridge construction a single normal random
variable (say Z1 ) determines the endpoint of the path; conditional on the
endpoint, a second normal random variable (say Z2 ) determines the midpoint
of the path, and so on. Thus, under this construction, much of the ultimate
shape of the Brownian path is determined (or explained ) by the values of just
the пђБrst few Zi . Is there a construction under which even more of the path
is determined by the пђБrst few Zi ? Is there a construction that maximizes the
variability of the path explained by Z1 , . . . , Zk for all k = 1, . . . , n?
    This optimality objective is achieved for any normal random vector by the
principal components construction discussed in Section 2.3.3. We now discuss
its application to a discrete Brownian path W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn ). It is useful to

    Consider, for example, a 32-step discrete Brownian path with equal time
increments ti+1 вИТ ti = 1/32. The corresponding covariance matrix has entries
88      3 Generating Sample Paths

Cij = min(i, j)/32, i, j = 1, . . . , 32. The magnitudes of the eigenvalues of this
matrix drop oпђА rapidly вАФ the пђБve largest are 13.380, 1.489, 0.538, 0.276, and
0.168. The variability explained by Z1 , . . . , Zk (in the sense of (2.34)) is 81%,
90%, 93%, 95%, and 96%, for k = 1, . . . , 5; it exceeds 99% at k = 16. This
indicates that although full construction of the 32-step path requires 32 normal
random variables, most of the variability of the path can be determined using
far fewer Zi .
    Figure 3.4 plots the normalized eigenvectors v1 , v2 , v3 , and v4 associated
with the four largest eigenvalues. (Each of these is a vector with 32 entries;
they are plotted against the jвИЖt, j = 1, . . . , 32, with вИЖt = 1/32.) The vi
appear to be nearly sinusoidal, with frequencies that increase with i. Indeed,
AћКkesson and Lehoczky [8] show that for an n-step path with equal spacing
ti+1 вИТ ti = вИЖt,
                          2              2i вИТ 1
               vi (j) = вИЪ       sin             jѕА ,     j = 1, . . . , n,

for i = 1, . . . , n. To contrast this with the Brownian bridge construction in
Figure 3.3, note    вИЪ that in the principal components construction the vi are
multiplied by ќїi Zi and then summed; thus, the discrete Brownian path
may be viewed    вИЪ as a random linear combination of the vectors vi , with random
coeпђГcients ќїi Zi . The coeпђГcient on vi has varianceвИЪќїi and we have seen that
the ќїi drop oпђА quickly. Thus, the пђБrst few vi (and ќїi Zi ) determine most of
the shape of the Brownian path and the later vi add high-frequency detail to
the path. As in the Brownian bridge construction, these features can be useful
in implementing variance reduction techniques by making it possible to focus
on the most important Zi . We return to this point in Sections 4.3.2 and 5.5.2.
    Although the principal components construction is optimal with respect
to explained variability, it has two drawbacks compared to the random walk
and Brownian bridge constructions. The пђБrst is that it requires O(n2 ) oper-
ations to construct W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn ) from Z1 , . . . , Zn , whereas the previous
constructions require O(n) operations. The second (potential) drawback is
that with principal components none of the W (ti ) is fully determined until
all Z1 , . . . , Zn have been processed вАФ i.e., until all terms in (3.9) have been
summed. In contrast, using either the random walk or Brownian bridge con-
struction, exactly k of the W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn ) are пђБxed by the пђБrst k normal
random variables, for all k = 1, . . . , n.

3.1.2 Multiple Dimensions

We call a process W (t) = (W1 (t), . . . , Wd (t)) , 0 вЙ§ t вЙ§ T , a standard Brown-

                        W (t) вИТ W (s) вИЉ N (0, (t вИТ s)I),
for all 0 вЙ§ s < t вЙ§ T , with I the d √Ч d identity matrix. It follows that each

    Suppose ¬µ is a vector in d and ќ£ is a d √Ч d matrix, positive deпђБnite

and independent increments with

                     X(t) вИТ X(s) вИЉ N ((t вИТ s)¬µ, (t вИТ s)ќ£).

The initial value X(0) is an arbitrary constant assumed to be 0 unless oth-
erwise speciпђБed. If B is a d √Ч k matrix satisfying BB  = ќ£ and if W is a
standard Brownian motion on k , then the process deпђБned by

                              X(t) = ¬µt + BW (t)                            (3.11)

is a BM(¬µ, ќ£). In particular, the law of X depends on B only through BB  .
    The process in (3.11) solves the SDE

                            dX(t) = ¬µ dt + B dW (t).

Random Walk Construction

Let Z1 , Z2 , . . . be independent N (0, I) random vectors in d . We can construct
a standard d-dimensional Brownian motion at times 0 = t0 < t1 < ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј < tn
by setting W (0) = 0 and

          W (ti+1 ) = W (ti ) +           ti+1 вИТ ti Zi+1 ,          i = 0, . . . , n вИТ 1.          (3.14)

This is equivalent to applying the one-dimensional random walk construction
(3.2) separately to each coordinate of W .
    To simulate X вИЉ BM(¬µ, ќ£), we пђБrst пђБnd a matrix B for which BB  = ќ£
(see Section 2.3.3). If B is d√Чk, let Z1 , Z2 , . . . be independent standard normal
random vectors in k . Set X(0) = 0 and

  X(ti+1 ) = X(ti ) + ¬µ(ti+1 вИТ ti ) +               ti+1 вИТ ti BZi ,         i = 0, . . . , n вИТ 1. (3.15)

Thus, simulation of BM(¬µ, ќ£) is straightforward once ќ£ has been factored.
For the case of time-dependent coeпђГcients, we may set
                          ti+1

thus requiring n factorizations.

Brownian Bridge Construction

Application of a Brownian bridge construction to a standard d-dimensional
Brownian motion is straightforward: we may simply apply independent one-
dimensional constructions to each of the coordinates. To include a drift vector
(i.e., for BM(¬µ, I) process), it suпђГces to add ¬µi tn to Wi (tn ) at the пђБrst step
92      3 Generating Sample Paths

of the construction of the ith coordinate, as explained in Section 3.1.1. The
rest of the construction is unaпђАected.
    To construct X вИЉ BM(¬µ, ќ£), we may use the fact that X can be rep-
resented as X(t) = ¬µt + BW (t) with B a d √Ч k matrix, k вЙ§ d, satisfy-
ing BB  = ќ£, and W a standard k-dimensional Brownian motion. We may
then apply a Brownian bridge construction to W (t1 ), . . . , W (tn ) and recover
X(t1 ), . . . , X(tn ) through a linear transformation.

Principal Components Construction

As with the Brownian bridge construction, one could apply a one-dimensional
principal components construction to each coordinate of a multidimensional
Brownian motion. Through a linear transformation this then extends to the
construction of BM(¬µ, ќ£). However, the optimality of principal components is
lost in this reduction; to recover it, we must work directly with the covariance
matrix of (3.13).
    It follows from (3.12) that the covariance matrix of (3.13) can be repre-
sented as (C вКЧ ќ£), where вКЧ denotes the Kronecker product producing
                                 п£Ђ                          п£ґ
                                    C11 ќ£ C12 ќ£ ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј C1n ќ£
                                 п£ђ C21 ќ£ C22 ќ£ ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј C2n ќ£ п£Ј
                                 п£ђ                          п£Ј
                     (C вКЧ ќ£) = п£ђ .          .. . .      . п£Ј.
                                 п£≠ ..        .      . .. п£Є
                                       Cn1 ќ£ Cn2 ќ£ ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј Cnn ќ£

If C has eigenvectors v1 , . . . , vn and eigenvalues ќї1 вЙ• ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј вЙ• ќїn , and if ќ£
has eigenvectors w1 , . . . , wd and eigenvalues ќЈ1 вЙ• ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј вЙ• ќЈd , then (C вКЧ ќ£)
has eigenvectors (vi вКЧ wj ) and eigenvalues ќїi ќЈj , i = 1, . . . , n, j = 1, √Ч, d. This
special structure of the covariance matrix of (3.12) makes it possible to reduce
the computational eпђАort required to пђБnd all eigenvalues and eigenvectors from
the O((nd)3 ) typically required for an (nd √Ч nd) matrix to O(n3 + d3 ).

                       (ќїi ќЈj )(1) вЙ• (ќїi ќЈj )(2) вЙ• ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј (ќїi ќЈj )(nd) ,

then for any k = 1, . . . , n,
                             $k                 $k

In other words, the variability explained by the пђБrst k factors is always smaller
for a d-dimensional Brownian motion than it would be for a scalar Brown-

dimensional process has greater total variability.
                                          3.2 Geometric Brownian Motion        93

3.2 Geometric Brownian Motion

A stochastic process S(t) is a geometric Brownian motion if log S(t) is a
Brownian motion with initial value log S(0); in other words, a geometric
Brownian motion is simply an exponentiated Brownian motion. Accordingly,
all methods for simulating Brownian motion become methods for simulating
geometric Brownian motion through exponentiation. This section therefore
focuses more on modeling than on algorithmic issues.
    Geometric Brownian motion is the most fundamental model of the value
of a пђБnancial asset. In his pioneering thesis of 1900, Louis Bachelier developed
a model of stock prices that in retrospect we describe as ordinary Brownian
motion, though the mathematics of Brownian motion had not yet been de-
veloped. The use of geometric Brownian motion as a model in пђБnance is due
primarily to work of Paul Samuelson in the 1960s. Whereas ordinary Brown-
ian motion can take negative values вАФ an undesirable feature in a model of
the price of a stock or any other limited liability asset вАФ geometric Brownian
motion is always positive because the exponential function takes only positive
values. More fundamentally, for geometric Brownian motion the percentage
changes
               S(t2 ) вИТ S(t1 ) S(t3 ) вИТ S(t2 )       S(tn ) вИТ S(tnвИТ1 )
                              ,                ,...,                       (3.16)
                   S(t1 )          S(t2 )                S(tnвИТ1 )
are independent for t1 < t2 < ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј < tn , rather than the absolute changes
S(ti+1 ) вИТ S(ti ). These properties explain the centrality of geometric rather
than ordinary Brownian motion in modeling asset prices.

3.2.1 Basic Properties

Suppose W is a standard Brownian motion and X satisпђБes

                           dX(t) = ¬µ dt + ѕГ dW (t),

so that X вИЉ BM(¬µ, ѕГ 2 ). If we set S(t) = S(0) exp(X(t)) вЙ° f (X(t)), then an
application of ItoћВвАЩs formula shows that

       dS(t) = f (X(t)) dX(t) + 12 ѕГ 2 f (X(t)) dt
             = S(0) exp(X(t))[¬µ dt + ѕГ dW (t)] + 12 ѕГ 2 S(0) exp(X(t)) dt
             = S(t)(¬µ + 12 ѕГ 2 ) dt + S(t)ѕГ dW (t).                         (3.17)

In contrast, a geometric Brownian motion process is often speciпђБed through
an SDE of the form
                          dS(t)
                                = ¬µ dt + ѕГ dW (t),                   (3.18)
                           S(t)
an expression suggesting a Brownian model of the вАЬinstantaneous returnsвАЭ
dS(t)/S(t). Comparison of (3.17) and (3.18) indicates that the models are
94      3 Generating Sample Paths

inconsistent and reveals an ambiguity in the role of вАЬ¬µ.вАЭ In (3.17), ¬µ is the
drift of the Brownian motion we exponentiated to deпђБne S(t) вАФ the drift of
log S(t). In (3.18), S(t) has drift ¬µS(t) and (3.18) implies

                      d log S(t) = (¬µ вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 ) dt + ѕГ dW (t),                (3.19)

as can be veriпђБed through ItoћВвАЩs formula or comparison with (3.17).
    We will use the notation S вИЉ GBM(¬µ, ѕГ2 ) to indicate that S is a process of
the type in (3.18). We will refer to ¬µ in (3.18) as the drift parameter though
it is not the drift of either S(t) or log S(t). We refer to ѕГ in (3.18) as the
volatility parameter of S(t); the diпђАusion coeпђГcient of S(t) is ѕГ 2 S 2 (t).
    From (3.19) we see that if S вИЉ GBM(¬µ, ѕГ2 ) and if S has initial value S(0),
then                                                      
                    S(t) = S(0) exp [¬µ вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 ]t + ѕГW (t) .                (3.20)
A bit more generally, if u < t then
                                                             
       S(t) = S(u) exp [¬µ вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 ](t вИТ u) + ѕГ(W (t) вИТ W (u)) ,               (3.21)

from which the claimed independence of the returns in (3.16) becomes ev-
ident. Moreover, since the increments of W are independent and normally
distributed, this provides a simple recursive procedure for simulating values
of S at 0 = t0 < t1 < ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј < tn :
                             
        S(ti+1 ) = S(ti ) exp [¬µ вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 ](ti+1 вИТ ti ) + ѕГ ti+1 вИТ ti Zi+1 , (3.22)
                   i = 0, 1, . . . , n вИТ 1,

with Z1 , Z2 , . . . , Zn independent standard normals. In fact, (3.22) is equivalent
to exponentiating both sides of (3.3) with ¬µ replaced by ¬µвИТ 12 ѕГ 2 . This method
is exact in the sense that the (S(t1 ), . . . , S(tn )) it produces has the joint dis-
tribution of the process S вИЉ GBM(¬µ, ѕГ2 ) at t1 , . . . , tn вАФ the method involves
no discretization error. Time-dependent parameters can be incorporated by
exponentiating both sides of (3.4).

Lognormal Distribution

From (3.20) we see that if S вИЉ GBM(¬µ, ѕГ2 ), then the marginal distribution
of S(t) is that of the exponential of a normal random variable, which is called
a lognormal distribution. We write Y вИЉ LN (¬µ, ѕГ 2 ) if the random variable Y
has the distribution of exp(¬µ + ѕГZ), Z вИЉ N (0, 1). This distribution is thus
given by

                       P (Y вЙ§ y) = P (Z вЙ§ [log(y) вИТ ¬µ]/ѕГ)
                                       log(y) вИТ ¬µ
                                 =ќ¶
                                           ѕГ
and its density by
                                              3.2 Geometric Brownian Motion          95

                                  1      log(y) вИТ ¬µ
                                    ѕЖ                    .                        (3.23)
                                 yѕГ          ѕГ
   Moments of a lognormal random variable can be calculated using the basic
identity
                                                 1   2
                                     E[eaZ ] = e 2 a
for the moment generating function of a standard normal. From this it follows
that Y вИЉ LN (¬µ, ѕГ 2 ) has
                             1   2                           2
                                                                  2
                 E[Y ] = e¬µ+ 2 ѕГ ,      Var[Y ] = e2¬µ+ѕГ           eѕГ вИТ 1 ;

in particular, the notation Y вИЉ LN (¬µ, ѕГ 2 ) does not imply that ¬µ and ѕГ 2 are
the mean and variance of Y . From

                          P (Y вЙ§ e¬µ ) = P (Z вЙ§ 0) = 12

we see that e¬µ is the median of Y . The mean of Y is thus larger than the

then (S(t)/S(0)) вИЉ LN ([¬µ вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 ]t, ѕГ 2 t) and

           E[S(t)] = e¬µt S(0), Var[S(t)] = e2¬µt S 2 (0) eѕГ t вИТ 1 .

In fact, we have

     E[S(t)|S(ѕД ), 0 вЙ§ ѕД вЙ§ u] = E[S(t)|S(u)] = e¬µ(tвИТu) S(u),             u < t,   (3.24)

and an analogous expression for the conditional variance. The пђБrst equality
in (3.24) is the Markov property (which follows from the fact that S is a one-
to-one transformation of a Brownian motion, itself a Markov process) and the
second follows from (3.21).
    Equation (3.24) indicates that ¬µ acts as an average growth rate for S, a
sort of average continuously compounded rate of return. Along a single sample
path of S the picture is diпђАerent. For a standard Brownian motion W , we have
tвИТ1 W (t) вЖТ 0 with probability 1. For S вИЉ GBM(¬µ, ѕГ2 ), we therefore пђБnd that
                              1
                                log S(t) вЖТ ¬µ вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 ,
                              t
with probability 1, so ¬µвИТ 12 ѕГ 2 serves as the growth rate along each path. If this
expression is positive, S(t) вЖТ вИЮ as t вЖТ вИЮ; if it is negative, then S(t) вЖТ 0.
In a model with ¬µ > 0 > ¬µ вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 , we пђБnd from (3.24) that E[S(t)] grows
exponentially although S(t) converges to 0. This seemingly pathological be-
havior is explained by the increasing skew in the distribution of S(t): although
S(t) вЖТ 0, rare but very large values of S(t) are suпђГciently likely to produce
an increasing mean.

3.2.2 Path-Dependent Options

Our interest in simulating paths of geometric Brownian motion lies primarily
in pricing options, particularly those whose payoпђАs depend on the path of an
underlying asset S and not simply its value S(T ) at a пђБxed exercise date T .
Through the principles of option pricing developed in Chapter 1, the price of
an option may be represented as an expected discounted payoпђА. This price
is estimated through simulation by generating paths of the underlying asset,
evaluating the discounted payoпђА on each path, and averaging over paths.

Risk-Neutral Dynamics

The one subtlety in this framework is the probability measure with respect to
which the expectation is taken and the nearly equivalent question of how the
payoпђА should be discounted. This bears on how the paths of the underlying
asset ought to be generated and more speciпђБcally in the case of geometric
Brownian motion, how the drift parameter ¬µ should be chosen.
    We start by assuming the existence of a constant continuously compounded
interest rate r for riskless borrowing and lending. A dollar invested at this rate
at time 0 grows to a value of
                                    ќ≤(t) = ert
at time t. Similarly, a contract paying one dollar at a future time t (a zero-
coupon bond) has a value at time 0 of eвИТrt . In pricing under the risk-neutral
measure, we discount a payoпђА to be received at time t back to time 0 by
dividing by ќ≤(t); i.e., ќ≤ is the numeraire asset.
    Suppose the asset S pays no dividends; then, under the risk-neutral mea-
sure, the discounted price process S(t)/ќ≤(t) is a martingale:
                                                          
                      S(u)         S(t)
                             =E         |{S(ѕД ), 0 вЙ§ ѕД вЙ§ u} .           (3.25)
                      ќ≤(u)         ќ≤(t)

Comparison with (3.24) shows that if S is a geometric Brownian motion under
the risk-neutral measure, then it must have ¬µ = r; i.e.,

                            dS(t)
                                  = r dt + ѕГ dW (t).                       (3.26)
                             S(t)

As discussed in Section 1.2.2, this equation helps explain the name вАЬrisk-
neutral.вАЭ In a world of risk-neutral investors, all assets would have the same
average rate of return вАФ investors would not demand a higher rate of return
for holding risky assets. In a risk-neutral world, the drift parameter for S(t)
would therefore equal the risk-free rate r.
    In the case of an asset that pays dividends, we know from Section 1.2.2 that
the martingale property (3.25) continues to hold but with S replaced by the
sum of S, any dividends paid by S, and any interest earned from investing the
                                         3.2 Geometric Brownian Motion        97

dividends at the risk-free rate r. Thus, let D(t) be the value of any dividends
paid over [0, t] and interest earned on those dividends. Suppose the asset pays
a continuous dividend yield of ќі, meaning that it pays dividends at rate ќіS(t)
at time t. Then D grows at rate
                            dD(t)
                                  = ќіS(t) + rD(t),
                             dt
the пђБrst term on the right reпђВecting the inпђВux of new dividends and the
second term reпђВecting interest earned on dividends already accumulated. If
S вИЉ GBM(¬µ, ѕГ 2 ), then the drift in (S(t) + D(t)) is

                            ¬µS(t) + ќіS(t) + rD(t).

The martingale property (3.25), now applied to the combined process (S(t) +
D(t)), requires that this drift equal r(S(t) + D(t)). We must therefore have
¬µ + ќі = r; i.e., ¬µ = r вИТ ќі. The net eпђАect of a dividend yield is to reduce the
growth rate by ќі.
   We discuss some speciпђБc settings in which this formulation is commonly
used:
вЧ¶ Equity Indices. In pricing index options, the level of the index is often
  modeled as geometric Brownian motion. An index is not an asset and it
  does not pay dividends, but the individual stocks that make up an index
  may pay dividends and this aпђАects the level of the index. Because an index
  may contain many stocks paying a wide range of dividends on diпђАerent
  dates, the combined eпђАect is often approximated by a continuous dividend
  yield ќі.
вЧ¶ Exchange Rates. In pricing currency options, the relevant underlying vari-
  able is an exchange rate. We may think of an exchange rate S (quoted as
  the number of units of domestic currency per unit of foreign currency) as
  the price of the foreign currency. A unit of foreign currency earns interest at
  some risk-free rate rf , and this interest may be viewed as a dividend stream.
  Thus, in modeling an exchange rate using geometric Brownian motion, we
  set ¬µ = r вИТ rf .
вЧ¶ Commodities. A physical commodity like gold or oil may in some cases
  behave like an asset that pays negative dividends because of the cost of
  storing the commodity. This is easily accommodated in the setting above
  by taking ќі < 0. There may, however, be some value in holding a physical
  commodity; for example, a party storing oil implicitly holds an option to sell
  or consume the oil in case of a shortage. This type of beneпђБt is sometimes
  approximated through a hypothetical convenience yield that accrues from
  physical storage. The net dividend yield in this case is the diпђАerence between
  the convenience yield and the cost rate for storage.
вЧ¶ Futures Contracts. A futures contract commits the holder to buying an
  underlying asset or commodity at a пђБxed price at a пђБxed date in the future.
  The futures price is the price specifed in a futures contract at which both
98        3 Generating Sample Paths

     the buyer and the seller would willingly enter into the contract without
     either party paying the other. A futures price is thus not the price of an
     asset but rather a price agreed upon for a transaction in the future.
     Let S(t) denote the price of the underlying asset (the spot price) and let
     F (t, T ) denote the futures prices at time t for a contract to be settled at a
     пђБxed time T in the future. Entering into a futures contract at time t to buy
     the underlying asset at time T > t is equivalent to committing to exchange
     a known amount F (t, T ) for an uncertain amount S(T ). For this contract
     to have zero value at the inception time t entails

                         0 = eвИТr(T вИТt) E[(S(T ) вИТ F (t, T ))|Ft ],           (3.27)

     where Ft is the history of market prices up to time t. At t = T the spot
     and futures prices must agree, so S(T ) = F (T, T ) and we may rewrite this
     condition as
                              F (t, T ) = E[F (T, T )|Ft].
     Thus, the futures price is a martingale (in its пђБrst argument) under the
     risk-neutral measure. It follows that if we choose to model a futures price
     (for пђБxed maturity T ) using geometric Brownian motion, we should set its
     drift parameter to zero:

                                 dF (t, T )
                                            = ѕГ dW (t).
                                 F (t, T )

     Comparison of (3.27) and (3.25) reveals that

                              F (t, T ) = e(rвИТќі)(T вИТt) S(t),

     with ќі the net dividend yield for S. If either process is a geometric Brownian
     motion under the risk-neutral measure then the other is as well and they
     have the same volatility ѕГ.
     This discussion blurs the distinction between futures and forward contracts.
     The argument leading to (3.27) applies more speciпђБcally to a forward price
     because a forward contract involves no intermediate cashпђВows. The holder
     of a futures contract typically makes or receives payments each day through
     a margin account; the discussion above ignores these cashпђВows. In a world
     with deterministic interest rates, futures and forward prices must be equal
     to preclude arbitrage so the conclusion in (3.27) is valid for both. With
     stochastic interest rates, it turns out that futures prices continue to be
     martingales under the risk-neutral measure but forward prices do not. The
     theoretical relation between futures and forward prices is investigated in
     Cox, Ingersoll, and Ross [90]; it is also discussed in many texts on derivative
     securities (e.g., Hull [189]).
                                               3.2 Geometric Brownian Motion              99

Path-Dependent PayoпђАs
We turn now to some examples of path-dependent payoпђАs frequently encoun-
tered in option pricing. We focus primarily on cases in which the payoпђА de-
pends on the values S(t1 ), . . . , S(tn ) at a пђБxed set of dates t1 , . . . , tn ; for these
it is usually possible to produce an unbiased simulation estimate of the op-
tion price. An option payoпђА could in principle depend on the complete path
{S(t), 0 вЙ§ t вЙ§ T } over an interval [0, T ]; pricing such an option by simula-
tion will often entail some discretization bias. In the examples that follow,
we distinguish between discrete and continuous monitoring of the underlying
asset.
вЧ¶ Asian option: discrete monitoring. An Asian option is an option on a time
  average of the underlying asset. Asian calls and puts have payoпђАs (SћД вИТ K)+
  and (K вИТ SћД)+ respectively, where the strike price K is a constant and

                                             1
                                                n
                                      SћД =         S(ti )                            (3.28)
                                             n i=1

  is the average price of the underlying asset over the discrete set of mon-
  itoring dates t1 , . . . , tn . Other examples have payoпђАs (S(T ) вИТ SћД)+ and
  (SћД вИТ S(T ))+ . There are no exact formulas for the prices of these options,
  largely because the distribution of SћД is intractable.
вЧ¶ Asian option: continuous monitoring. The continuous counterparts of the
  discrete Asian options replace the discrete average above with the continu-
  ous average                                  t
                                          1

  over an interval [u, t]. Though more diпђГcult to simulate, some instances

  analysis of Geman and Yor [135] and the eigenfunction expansion of Linet-
  sky [237].

вЧ¶ Barrier options. A typical example of a barrier option is one that gets
  вАЬknocked outвАЭ if the underlying asset crosses a prespeciпђБed level. For in-
  stance, a down-and-out call with barrier b, strike K, and expiration T has
  payoпђА
                           1{ѕД (b) > T }(S(T ) вИТ K)+ ,
  where
                                 ѕД (b) = inf{ti : S(ti ) < b}
  is the пђБrst time in {t1 , . . . , tn } the price of the underlying asset drops below
  b (understood to be вИЮ if S(ti ) > b for all i) and 1{ } denotes the indicator of
  the event in braces. A down-and-in call has payoпђА 1{ѕД (b) вЙ§ T }(S(T )вИТK)+:
  it gets вАЬknocked inвАЭ only when the underlying asset crosses the barrier. Up-
  and-out and up-and-in calls and puts are deпђБned analogously. Some knock-
  out options pay a rebate if the underlying asset crosses the barrier, with
  the rebate possibly paid either at the time of the barrier crossing or at the
  expiration of the option.
  These examples of discretely monitored barrier options are easily priced
  by simulation through sampling of S(t1 ), . . . , S(tn ), S(T ). A continuously
  monitored barrier option is knocked in or out the instant the underlying
  asset crosses the barrier; in other words, it replaces ѕД (b) as deпђБned above
  with
                              ѕДћГ (b) = inf{t вЙ• 0 : S(t) вЙ§ b}.
  Both discretely monitored and continuously monitored barrier options are
  found in practice. Many continuously monitored barrier options can be
                                               3.2 Geometric Brownian Motion       101

  priced in closed form; Merton [261] provides what is probably the пђБrst such
  formula and many other cases can be found in, e.g., Briys et al. [62]. Dis-
  cretely monitored barrier options generally do not admit pricing formulas
  and hence require computational procedures.
вЧ¶ Lookback options. Like barrier options, lookback options depend on extremal
  values of the underlying asset price. Lookback puts and calls expiring at tn
  have payoпђАs

             ( max S(ti ) вИТ S(tn ))           and       (S(tn ) вИТ min S(ti ))
                 i=1,...,n                                      i=1,...,n

  respectively. A lookback call, for example, may be viewed as the proпђБt from
  buying at the lowest price over t1 , . . . , tn and selling at the пђБnal price S(tn ).
  Continuously monitored versions of these options are deпђБned by taking the
  maximum or minimum over an interval rather than a пђБnite set of points.

Incorporating a Term Structure

Thus far, we have assumed that the risk-free interest rate r is constant. This
implies that the time-t price of a zero-coupon bond maturing (and paying 1)
at time T > t is
                              B(t, T ) = eвИТr(T вИТt) .                    (3.29)

indexed by maturity T , incompatible with (3.29). To price an option on an
underlying asset price S consistent with the observed term structure of bond
prices, we can introduce a deterministic but time-varying risk-free rate r(u)
by setting                                        -

Simulating OпђА a Forward Curve

For some types of underlying assets, particularly commodities, we may observe
not just a spot price S(0) but also a collection of forward prices F (0, T ). Here,
F (0, T ) denotes the price speciпђБed in a contract at time 0 to be paid at time T
for the underlying asset. Under the risk-neutral measure, F (0, T ) = E[S(T )];
in particular, the forward prices reпђВect the risk-free interest rate and any div-
idend yield (positive or negative) on the underlying asset. In pricing options,
we clearly want to simulate price paths of the underlying asset consistent with
the forward prices observed in the market.
    The equality F (0, T ) = E[S(T )] implies
                                                             
                    S(T ) = F (0, T ) exp вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 T + ѕГW (T ) .

Given forward prices F (0, t1 ), . . . , F (0, tn ), we can simulate using
                          F (0, ti+1 )    
      S(ti+1 ) = S(ti )                exp вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 (ti+1 вИТ ti ) + ѕГ      ti+1 вИТ ti Zi+1 .
                           F (0, ti )
This generalizes (3.30) because in the absence of dividends we have F (0, T ) =
S(0)/B(0, T ). Alternatively, we may deпђБne M (0) = 1,
                       

and set S(ti ) = F (0, ti )M (ti ), i = 1, . . . , n.

Deterministic Volatility Functions

Although geometric Brownian motion remains an important benchmark, it has
been widely observed across many markets that option prices are incompatible
                                             3.2 Geometric Brownian Motion            103

with a GBM model for the underlying asset. This has fueled research into
alternative speciпђБcations of the dynamics of asset prices.
    Consider a market in which several options with various strikes and ma-
turities are traded simultaneously on the same underlying asset. Suppose the
market is suпђГciently liquid that we may eпђАectively observe prices of the op-
tions without error. If the assumptions underlying the Black-Scholes formula
held exactly, all of these option prices would result from using the same volatil-
ity parameter ѕГ in the formula. In practice, one usually пђБnds that this implied
volatility actually varies with strike and maturity. It is therefore natural to
seek a minimal modiпђБcation of the Black-Scholes model capable of reproduc-
ing market prices.
    Consider the extreme case in which we observe the prices C(K, T ) of call
options on a single underlying asset for a continuum of strikes K and matu-
rities T . Dupire [107] shows that, subject only to smoothness conditions on
C as a function of K and T , it is possible to пђБnd a function ѕГ(S, t) such that
the model
                          dS(t)
                                = r dt + ѕГ(S(t), t) dW (t)
                           S(t)
reproduces the given option prices, in the sense that
                         eвИТrT E[(S(T ) вИТ K)+ ] = C(K, T )
for all K and T . This is sometimes called a deterministic volatility function to
emphasize that it extends geometric Brownian motion by allowing ѕГ to be a
deterministic function of the current level of the underlying asset. This feature
is important because it ensures that options can still be hedged through a
position in the underlying asset, which would not be the case in a stochastic
volatility model.
    In practice, we observe only a пђБnite set of option prices and this leaves a
great deal of пђВexibility in specifying ѕГ(S, t) while reproducing market prices.
We may, for example, impose smoothness constraints on the choice of volatility
function. This function will typically be the result of a numerical optimization
procedure and may never be given explicitly.
    Once ѕГ(S, t) has been chosen to match a set of actively traded options,
simulation may still be necessary to compute the prices of less liquid path-
dependent options. In general, there is no exact simulation procedure for these
models and it is necessary to use an Euler scheme of the form
                         
        S(ti+1 ) = S(ti ) 1 + r(ti+1 вИТ ti ) + ѕГ(S(ti ), ti ) ti+1 вИТ ti Zi+1 ,

with Z1 , Z2 , . . . independent standard normals, or
  S(ti+1 ) =
              
    S(ti ) exp [r вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 (S(ti ), ti )](ti+1 вИТ ti ) + ѕГ(S(ti ), ti ) ti+1 вИТ ti Zi+1 ,

which is equivalent to an Euler scheme for log S(t).

3.2.3 Multiple Dimensions

A multidimensional geometric Brownian motion can be speciпђБed through a

                     dSi (t)

                      Si (t)
where each Xi is a standard one-dimensional Brownian motion and Xi (t)
and Xj (t) have correlation ѕБij . If we deпђБne a d √Ч d matrix ќ£ by setting

the process S = (S1 , . . . , Sd ) as GBM(¬µ, ќ£) with ¬µ = (¬µ1 , . . . , ¬µd ). In a con-

covariance matrix and to the matrix with entries ѕБij as its correlation matrix;
the actual drift vector is (¬µ1 S1 (t), . . . , ¬µd Sd (t)) and the covariances are given
by
              Cov[Si (t), Sj (t)] = Si (0)Sj (0)e(¬µi +¬µj )t (eѕБij ѕГi ѕГj вИТ 1) .
This follows from the representation
                                          1   2
                  Si (t) = Si (0)e(¬µi вИТ 2 ѕГi )t+ѕГi Xi (t) ,      i = 1, . . . , d.

with W a standard Brownian motion BM(0, I) and A any matrix for which
AA = ќ£. We may apply this to (ѕГ1 X1 , . . . , ѕГd Xd ) and rewrite (3.31) as
                     dSi (t)
                             = ¬µi dt + ai dW (t),             i = 1, . . . , d,       (3.32)

with ai the ith row of A. A bit more explicitly, this is

                                              3.2 Geometric Brownian Motion     105

¬µi = r вИТ ќіi where r is the risk-free interest rate and ќіi is the dividend yield
on the ith asset Si .
   We list a few examples of option payoпђАs depending on multiple assets:
вЧ¶ Spread option. A call option on the spread between two assets S1 , S2 has
  payoпђА
                           ([S1 (T ) вИТ S2 (T )] вИТ K)+
  with K a strike price. For example, crack spread options traded on the New
  York Mercantile Exchange are options on the spread between heating oil
  and crude oil futures.
вЧ¶ Basket option. A basket option is an option on a portfolio of underlying
  assets and has a payoпђА of, e.g.,

                 ([c1 S1 (T ) + c2 S2 (T ) + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + cd Sd (T )] вИТ K)+ .

  Typical examples would be options on a portfolio of related assets вАФ bank
  stocks or Asian currencies, for instance.
вЧ¶ Outperformance option. These are options on the maximum or minimum of
  multiple assets and have payoпђАs of, e.g., the form

                 (max{c1 S1 (T ), c2 S2 (T ), ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј , cd Sd (T )} вИТ K)+ .

вЧ¶ Barrier options. A two-asset barrier option may have a payoпђА of the form

                       1{ min S2 (ti ) < b}(K вИТ S1 (T ))+ ;
                          i=1,...,n

  This is a down-and-in put on S1 that knocks in when S2 drops below a
  barrier at b. Many variations on this basic structure are possible. In this
  example, one may think of S1 as an individual stock and S2 as the level
  of an equity index: the put on the stock is knocked in only if the market
  drops.
вЧ¶ Quantos. Quantos are options sensitive both to a stock price and an ex-
  change rate. For example, consider an option to buy a stock denominated
  in a foreign currency with the strike price пђБxed in the foreign currency but
  the payoпђА of the option to be made in the domestic currency. Let S1 de-
  note the stock price and S2 the exchange rate, expressed as the quantity of
  domestic currency required per unit of foreign currency. Then the payoпђА of
  the option in the domestic currency is given by

                                S2 (T )(S1 (T ) вИТ K)+ .                       (3.34)

  The payoпђА
                                                            +
                                                    K
                                      S1 (T ) вИТ
                                                  S2 (T )
  corresponds to a quanto in which the level of the strike is пђБxed in the do-
  mestic currency and the payoпђА of the option is made in the foreign currency.

3.3 Gaussian Short Rate Models

This section and the next develop methods for simulating some simple but
important stochastic interest rate models. These models posit the dynamics
of an instantaneous continuously compounded short rate r(t). An investment
in a money market account earning interest at rate r(u) at time u grows from
a value of 1 at time 0 to a value of
                                       t
                           ќ≤(t) = exp     r(u) du
                                              0

at time t. Though this is now a stochastic quantity, it remains the numeraire
for risk-neutral pricing. The price at time 0 of a derivative security that pays
X at time T is the expectation of X/ќ≤(T ), i.e.,
                                               
                                          T
                         E exp вИТ              r(u) du X ,                 (3.37)
                                      0


the expectation taken with respect to the risk-neutral measure. In particular,
the time-0 price of a bond paying 1 at T is given by
                                                   
                                                      T
                     B(0, T ) = E exp вИТ                   r(u) du   .     (3.38)
                                                  0


We focus primarily on the dynamics of the short rate under the risk-neutral
measure.
    The Gaussian models treated in this section oпђАer a high degree of tractabil-
ity. Many simple instruments can be priced in closed form in these models or
using deterministic numerical methods. Some extensions of the basic models
and some pricing applications do, however, require simulation for the calcu-
lation of expressions of the form (3.37). The tractability of the models oпђАers
opportunities for increasing the accuracy of simulation.

3.3.1 Basic Models and Simulation

The classical model of Vasicek [352] describes the short rate through an
Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process (cf. Karatzas and Shreve [207], p.358)

                       dr(t) = ќ±(b вИТ r(t)) dt + ѕГ dW (t).                 (3.39)

Here, W is a standard Brownian motion and ќ±, b, and ѕГ are positive constants.
Notice that the drift in (3.39) is positive if r(t) < b and negative if r(t) > b;
thus, r(t) is pulled toward level b, a property generally referred to as mean
reversion. We may interpret b as a long-run interest rate level and ќ± as the
speed at which r(t) is pulled toward b. The mean-reverting form of the drift is
                                                   3.3 Gaussian Short Rate Models           109

an essential feature of the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process and thus of the Vasicek
model.
   The continuous-time Ho-Lee model [185] has

                                dr(t) = g(t) dt + ѕГ dW (t)                              (3.40)

with g a deterministic function of time. Both (3.39) and (3.40) deпђБne Gaussian
processes, meaning that the joint distribution of r(t1 ), . . . , r(tn ) is multivariate
normal for any t1 , . . . , tn . Both deпђБne Markov processes and are special cases
of the general Gaussian Markov process speciпђБed by

                     dr(t) = [g(t) + h(t)r(t)] dt + ѕГ(t) dW (t),                        (3.41)

with g, h, and ѕГ all deterministic functions of time. Natural extensions of
(3.39) and (3.40) thus allow ѕГ, b, and ќ± to vary with time. Modeling with the
Vasicek model when b in particular is time-varying is discussed in Hull and
White [190].
    The SDE (3.41) has solution
                          t                       t
     r(t) = eH(t) r(0) +     eH(t)вИТH(s) g(s) ds +     eH(t)вИТH(s) ѕГ(s) dW (s),
                            0                                  0

with                                           t
                                    H(t) =          h(s) ds,
                                               0
as can be veriпђБed through an application of ItoћВвАЩs formula. Because this pro-
duces a Gaussian process, simulation of r(t1 ), . . . , r(tn ) is a special case of the
general problem of sampling from a multivariate normal distribution, treated
in Section 2.3. But it is a suпђГciently interesting special case to merit consid-
eration. To balance tractability with generality, we will focus on the Vasicek
model (3.39) with time-varying b and on the Ho-Lee model (3.40). Similar
ideas apply to the general case (3.41).

Simulation

entails some discretization error. Exact simulation of the Ho-Lee process (3.40)

                                                          #
             вИТќ±(ti+1 вИТti )              вИТќ±(ti+1 вИТti )        1                    
r(ti+1 ) = e               r(ti )+b(1вИТe               )+ѕГ       1 вИТ eвИТ2ќ±(ti+1 вИТti ) Zi+1 .
                                                            2ќ±
                                                                                   (3.46)
The Euler scheme is then equivalent to making the approximation ex вЙИ 1 + x
for the exponentials in this recursion.
    Evaluation of the integral deпђБning ¬µ(ti , ti+1 ) and required in (3.45) may
seem burdensome. The eпђАort involved in evaluating this integral clearly de-
pends on the form of the function b(t) so it is worth discussing how this
function is likely to be speciпђБed in practice. Typically, the пђВexibility to make
b vary with time is used to make the dynamics of the short rate consistent
with an observed term structure of bond prices. The same is true of the func-
tion g in the Ho-Lee model (3.40). We return to this point in Section 3.3.2,
where we discuss bond prices in Gaussian models.

Stationary Version

Suppose b(t) вЙ° b and ќ± > 0. Then from (3.43) we see that

                E[r(t)] = eвИТќ±t r(0) + (1 вИТ eвИТќ±t )b вЖТ b              as t вЖТ вИЮ,

so the process r(t) has a limiting mean. It also has a limiting variance given
(via (3.44)) by
                                                 3.3 Gaussian Short Rate Models                  111

                                             ѕГ2            ѕГ2
                       lim Var[r(t)] = lim       1 вИТ eвИТ2ќ±t =    .
                       tвЖТвИЮ               tвЖТвИЮ 2ќ±              2ќ±
In fact, r(t) converges in distribution to a normal distribution with this mean
and variance, in the sense that for any x вИИ 

                                                         xвИТb
                                 P (r(t) вЙ§ x) вЖТ ќ¶         вИЪ          ,
                                                        ѕГ/ 2ќ±

with ќ¶ the standard normal distribution. The fact that r(t) has a limiting
distribution is a reпђВection of the stabilizing eпђАect of mean reversion in the drift
and contrasts with the long-run behavior of, for example, geometric Brownian
motion.
    The limiting distribution of r(t) is also a stationary distribution in the
sense that if r(0) is given this distribution then every r(t), t > 0, has this
distribution as well. Because (3.46) provides an exact discretization of the
process, the N (b, ѕГ 2 /2ќ±) distribution is also stationary for the discretized
process. To simulate a stationary version of the process, it therefore suпђГces
to draw r(0) from this normal distribution and then proceed as in (3.46).

3.3.2 Bond Prices

As already noted, time-dependent drift parameters are typically used to make
a short rate model consistent with an observed set of bond prices. Implemen-
tation of the simulation algorithm (3.45) is thus linked to the calibration of the
model through the choice of the function b(t). The same applies to the func-
tion g(t) in the Ho-Lee model and as this case is slightly simpler we consider
it пђБrst.
    Our starting point is the bond-pricing formula (3.38). The integral of r(u)
from 0 to T appearing in that formula is normally distributed because r(u)
is a Gaussian process. It follows that the bond price is the expectation of
the exponential of a normal random variable. For a normal random variable
X вИЉ N (m, v 2 ), we have E[exp(X)] = exp(m + (v 2 /2)), so

of the integral of the short rate.

values of r on the time grid t1 , . . . , tn .

Change of Numeraire

Thus far, we have considered the dynamics of the short rate r(t) only under
the risk-neutral measure. Recall that
                                   2 t the numeraire asset associated with the
risk-neutral measure is ќ≤(t) = exp( 0 r(u) du) and the deпђБning feature of this
probability measure is that it makes the discounted bond prices B(t, T )/ќ≤(t)
martingales. In fact, the dynamics of the bond prices under the Gaussian
models we have considered are of the form (for пђБxed T )

                        dB(t, T )
                                  = r(t) dt вИТ A(t, T )ѕГ dW (t)                         (3.56)
                         B(t, T )

with A(t, T ) deterministic; this follows from (3.55). The solution of this equa-
tion is
                          t                                    t
 B(t, T ) = B(0, T ) exp     [r(u) вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 A2 (u, T )] du вИТ ѕГ     A(u, T ) dW (u) ,
                              0                                           0

from which it is evident that
                                               3.3 Gaussian Short Rate Models             117
                                         t                        t
    B(t, T )
             = B(0, T ) exp вИТ 21 ѕГ 2          A2 (u, T ) du вИТ ѕГ         A(u, T ) dW (u)
     ќ≤(t)                                0                         0
                                                                          (3.57)
is an exponential martingale.
    As discussed in Section 1.2.3, the forward measure for any date TF is the
measure associated with taking the TF -maturity bond B(t, TF ) as numeraire
asset. The deпђБning feature of the forward measure is that it makes the ratios
B(t, T )/B(t, TF ) martingales for T < TF . It is deпђБned by the likelihood ratio
process
                            dPTF        B(t, TF )ќ≤(0)
                                     =                ,
                             dPќ≤ t      ќ≤(t)B(0, TF )
and this is given in (3.57) up to a factor of 1/B(0, TF ). From GirsanovвАЩs
Theorem, it follows that the process W TF deпђБned by

                       dW TF (t) = dW (t) + ѕГA(t, TF ) dt

is a standard Brownian motion under PTF . Accordingly, the dynamics of the
Vasicek model become

            dr(t) = ќ±(b(t) вИТ r(t)) dt + ѕГ dW (t)
                  = ќ±(b(t) вИТ r(t)) dt + ѕГ (dW TF (t) вИТ ѕГA(t, TF ) dt)
                  = ќ±(b(t) вИТ ѕГ 2 A(t, TF ) вИТ r(t)) dt + ѕГ dW TF (t).                  (3.58)

Thus, under the forward measure, the short rate process remains a Vasicek
process but the reversion level b(t) becomes b(t) вИТ ѕГ 2 A(t, TF ).
    The process in (3.58) can be simulated using (3.45) with b(t) replaced by
b(t) вИТ ѕГ 2 A(t, TF ). In particular, we simulate W TF the way we would simu-
late any other standard Brownian motion. The simulation algorithm does not
вАЬknowвАЭ that it is simulating a Brownian motion under the forward measure
rather than under the risk-neutral measure.
    Suppose we want to price a derivative security making a payoпђА of g(r(TF ))
at time TF . Under the risk-neutral measure, we would price the security by
computing                      2                   

                          E e       0              g(r(TF )) .

In fact, g could be a function of the path of r(t) rather than just its terminal
value. Switching to the forward measure, this becomes
                   2                                    
