where ETF denotes expectation under the forward measure. Thus, we may
price the derivative security by simulating r(t) under the forward measure
PTF , estimating the expectation of g(r(TF )) and multiplying by B(0, TF ).
Notice that discounting in this case is deterministic вАФ we do not need to
simulate a discount factor. This apparent simpliпђБcation results from inclusion
of the additional term вИТѕГ 2 A(t, TF ) in the drift of r(t).
    A consequence of working under the forward measure is that the simulation
prices the bond maturing at TF exactly: pricing this bond corresponds to tak-
ing g(r(TF )) вЙ° 1. Again, this apparent simpliпђБcation is really a consequence
of the form of the drift of r(t) under the forward measure.

3.3.3 Multifactor Models

A general class of Gaussian Markov processes in d have the form
                      dX(t) = C(b вИТ X(t)) dt + D dW (t)                    (3.59)
where C and D are d √Ч d matrices, b and X(t) are in d , W is a standard
d-dimensional Brownian motion, and X(0) is Gaussian or constant. Such a
process remains Gaussian and Markovian if the coeпђГcients C, b, and D are
made time-varying but deterministic. The solution of (3.59) is
                            t                  t
        X(t) = eвИТCt X(0) +     eвИТC(tвИТs) b ds +     eвИТC(tвИТs) D dW (s),
                               0                   0

from which it is possible to deпђБne an exact time-discretization similar to (3.45).
    A model of the short rate process can be speciпђБed by setting r(t) = a X(t)
with a вИИ d (or with a deterministically time-varying). The elements of X(t)
are then interpreted as вАЬfactorsвАЭ driving the evolution of the short rate. Be-
cause each X(t) is normally distributed, r(t) is normally distributed. However,
r(t) is not in general a Markov process: to make the future evolution of r in-
dependent of the past, we need to condition on the full state information X(t)
and not merely r(t).
    Recall from (3.55) that in the Vasicek model (with constant or time-varying
coeпђГcients), bond prices are exponentials of aпђГne functions of the short rate.
A similar representation applies if the short rate has the form r(t) = a X(t)
and X(t) is as in (3.59); in particular, we have
                   B(t, T ) = exp(вИТA(t, T ) X(t) + C(t, T ))
for some d -valued function A(t, T ) and some scalar function C(t, T ). In the
single-factor setting, diпђАerentiating (3.55) and then simplifying leads to
                     dB(t, T )
                               = r(t) dt вИТ A(t, T )ѕГ dW (t),
                     B(t, T )
with ѕГ the diпђАusion parameter of r(t). The instantaneous correlation between
the returns on bonds with maturities T1 and T2 is therefore
                                                             3.3 Gaussian Short Rate Models             119

                                          A(t, T1 )ѕГ ¬Ј A(t, T2 )ѕГ
                                                                       = 1.
                                         A2 (t, T1 )ѕГ 2 A2 (t, T2 )ѕГ 2

In other words, all bonds are instantaneously perfectly correlated. In the mul-
tifactor setting, the bond price dynamics are given by

                              dB(t, T )
                                        = r(t) dt вИТ A(t, T ) D dW (t).
                               B(t, T )

The instantaneous correlation for maturities T1 and T2 is

                                          A(t, T1 ) DD A(t, T2 )
                                                                     ,
                                        A(t, T1 ) DA(t, T2 ) D

which can certainly take values other than 1. The пђВexibility to capture less
than perfect instantaneous correlation between bond returns is the primary
motivation for considering multifactor models.
   Returning to the general formulation in (3.59), suppose that C can be
diagonalized in the sense that V CV вИТ1 = ќЫ for some matrix V and diagonal
matrix ќЫ with diagonal entries ќї1 , . . . , ќїd . Suppose further that C is nonsin-
gular and deпђБne Y (t) = V X(t). Then

                           dY (t) = V dX(t)
                                      = V [C(b вИТ X(t) dt + D dW (t)]
                                      = (V Cb вИТ ќЫY (t)) dt + V D dW (t)
                                      = ќЫ(ќЫвИТ1 V Cb вИТ Y (t)) dt + V D dW (t)
                                      = ќЫ(V b вИТ Y (t)) dt + V D dW (t)
                                      вЙ° ќЫ(bћГ вИТ Y (t)) dt + dWћГ (t)

with WћГ a BM(0, ќ£) process, ќ£ = V DD V  . It follows that the components
of (Y1 , . . . , Yd ) satisfy

                       dYj (t) = ќїj (bћГj вИТ Yj (t)) dt + dWћГj (t),              j = 1, . . . , d.     (3.60)

In particular, each Yj is itself a Markov process. The Yj remain coupled,
however, through the correlation across the components of WћГ . They can be
simulated as in (3.46) by setting

Yj (ti+1 ) =
                                                                   +
     ќїj (ti+1 вИТti )                   ќїj (ti+1 вИТti )                    1                      
 e                    Yj (ti ) + (e                    вИТ 1)bћГj +           1 вИТ eвИТ2ќїj (ti+1 вИТti ) ќЊj (i + 1),
                                                                       2ќїj

where ќЊ(1), ќЊ(2), . . . are independent N (0, ќ£) random vectors, ќЊ(i) = (ќЊ1 (i),
. . . , ќЊd (i)). Thus, when C is nonsingular and diagonalizable, simulation of
(3.59) can be reduced to a system of scalar simulations.
120    3 Generating Sample Paths

   As noted by Andersen and Andreasen [14], a similar reduction is possible
even if C is not diagonalizable, but at the expense of making all coeпђГcients
time-dependent. If V (t) is a deterministic d√Ч d matrix-valued function of time
and we set Y (t) = V (t)X(t), then

           dY (t) = VћЗ (t)X(t) dt + V (t)dX(t)
                  = [VћЗ (t)X(t) + V (t)C(b вИТ X(t))] dt + V (t)D dW (t),

where VћЗ (t) denotes the time derivative of V (t). If we choose V (t) = exp([C вИТ
I]t), then
                             VћЗ (t) = V (t)C вИТ V (t)
and thus

                dY (t) = [V (t)Cb вИТ V (t)X(t)] dt + V (t)D dW (t)
                      = (bћГ(t) вИТ Y (t)) dt + DћГ(t) dW (t),                (3.61)

with bћГ(t) = V (t)Cb and DћГ(t) = V (t)D. Notice that the drift of each com-
ponent Yi (t) depends only on that Yi (t). This transformation therefore de-
couples the drifts of the components of the state vector, making each Yi a
Markov process, though the components remain linked through the diпђАusion
term. We can recover the original state vector by setting X(t) = V (t)вИТ1 Y (t)
because V (t) is always invertible. The seemingly special form of the dynamics
in (3.61) is thus no less general than the dynamics in (3.59) with time-varying
coeпђГcients.


3.4 Square-Root DiпђАusions

Feller [118] studied a class of processes that includes the square-root diпђАusion

                    dr(t) = ќ±(b вИТ r(t)) dt + ѕГ    r(t) dW (t),            (3.62)

with W a standard one-dimensional Brownian motion. We consider the case
in which ќ± and b are positive. If r(0) > 0, then r(t) will never be negative; if
2ќ±b вЙ• ѕГ 2 , then r(t) remains strictly positive for all t, almost surely.
    This process was proposed by Cox, Ingersoll, and Ross [91] as a model
of the short rate, generally referred to as the CIR model. They developed a
general equilibrium framework in which if the change in production opportu-
nities is assumed to follow a process of this form, then the short rate does as
well. As with the Vasicek model, the form of the drift in (3.62) suggests that
r(t) is pulled towards b at a speed controlled by ќ±. In contrast to the Vasicek
model, in the CIR model the diпђАusion term ѕГ r(t) decreases to zero as r(t)
approaches the origin and this prevents r(t) from taking negative values. This
feature of (3.62) is attractive in modeling interest rates.
                                                  3.4 Square-Root DiпђАusions        121

   All of the coeпђГcients in (3.62) could in principle be made time-dependent.
In practice, it can be particularly useful to replace the constant b with a
function of time and thus consider

                    dr(t) = ќ±(b(t) вИТ r(t)) dt + ѕГ       r(t) dW (t).            (3.63)

As with the Vasicek model, this extension is frequently used to make the bond
price function                                     
                                         T
                      T вЖТ E exp вИТ          r(u) du
                                              0

match a set of observed bond prices B(0, T ).
   Although we stress the application of (3.63) to interest rate modeling, it
should be noted that this process has other пђБnancial applications. For example,
Heston [179] proposed a stochastic volatility model in which the price of an
asset S(t) is governed by

                   dS(t)
                         = ¬µ dt +       V (t) dW1 (t)                           (3.64)
                   S(t)
                   dV (t) = ќ±(b вИТ V (t)) dt + ѕГ       V (t) dW2 (t),            (3.65)

where (W1 , W2 ) is a two-dimensional Brownian motion. Thus, in HestonвАЩs
model, the squared volatility V (t) follows a square-root diпђАusion. In addition,
the process in (3.63) is sometimes used to model a stochastic intensity for a
jump process in, for example, modeling default.
      A simple Euler discretization of (3.62) suggests simulating r(t) at times
t1 , . . . , tn by setting

  r(ti+1 ) = r(ti ) + ќ±(b вИТ r(ti ))[ti+1 вИТ ti ] + ѕГ   r(ti )+   ti+1 вИТ ti Zi+1 , (3.66)

with Z1 , . . . , Zn independent N (0, 1) random variables. Notice that we have
taken the positive part of r(ti ) inside the square root; some modiпђБcation of
this form is necessary because the values of r(ti ) produced by Euler discretiza-
tion may become negative. We will see, however, that this issue can be avoided
(along with any other discretization error) by sampling from the exact tran-
sition law of the process.

3.4.1 Transition Density

The SDE (3.62) is not explicitly solvable the way those considered in Sec-
tions 3.2 and 3.3 are; nevertheless, the transition density for the process is
known. Based on results of Feller [118], Cox et al. [91] noted that the distri-
bution of r(t) given r(u) for some u < t is, up to a scale factor, a noncentral
chi-square distribution. This property can be used to simulate the process
(3.62). We follow the approach suggested by Scott [324].

d degrees of freedom and noncentrality parameter 0 (making it an ordinary
chi-square random variable). This is a stationary distribution in the sense that
if r(0) is drawn from this distribution, then r(t) has the same distribution for
all t.

3.4.2 Sampling Gamma and Poisson
The discussion leading to Figure 3.5 reduces the problem of simulating the
square-root diпђАusion (3.62) to one of sampling from a chi-square distribution
and possibly also the normal and Poisson distributions. We discussed sampling
from the normal distribution in Section 2.3; we now consider methods for
sampling from the chi-square and Poisson distributions.

Gamma Distribution
The gamma distribution with shape parameter a and scale parameter ќ≤ has
density
                                   1
              f (y) = fa,ќ≤ (y) =         y aвИТ1 eвИТy/ќ≤ , y вЙ• 0.     (3.74)
                                 ќУ(a)ќ≤ a
It has mean aќ≤ and variance aќ≤ 2 . Comparison with (3.71) reveals that the
chi-square distribution is the special case of scale parameter ќ≤ = 2 and shape
parameter a = ќљ/2. We therefore consider the more general problem of gen-
erating samples from gamma distributions.
    Methods for sampling from the gamma distribution typically distinguish
the cases a вЙ§ 1 and a > 1. For the application to the square-root diпђАusion
(3.62), the shape parameter a is given by d/2 with d as in (3.69). At least in
the case of an interest rate model, d would typically be larger than 2 so the
case a > 1 is most relevant. We include the case a вЙ§ 1 for completeness and
other potential applications. There is no loss of generality in пђБxing the scale
parameter ќ≤ at 1: if X has the gamma distribution with parameters (a, 1),
then ќ≤X has the gamma distribution with parameters (a, ќ≤).
    Cheng and Feast [83] develop a method based on a general approach to
random variate generation known as the ratio-of-uniforms method. The ratio-
of-uniforms method is closely related to the acceptance-rejection method dis-
cussed in Section 2.2.2. It exploits the following property. Suppose f is a
126     3 Generating Sample Paths

nonnegative, integrable function on [0, вИЮ); if (X, Y ) is uniformly distributed
over the set A = {(x, y) : x вЙ§ f (y/x)}, then the density of Y /X is propor-
tional to f . (See p.180 of Fishman [121] or p.59 of Gentle [136].) Suppose A
is contained in a bounded rectangle. Then to sample uniformly from A, we
can repeatedly sample pairs (X, Y ) uniformly over the rectangle and keep the
пђБrst one that satisпђБes X вЙ§ f (Y /X). The ratio-of-uniforms method delivers
Y /X as a sample from the density proportional to f .
    To sample from the gamma density with a > 1, deпђБne
                                                          3
                   A = (x, y) : 0 вЙ§ x вЙ§ [(y/x)  aвИТ1 e вИТy/x ] .

This set is contained in the rectangle [0, xћД] √Ч [0, yћД] with xћД = [(a вИТ 1)/e](aвИТ1)/2
and yћД = [(a + 1)/e](a+1)/2 . Sampling uniformly over this rectangle, the ex-
pected number of samples needed until one lands in A is given by the ratio of
the area
     вИЪ of A to that of the rectangle. As shown in Fishman [121], this ratio
is O( a), so the time required to generate a sample using this method grows
with the shape parameter. Cheng and Feast [83] and Fishman [121] develop
modiпђБcations of this basic approach that accelerate sampling. In Figure 3.7,
which is FishmanвАЩs Algorithm GKM1, the пђБrst acceptance test is a fast check
that reduces the number of logarithmic evaluations. When many samples are
to be generated using the same shape parameter (as would be the case in the
application to the square-root diпђАusion), the constants in the setup step in
Figure 3.8 should be computed just once and then passed as arguments to
the sampling routine. For large values of the shape parameter a, Algorithm
GKM2 in Fishman [121] is faster than the method in Figure 3.7.



          Setup: aћД вЖР a вИТ 1, b вЖР (a вИТ (1/(6a)))/aћД, m вЖР 2/aћД, d вЖР m + 2
          repeat
              generate U1 , U2 вИЉ Unif[0,1]
              V вЖР bU2 /U1
              if mU1 вИТ d + V + (1/V ) вЙ§ 0, accept
              elseif m log U1 вИТ log V + V вИТ 1 вЙ§ 0, accept
          until accept
          return Z вЖР aћДV


Fig. 3.7. Algorithm GKM1 from Fishman [121], based on Cheng and Feast [83], for
sampling from the gamma distribution with parameters (a, 1), a > 1.


   Ahrens and Dieter [6] provide a fast acceptance-rejection algorithm for
the case a вЙ§ 1. Their method generates candidates by sampling from distri-
butions concentrated on [0, 1] and (1, вИЮ) with appropriate probabilities. In
more detail, let p = e/(a + e) (e = exp(1)) and deпђБne

eвИТz+1 on (1, вИЮ), with weights p and (1 вИТ p), respectively. We can sample from

Poisson Distribution

The Poisson distribution with mean ќЄ > 0 is given by
128    3 Generating Sample Paths

                                      ќЄk
                    P (N = k) = eвИТќЄ      ,       k = 0, 1, 2, . . . .     (3.75)
                                      k!
We abbreviate this by writing N вИЉ Poisson(ќЄ). This is the distribution of
the number of events in [0, 1] when the times between consecutive events are
independent and exponentially distributed with mean 1/ќЄ. Thus, a simple
method for generating Poisson samples is to generate exponential random
variables Xi = вИТ log(Ui )/ќЄ from independent uniforms Ui and then take N to
be the largest integer for which X1 +¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј+XN вЙ§ 1. This method is rather slow,
especially if ќЄ is large. In the intended application in Figure 3.5, the mean of
the Poisson random variable вАФ equal to half the noncentrality parameter in
the transition density of the square-root diпђАusion вАФ could be quite large for
plausible parameter values.
    An alternative is to use the inverse transform method. For discrete dis-
tributions, this amounts to a sequential search for the smallest n at which
F (n) вЙ§ U , where F denotes the cumulative distribution function and U
is Unif[0,1]. In the case of a Poisson distribution, F (n) is calculated as
P (N = 0) + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + P (N = n); rather than calculate each term in this sum
using (3.75), we can use the relation P (N = k + 1) = P (N = k)ќЄ/(k + 1).
Figure 3.9 illustrates the method.



                             p вЖР exp(вИТќЄ), F вЖР p
                             N вЖР0
                             generate U вИЉ Unif[0,1]
                             while U > F
                                 N вЖРN +1
                                 p вЖР pќЄ/N
                                 F вЖРF +p
                             return N

tribution with mean ќЄ.

                        ѕГ 2 ќї(eќ≥(ќЄ)s вИТ 1) + ќ≥(ќЄ) вИТ ќ± + eќ≥(ќЄ)s(ќ≥(ќЄ) + ќ±)
                вИЪ
and ќ≥(ќЄ) = ќ±2 + 2ѕГ 2 ќЄ. The bond pricing formula is the special case ќї = 0,
ќЄ = 1.
    The bivariate Laplace transform in (3.76) characterizes the joint distri-
bution of the short rate and its integral. This makes it possible, at least
in principle, to sample from the joint distribution of (r(ti+1 ), Y (ti+1 ) given
(r(ti ), Y (ti )) with
                                      t
                             Y (t) =     r(u) du.
                                                    0
As explained in Section 3.3.2, this would allow exact simulation of the short
rate and the discount factor on a discrete time grid. In the Gaussian setting,
the joint distribution of r(t) and Y (t) is normal and therefore easy to sample;
in contrast, the joint distribution determined by (3.76) is not explicitly avail-
able. Scott [324] derives the Laplace transform of the conditional distribution
of Y (ti+1 ) вИТ Y (ti ) given r(ti ) and r(ti+1 ), and explains how to use numerical
transform inversion to sample from the conditional distribution. Through this
method, he is able to simulate (r(ti ), Y (ti )) without discretization error.
130       3 Generating Sample Paths

Time-Dependent CoeпђГcients

As noted earlier, the parameter b is often replaced with a deterministic func-
tion of time b(t) in order to calibrate the model to an initial term structure,
resulting in the dynamics speciпђБed in (3.63). In this more general setting, a
result of the form in (3.76) continues to hold but with functions ѕИ1 and ѕИ2 de-
pending on both t and T rather than merely on T вИТt. Moreover, these functions
will not in general be available in closed form, but are instead characterized by
a system of ordinary diпђАerential equations. By solving these diпђАerential equa-
tions numerically, it then becomes possible to compute bond prices. Indeed,
bond prices continue to have the exponential aпђГne form, though the functions
A(t, T ) and C(t, T ) in the exponent are no longer available explicitly but are
also determined through ordinary diпђАerential equations (see DuпђГe, Pan, Sin-
gleton [105] and Jamshidian [195]). This makes it possible to use a numerical
procedure to choose the function b(t) to match an initial set of bond prices
B(0, T ).
    Once the constant b is replaced with a function of time, the transition
density of the short rate process ceases to admit the relatively tractable form
discussed in Section 3.4.1. One can of course simulate using an Euler scheme
of the form

      r(ti+1 ) = r(ti ) + ќ±(b(ti ) вИТ r(ti ))[ti+1 вИТ ti ] + ѕГ   r(ti )+    ti+1 вИТ ti Zi+1 ,

with independent Zi вИЉ N (0, 1). However, it seems preferable (at least from a
distributional perspective) to replace this normal approximation to the tran-
sition law with a noncentral chi-square approximation. For example, if we
let                                         ti+1
                                      1
                        bћД(ti ) =                 b(s) ds
                                  ti+1 вИТ ti ti
denote the average level of b(t) over [ti , ti+1 ] (assumed positive), then (3.68)
suggests simulating by setting

                   ѕГ 2 (1 вИТ eвИТќ±(ti+1 вИТti ) ) 2       4ќ±eвИТќ±(ti+1 вИТti )
      r(ti+1 ) =                            ѕЗd                             r(ti ) ,    (3.77)
                             4ќ±                  ѕГ 2 (1 вИТ eвИТќ±(ti+1 вИТti ) )

with d = 4bћДќ±/ѕГ 2 . We can sample from the indicated noncentral chi-square
distribution using the methods discussed in Section 3.4.1. However, it must be
stressed that whereas (3.68) is an exact representation in the case of constant
coeпђГcients, (3.77) is only an approximate procedure. If it suпђГces to choose
the function b(t) to match only bonds maturing at the simulation grid dates
t1 , . . . , tn , then it may be possible to choose b to be constant over each interval
[ti , ti+1 ], in which case (3.77) becomes exact.
      Jamshidian [195] shows that if ќ±, b, and ѕГ are all deterministic functions
of time, the transition density of r(t) can be represented through a noncen-
tral chi-square distribution provided ќ±(t)b(t)/ѕГ 2 (t) is independent of t. From
(3.69) we see that this is equivalent to requiring that the degrees-of-freedom
                                                      3.4 Square-Root DiпђАusions       131

parameter d = 4bќ±/ѕГ 2 be constant. However, in this setting, the other para-
meters of the transition density are not given explicitly but rather as solutions
to ordinary diпђАerential equations.

Change of Numeraire
Recall from Sections 1.2.3 and 3.3.2 that the forward measure for any date TF
is the measure associated with taking as numeraire asset the bond B(t, TF )
maturing at TF . We saw in Section 3.3.2 that if the short rate follows an
Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process under the risk-neutral measure, then it continues
to follow an OU process under a forward measure. An analogous property
holds if the short rate follows a square-root diпђАusion.
    Most of the development leading to (3.58) results from the exponential
aпђГne formula for bond prices and thus extends to the square-root model. In
this setting, the bond price dynamics become
                  dB(t, T )
                            = r(t) dt вИТ A(t, T )ѕГ         r(t) dW (t);
                   B(t, T )

in particular, the coeпђГcient ѕГ r(t) replaces the ѕГ of the Gaussian case. Pro-
ceeding as in (3.56)вАУ(3.58) but with this substitution, we observe that Gir-
sanovвАЩs Theorem implies that the process W TF deпђБned by
                    dW TF (t) = dW (t) + ѕГ        r(t)A(t, TF ) dt
is a standard Brownian motion under the forward measure PTF . The dynamics
of the short rate thus become
      dr(t) = ќ±(b(t) вИТ r(t)) dt + ѕГ    r(t) dW (t)
            = ќ±(b(t) вИТ r(t)) dt + ѕГ    r(t)[dW TF (t) вИТ ѕГ         r(t)A(t, TF )dt]
            = ќ±(b(t) вИТ (1 + ѕГ 2 A(t, TF ))r(t)] dt + ѕГ         r(t) dW TF (t).
This can be written as
                                      b(t)
 dr(t) = ќ±(1 + ѕГ 2 A(t, TF ))                         вИТ r(t)   dt + ѕГ    r(t) dW TF (t),
                                1 + ѕГ 2 A(t, T   F)

which shows that under the forward measure the short rate is again a square-
root diпђАusion but one in which both the level to which the process reverts and
the speed with which it reverts are functions of time. The pricing of derivative
securities through simulation in the forward measure works the same way here
as in the Vasicek model.

3.4.4 Extensions
In this section we consider further properties and extensions of the square-root
diпђАusion. We discuss multifactor models, a connection with squared Gaussian
processes, and a connection with CEV (constant elasticity of variance) models.
132    3 Generating Sample Paths

Multifactor Models

The simplest multifactor extension of the CIR interest rate model deпђБnes
independent processes

        dXi (t) = ќ±i (bi вИТ Xi (t)) dt + ѕГi      Xi (t) dWi (t),      i = 1, . . . , d,

and takes the short rate to be r(t) = X1 (t) + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + Xd (t). Much as in the
discussion of Section 3.3.3, this extension allows imperfect instantaneous cor-
relation among bonds of diпђАerent maturities. Each Xi can be simulated using
the method developed in the previous sections for a single-factor model.
    It is possible to consider more general models in which the underlying
processes X1 , . . . , Xd are correlated. However, once one goes beyond the case
of independent square-root factors, it seems more natural to move directly to
the full generality of the aпђГne models characterized by DuпђГe and Kan [101].
This class of models has a fair amount of tractability and computationally
attractive features, but we will not consider it further here.

Squared Gaussian Models

We next point out a connection between the (single-factor) square-root diпђАu-
sion and a Gaussian model of the type considered in Section 3.3. This connec-
tion is of intrinsic interest, it sheds further light on the simulation procedure
of Section 3.4.1, and it suggests a wider class of interest rate models. The
link between the CIR model and squared Gaussian models is noted in Rogers
[307]; related connections are developed in depth by Revuz and Yor [306] in
their discussion of Bessel processes.
    Let X1 (t), . . . , Xd (t) be independent Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes of the
form

scalar multiple of a noncentral chi-square random variable, so this construction
is really just a special case of the method in Section 3.4.1. It sheds some light
on the appearance of the noncentral chi-square distribution in the law of r(t).
    This construction also points to another strategy for constructing inter-
est rate models: rather than restricting ourselves to a sum of independent,
identical squared OU processes, we can consider other quadratic functions
of multivariate Gaussian processes. This idea has been developed in Beagle-
hole and Tenney [42] and Jamshidian [196]. The resulting models are closely
related to the aпђГne family.

CEV Process

We conclude this section with a digression away from interest rate models
to consider a class of asset price processes closely related to the square-root
diпђАusion.
    Among the important alternatives to the lognormal model for an asset
price considered in Section 3.2 is the constant elasticity of variance (CEV)
process (see Cox and Ross [89], Schroder [322] and references there)
                         dS(t) = ¬µS(t) dt + ѕГS(t)ќ≤/2 dW (t).                (3.78)
This includes geometric Brownian motion as the special case ќ≤ = 2; some
empirical studies have found that ќ≤ < 2 gives a better пђБt to stock price data.

we see that the instantaneous volatility ѕГS(t)(ќ≤вИТ2)/2 depends on the current

and then invert the transformation from S to X to get S(t) = X(t)1/(2вИТќ≤) .
The case ќ≤ < 1 presents special complications because of the behavior of S
near 0; simulation of this case is investigated in Andersen and Andreasen [13].



3.5 Processes with Jumps
Although the vast majority of models used in derivatives pricing assume that
the underlying assets have continuous sample paths, many studies have found
evidence of the importance of jumps in prices and have advocated the inclu-
sion of jumps in pricing models. Compared with a normal distribution, the
logarithm of a price process with jumps is often leptokurtotic, meaning that it
has a high peak and heavy tails, features typical of market data. In this sec-
tion we discuss a few relatively simple models with jumps, highlighting issues
that aпђАect the implementation of Monte Carlo.

3.5.1 A Jump-DiпђАusion Model

Merton [263] introduced and analyzed one of the пђБrst models with both jump
and diпђАusion terms for the pricing of derivative securities. Merton applied this
model to options on stocks and interpreted the jumps as idiosyncratic shocks
aпђАecting an individual company but not the market as a whole. Similar models
have subsequently been applied to indices, exchange rates, commodity prices,
and interest rates.
   MertonвАЩs jump-diпђАusion model can be speciпђБed through the SDE
                       dS(t)
                             = ¬µ dt + ѕГ dW (t) + dJ(t)                    (3.79)
                       S(tвИТ)
where ¬µ and ѕГ are constants, W is a standard one-dimensional Brownian
motion, and J is a process independent of W with piecewise constant sample
paths. In particular, J is given by
                                                    3.5 Processes with Jumps     135

                                       
                                       N (t)
                              J(t) =           (Yj вИТ 1)                        (3.80)
                                       j=1

where Y1 , Y2 , . . . are random variables and N (t) is a counting process. This
means that there are random arrival times

                               0 < ѕД1 < ѕД2 < ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј

and
                            N (t) = sup{n : ѕДn вЙ§ t}
counts the number of arrivals in [0, t]. The symbol dJ(t) in (3.79) stands for
the jump in J at time t. The size of this jump is Yj вИТ 1 if t = ѕДj and 0 if t
does not coincide with any of the ѕДj .
    In the presence of jumps, a symbol like S(t) is potentially ambiguous: if
it is possible for S to jump at t, we need to specify whether S(t) means the
value of S just before or just after the jump. We follow the usual convention
of assuming that our processes are continuous from the right, so

                                S(t) = lim S(u)
                                         uвЖУt

includes the eпђАect of any jump at t. To specify the value just before a potential
jump we write S(tвИТ), which is the limit

                               S(tвИТ) = lim S(u)
                                          uвЖСt

from the left.
   If we write (3.79) as

              dS(t) = ¬µS(tвИТ) dt + ѕГS(tвИТ) dW (t) + S(tвИТ) dJ(t),

we see that the increment dS(t) in S at t depends on the value of S just before
a potential jump at t and not on the value just after the jump. This is as it
should be. The jump in S at time t is S(t) вИТ S(tвИТ). This is 0 unless J jumps
at t, which is to say unless t = ѕДj for some j. The jump in S at ѕДj is

        S(ѕДj ) вИТ S(ѕДj вИТ) = S(ѕДj вИТ)[J(ѕДj ) вИТ J(ѕДj вИТ)] = S(ѕДj вИТ)(Yj вИТ 1),

hence
                              S(ѕДj ) = S(ѕДj вИТ)Yj .
This reveals that the Yj are the ratios of the asset price before and after a
jump вАФ the jumps are multiplicative. This also explains why we wrote Yj вИТ 1
rather than simply Yj in (3.80).
   By restricting the Yj to be positive random variables, we ensure that S(t)
can never become negative. In this case, we see that
136     3 Generating Sample Paths

                           log S(ѕДj ) = log S(ѕДj вИТ) + log Yj ,

so the jumps are additive in the logarithm of the price. Additive jumps are a
natural extension of Brownian motion and multiplicative jumps (as in (3.79))
provide a more natural extension of geometric Brownian motion; see the dis-
cussion at the beginning of Section 3.2. The solution of (3.79) is given by

                                             1   2            ,

which evidently generalizes the corresponding solution for geometric Brownian
motion.
    Thus far, we have not imposed any distributional assumptions on the jump
process J(t). We now consider the simplest model вАФ the one studied by
Merton [263] вАФ which takes N (t) to be a Poisson process with rate ќї. This
makes the interarrival times ѕДj+1 вИТѕДj independent with a common exponential
distribution,
                    P (ѕДj+1 вИТ ѕДj вЙ§ t) = 1 вИТ eвИТќїt , t вЙ• 0.
We further assume that the Yj are i.i.d. and independent of N (as well as W ).
Under these assumptions, J is called a compound Poisson process.
     As noted by Merton [263], the model is particularly tractable when the Yj
are lognormally distributed, because a product of lognormal random variables
is itself lognormal. In more detail, if Yj вИЉ LN (a, b2 ) (so that log Yj вИЉ N (a, b2 ))
then for any пђБxed n,
                              ,

a Poisson mixture of lognormal distributions. Merton [263] used this property

    Recall that in the absence of jumps the drift ¬µ in (3.79) would be the
risk-free rate, assuming the asset pays no dividends and assuming the model
represents the dynamics under the risk-neutral measure. Suppose, for simplic-
ity, that the risk-free rate is a constant r; then the drift is determined by the
condition that S(t)eвИТrt be a martingale. Merton [263] extends this principle
to his jump-diпђАusion model under the assumption that jumps are speciпђБc to
a single stock and can be diversiпђБed away; that is, by assuming that the mar-
ket does not compensate investors for bearing the risk of jumps. We brieпђВy
describe how this assumption determines the drift parameter ¬µ in (3.79).
    A standard property of the Poisson process is that N (t)вИТќїt is a martingale.
A generalization of this property is that

                                  
                                  N (t)
                                          h(Yj ) вИТ ќїE[h(Y )]t

is a martingale for i.i.d. Y, Y1 , Y2 and any function h for which E[h(Y )] is пђБnite.
Accordingly, the process
                                       J(t) вИТ ќїmt
is a martingale if m = E[Yj ] вИТ 1. The choice of drift parameter in (3.79) that
makes S(t)eвИТrt a martingale is therefore ¬µ = r вИТќїm. In this case, if we rewrite
(3.79) as
                   dS(t)
                         = r dt + ѕГ dW (t) + [dJ(t) вИТ ќїm dt],
                   S(tвИТ)
the last two terms on the right are martingales and the net growth rate in

Simulating at Fixed Dates

We consider two approaches to simulating the jump-diпђАusion model (3.79),
each of which is an instance of a more general strategy for simulating a broader
class of jump-diпђАusion models. In the пђБrst method, we simulate the process
at a пђБxed set of dates 0 = t0 < t1 < ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј < tn without explicitly distinguishing
138     3 Generating Sample Paths

the eпђАects of the jump and diпђАusion terms. In the second method, we simulate
the jump times ѕД1 , ѕД2 , . . . explicitly.
     We continue to assume that N is a Poisson process, that Y1 , Y2 , . . . are
i.i.d., and that N , W , and {Y1 , Y2 , . . .} are mutually independent. We do not
assume the Yj are lognormally distributed, though that will constitute an
interesting special case.
     To simulate S(t) at time t1 , . . . , tn , we generalize (3.81) to

                                 1   2                                    ,
                                                                        N (ti+1 )
         S(ti+1 ) = S(ti )e(¬µвИТ 2 ѕГ )(ti+1 вИТti )+ѕГ[W (ti+1 )вИТW (ti )]                 Yj ,
                                                                       j=N (ti )+1


with the usual convention that the product over j is equal to 1 if N (ti+1 ) =
N (ti ). We can simulate directly from this representation or else set X(t) =
log S(t) and

                                                                                
                                                                              N (ti+1 )
X(ti+1 ) = X(ti ) + (¬µ вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 )(ti+1 вИТ ti ) + ѕГ[W (ti+1 ) вИТ W (ti )] +                    log Yj ;
                                                                            j=N (ti )+1

this recursion replaces products with sums and is preferable, at least if sam-
pling log Yj is no slower than sampling Yj . We can exponentiate simulated
values of the X(ti ) to produce samples of the S(ti ).
    A general method for simulating (3.82) from ti to ti+1 consists of the
following steps:
 1. generate Z вИЉ N (0, 1)
 2. generate N вИЉ Poisson(ќї(ti+1 вИТ ti )) (see Figure 3.9); if N = 0, set M = 0
    and go to Step 4
 3. generate log Y1 , . . . , log YN from their common distribution and set M =
    log Y1 + . . . + log YN
 4. set

           X(ti+1 ) = X(ti ) + (¬µ вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 )(ti+1 вИТ ti ) + ѕГ       ti+1 вИТ ti Z + M.

This method relies on two properties of the Poisson process: the increment
N (ti+1 ) вИТ N (ti ) has a Poisson distribution with mean ќї(ti+1 вИТ ti ), and it is
independent of increments of N over [0, ti ].
    Under further assumptions on the distribution of the Yj , this method can
sometimes be simpliпђБed. If the Yj have the lognormal distribution LN (a, b2 ),
then log Yj вИЉ N (a, b2 ) and

                   

                         log Yj вИЉ N (an, b2 n) = an + b nN (0, 1).
                   j=1

                                                 3.5 Processes with Jumps       139

If the log Yj have a gamma distribution with shape parameter a and scale

has the gamma distribution with shape parameter an and scale parameter
ќ≤. Consequently, in Step 3 above we may sample M directly from a gamma

    Kou [215] proposes and analyzes a model in which | log Yj | has a gamma

     M = R1 вИТ R2
   In 3b , interpret a gamma random variable with shape parameter zero as

                              n!

                          k!(n вИТ k)!

Samples from this distribution can be generated using essentially the same

becomes p вЖР pq(n + 1 вИТ N )/N (1 вИТ q) (where N now refers to the binomial
random variable produced by the algorithm).

Simulating Jump Times

Simulation methods based on (3.82) produce values S(ti ) = exp(X(ti )),
i = 1, . . . , n, with the exact joint distribution of the target process (3.79)
at dates t1 , . . . , tn . Notice, however, that this approach does not identify the
times at which S(t) jumps; rather, it generates the total number of jumps in
each interval (ti , ti+1 ], using the fact that the number of jumps has a Poisson
distribution.
     An alternative approach to simulating (3.79) simulates the jump times
ѕД1 , ѕД2 , . . . explicitly. From one jump time to the next, S(t) evolves like an
140     3 Generating Sample Paths

ordinary geometric Brownian motion because we have assumed that W and
J in (3.79) are independent of each other. It follows that, conditional on the
times ѕД1 , ѕД2 , . . . of the jumps,
                                           1   2
             S(ѕДj+1 вИТ) = S(ѕДj )e(¬µвИТ 2 ѕГ )(ѕДj+1 вИТѕДj )+ѕГ[W (ѕДj+1 )вИТW (ѕДj )]

and
                            S(ѕДj+1 ) = S(ѕДj+1 вИТ)Yj+1 .
Taking logarithms and combining these steps, we get

 X(ѕДj+1 ) = X(ѕДj ) + (¬µ вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 )(ѕДj+1 вИТ ѕДj ) + ѕГ[W (ѕДj+1 ) вИТ W (ѕДj )] + log Yj+1 .

A general scheme for simulating one step of this recursion now takes the
following form:
1. generate Rj+1 from the exponential distribution with mean 1/ќї
2. generate Zj+1 вИЉ N (0, 1)
3. generate log Yj+1
4. set ѕДj+1 = ѕДj + Rj+1 and

          X(ѕДj+1 ) = X(ѕДj ) + (¬µ вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 )Rj+1 + ѕГ      Rj+1 Zj+1 + log Yj+1 .

Recall from Section 2.2.1 that the exponential random variable Rj+1 can be
generated by setting Rj+1 = вИТ log(U )/ќї with U вИЉ Unif[0,1].
    The two approaches to simulating S(t) can be combined. For example,
suppose we пђБx a date t in advance that we would like to include among the
simulated dates. Suppose it happens that ѕДj < t < ѕДj+1 (i.e., N (t) = N (tвИТ) =
j). Then
                                           1   2
                    S(t) = S(ѕДj )e(¬µвИТ 2 ѕГ )(tвИТѕДj )+ѕГ[W (t)вИТW (ѕДj )]
and
                                   1   2
             S(ѕДj+1 ) = S(t)e(¬µвИТ 2 ѕГ )(ѕДj+1 вИТt)+ѕГ[W (ѕДj+1 )вИТW (t)] Yj+1 .
    Both approaches to simulating the basic jump-diпђАusion process (3.79) вАФ
simulating the number of jumps in пђБxed subintervals and simulating the times
at which jumps occur вАФ can be useful at least as approximations in simulating
more general jump-diпђАusion models. Exact simulation becomes diпђГcult when
the times of the jumps and the evolution of the process between jumps are no
longer independent of each other.

Inhomogeneous Poisson Process

A simple extension of the jump-diпђАusion model (3.79) replaces the constant
jump intensity ќї of the Poisson process with a deterministic (nonnegative)
function of time ќї(t). This means that

                 P (N (t + h) вИТ N (t) = 1|N (t)) = ќї(t)h + o(h)
                                                        3.5 Processes with Jumps        141

and N (t) is called an inhomogeneous Poisson process. Like an ordinary Pois-
son process it has independent increments and these increments are Poisson
distributed, but increments over diпђАerent intervals of equal length can have
diпђАerent means. In particular, the number of jumps in an interval (ti , ti+1 ]
has a Poisson distribution with mean ќЫ(ti+1 ) вИТ ќЫ(ti ), where
                                     t
                             ќЫ(t) =     ќї(u) du.
                                            0

Provided this function can be evaluated, simulation based on (3.82) general-
izes easily to the inhomogeneous case: where we previously sampled from the
Poisson distribution with mean ќї(ti+1 вИТ ti ), we now sample from the Poisson
distribution with mean ќЫ(ti+1 ) вИТ ќЫ(ti ).
    It is also possible to simulate the interarrival times of the jumps. The key
property is

    P (ѕДj+1 вИТ ѕДj вЙ§ t|ѕД1 , . . . , ѕДj ) = 1 вИТ exp(вИТ[ќЫ(ѕДj + t) вИТ ќЫ(ѕДj )]),       t вЙ• 0,

provided ќЫ(вИЮ) = вИЮ. We can (at least in principle) sample from this distri-
bution using the inverse transform method discussed in Section 2.2.1. Given
ѕДj , let
                                               

      X = inf    t вЙ• 0 : 1 вИТ exp вИТ           ќї(u) du      =U       ,   U вИЉ Unif[0,1]

then X has the required interarrival time distribution and we may set ѕДj+1 =

where ќЊ is exponentially distributed with mean 1. We may therefore inter-

   If the time-varying intensity ќї(t) is bounded by a constant ќїћД, the jumps of

3.5.2 Pure-Jump Processes

If S(t) is the jump-diпђАusion process in (3.79) with J(t) a compound Poisson
process, then X(t) = log S(t) is a process with independent increments. This
is evident from (3.82) and the fact that both W and J have independent
increments. Geometric Brownian motion also has the property that its loga-
rithm has independent increments. It is therefore natural to ask what other
potentially fruitful models of asset prices might arise from the representation

                               S(t) = S(0) exp(X(t))                              (3.84)

with X having independent increments. Notice that we have adopted the
normalization X(0) = 0.
    The process X is a LeћБvy process if it has stationary, independent incre-
ments and satisпђБes the technical requirement that X(t) converges in dis-
tribution to X(s) as t вЖТ s. Stationarity of the increments means that
X(t + s) вИТ X(s) has the distribution of X(t). Every LeћБvy process can be
represented as the sum of a deterministic drift, a Brownian motion, and a
pure-jump process independent of the Brownian motion (see, e.g., Chapter 4
of Sato [317]). If the number of jumps in every пђБnite interval is almost surely
пђБnite, then the pure-jump component is a compound Poisson process. Hence,
in constructing processes of the form (3.84) with X a LeћБvy process, the only
way to move beyond the jump-diпђАusion process (3.79) is to consider processes
with an inпђБnite number of jumps in пђБnite intervals. We will in fact focus on
pure-jump processes of this type вАФ that is, LeћБvy processes with no Brown-
ian component. Several processes of this type have been proposed as models
of asset prices, and we consider some of these examples. A more extensive
discussion of the simulation of LeћБvy process can be found in Asmussen [21].
                                                 3.5 Processes with Jumps      143

    It should be evident that in considering processes with an inпђБnite number
of jumps in пђБnite intervals, only the пђБrst of the two approaches developed in
Section 3.5.1 is viable: we may be able to simulate the increments of such a
process, but we cannot hope to simulate from one jump to the next. To sim-
ulate a pure-jump LeћБvy process we should therefore consider the distribution
of its increments over a пђБxed time grid.
    A random variable Y (more precisely, its distribution) is said to be in-
пђБnitely divisible if for each n = 2, 3, . . ., there are i.i.d. random variables
  (n)         (n)           (n)           (n)
Y1 , . . . , Yn such that Y1 + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + Yn has the distribution of Y . If X is
a LeћБvy process (X(0) = 0), then

    X(t) = X(t/n) + [X(2t/n) вИТ X(t/n)] + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + [X(t) вИТ X((n вИТ 1)t/n)]

decomposes X(t) as the sum of n i.i.d. random variables and shows that X(t)
has an inпђБnitely divisible distribution. Conversely, for each inпђБnitely divisible
distribution there is a LeћБvy process for which X(1) has that distribution.
Simulating a LeћБvy process on a пђБxed time grid is thus equivalent to sampling
from inпђБnitely divisible distributions.
    A LeћБvy process with nondecreasing sample paths is called a subordinator .
A large class of LeћБvy processes (sometimes called processes of type G) can
be represented as W (G(t)) with W Brownian motion and G a subordinator
independent of W . Several of the examples we consider belong to this class.

Gamma Processes

If Y1 , . . . , Yn are independent with distribution Gamma(a/n, ќ≤), then Y1 +
¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј+Yn has distribution Gamma(a, ќ≤); thus, gamma distributions are inпђБnitely
divisible. For each choice of the parameters a and ќ≤ there is a LeћБvy process
(called a gamma process) such that X(1) has distribution Gamma(a, ќ≤). We
can simulate this process on a time grid t1 , . . . , tn by sampling the increments

                 X(ti+1 ) вИТ X(ti ) вИЉ Gamma(a ¬Ј (ti+1 вИТ ti ), ќ≤)

independently, using the methods of Sections 3.4.2.
    A gamma random variable takes only positive values so a gamma process
is nondecreasing. This makes it unsuitable as a model of (the logarithm of) a
risky asset price. Madan and Seneta [243] propose a model based on (3.84) and
X(t) = U (t)вИТD(t), with U and D independent gamma processes representing
the up and down moves of X. They call this the variance gamma process.
Increments of X can be simulated through the increments of U and D.
    If U (1) and D(1) have the same shape and scale parameters, then X admits
an alternative representation as W (G(t)) where W is a standard Brownian
motion and G is a gamma process. In other words, X can be viewed as the
result of applying a random time-change to an ordinary Brownian motion: the
deterministic time argument t has been replaced by the random time G(t),
144    3 Generating Sample Paths

which becomes the conditional variance of W (G(t)) given G(t). This explains
the name вАЬvariance gamma.вАЭ
     Madan et al. [242] consider the more general case W (G(t)) where W now
has drift parameter ¬µ and variance parameter ѕГ 2 . They restrict the shape
parameter of G(1) to be the reciprocal of its scale parameter ќ≤ (so that
E[G(t)] = t) and show that this more general variance gamma process can
still be represented as the diпђАerence U (t) вИТ D(t) of two independent gamma
processes. The shape and scale parameters of U (1) and D(1) should be chosen
to satisfy aU = aD = 1/ќ≤ and

                                 ѕГ2 ќ≤
                       ќ≤U ќ≤D =        ,   ќ≤U вИТ ќ≤D = ¬µќ≤.
                                  2
    The general variance gamma process can therefore still be simulated as
the diпђАerence between two independent gamma processes. Alternatively, we
can use the representation X(t) = W (G(t)) for simulation. Conditional on the
increment G(ti+1 )вИТG(ti ), the increment W (G(ti+1 ))вИТW (G(ti )) has a normal
distribution with mean ¬µ[G(ti+1 ) вИТ G(ti )] and variance ѕГ 2 [G(ti+1 ) вИТ G(ti )].
Hence, we can simulate X as follows:
 1. generate Y вИЉ Gamma((ti+1 вИТ ti )/ќ≤, ќ≤) (this is the increment in G)
 2. generate Z вИЉ N (0, 1)         вИЪ
 3. set X(ti+1 ) = X(ti ) + ¬µY + ѕГ Y Z.
The relative merits of this method and simulation through the diпђАerence of U
and D depend on the implementation details of the methods used for sampling
from the gamma and normal distributions.
    Figure 3.11 compares two variance gamma densities with a normal density;
all three have mean 0 and standard deviation 0.4. The пђБgure illustrates the
much higher kurtosis that can be achieved within the variance gamma family.
Although the examples in the пђБgure are symmetric, positive and negative
skewness can be introduced through the parameter ¬µ.

Normal Inverse Gaussian Processes

This class of processes, described in BarndorпђА-Nielsen [36], has some similari-
ties to the variance gamma model. It is a LeћБvy process whose increments have
a normal inverse Gaussian distribution; it can also be represented through a
random time-change of Brownian motion.
    The inverse Gaussian distribution with parameters ќі, ќ≥ > 0 has density

    Eberlein [109] discusses the use of the NIG LeћБvy processes (in fact, a more
general family called generalized hyperbolic LeћБvy processes) in modeling log
returns. BarndorпђА-Nielsen [36] proposes several mechanisms for constructing
models of price processes using NIG LeћБvy processes as a building block.
    As with the variance gamma process of Madan and Seneta [243], there
are in principle two strategies for simulating X on a discrete time grid. We
can simulate the increments by sampling from the NIG distribution directly
or we can use the representation as a time-changed Brownian motion (as in
(3.86)). However, direct sampling from the NIG distribution does not appear
to be particularly convenient, so we consider only the second of these two
alternatives.
    To simulate X(t) as W (Y (t)) we need to be able to generate the incre-
ments of Y by sampling from the (ordinary) inverse Gaussian distribution. An
interesting method for doing this was developed by Michael, Schucany, and
Haas [264]. Their method uses the fact that if Y has the density in (3.85),
then

    To simulate an increment of the NIG process X(t) = W (Y (t)) from ti to
ti+1 , we use the algorithm in Figure 3.13 to generate a sample Y from the
inverse Gaussian distribution with parameters ќі(ti+1 вИТ ti ) and ќ≥; we then set
                                                вИЪ
                        X(ti+1 ) = X(ti ) + ќ≤Y + Y Z

with Z вИЉ N (0, 1). (Recall that ќ≤ is the drift of W in the NIG parameteriza-
tion.)
    Despite the evident similarity between this construction and the one used
for the variance gamma process, a result of Asmussen and RosinћБski [25] points
to an important distinction between the two processes: the cumulative eпђАect of
small jumps can be well-approximated by Brownian motion in a NIG process
but not in a variance gamma process. Loosely speaking, even the small jumps
of the variance gamma process are too large or too infrequent to look like
Brownian motion. Asmussen and RosinћБski [25] discuss the use and applicabil-
ity of a Brownian approximation to small jumps in simulating LeћБvy processes.



Stable Paretian Processes

A distribution is called stable if for each n вЙ• 2 there are constants an > 0 and
bn such that
                       X 1 + X 2 + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + X n = d an X + b n ,
148    3 Generating Sample Paths

where X, X1 , . . . , Xn are independent random variables with that distribution.
(The symbol вАЬ=d вАЭ indicates equality in distribution.) If bn = 0 for all n, the
distribution is strictly stable. The best known example is the standard normal
distribution for which

                        X1 + X2 + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + Xn =d n1/2 X.

In fact, an must be of the form n1/ќ± for some 0 < ќ± вЙ§ 2 called the index
of the stable distribution. This is Theorem VI.1.1 of Feller [119]; for broader
coverage of the topic see Samorodnitsky and Taqqu [316].
    Stable random variables are inпђБnitely divisible and thus deпђБne LeћБvy
processes. Like the other examples in this section, these LeћБvy processes have
no Brownian component (except in the case of Brownian motion itself) and
are thus pure-jump processes. They can often be constructed by applying a
random time change to an ordinary Brownian motion, the time change itself
having stable increments.
    Only the normal distribution has stable index ќ± = 2. Non-normal stable
distributions (those with ќ± < 2) are often called stable Paretian. These are
heavy-tailed distributions: if X has stable index ќ± < 2, then E[|X|p ] is inпђБnite
for p вЙ• ќ±. In particular, all stable Paretian distributions have inпђБnite variance
and those with ќ± вЙ§ 1 have E[|X|] = вИЮ. Mandelbrot [246] proposed using sta-
ble Paretian distributions to model the high peaks and heavy tails (relative to
the normal distribution) of market returns. InпђБnite variance suggests that the
tails of these distributions may be too heavy for market data, but see Rachev
and Mittnik [302] for a comprehensive account of applications in пђБnance.
    Stable random variables have probability densities but these are rarely
available explicitly; stable distributions are usually described through their
characteristic functions. The density is known for the normal case ќ± = 2; the
Cauchy (or t1 ) distribution, corresponding to ќ± = 1 and density
                                1 1
                      f (x) =            ,   вИТвИЮ < x < вИЮ;
                                ѕА 1 + x2
and the case ќ± = 1/2 with density
                           1
                  f (x) = вИЪ xвИТ3/2 exp(вИТ1/(2x)),        x > 0.
                           2ѕА
This last example may be viewed as a limiting case of the inverse Gaussian
distribution with ќ≥ = 0. Through a пђБrst passage time interpretation (see Feller
[119], Example VI.2(f)), the Cauchy distribution may be viewed as a limiting
case of the NIG distribution ќ± = ќ≤ = ¬µ = 0. Both densities given above can be
generalized by introducing scale and location parameters (as in [316], p.10).
This follows from the simple observation that if X has a stable distribution
then so does ¬µ + ѕГX, for any constants ¬µ, ѕГ.
    As noted in Example 2.1.2, samples from the Cauchy distribution can be
generated using the inverse transform method. If Z вИЉ N (0, 1) then 1/Z 2 has
                             3.6 Forward Rate Models: Continuous Rates        149

the stable density above with ќ± = 1/2, so this case is also straightforward.
Perhaps surprisingly, it is also fairly easy to sample from other stable distribu-
tions even though their densities are unknown. An important tool in sampling
from stable distributions is the following representation: if V is uniformly dis-
tributed over [вИТѕА/2, ѕА/2] and W is exponentially distributed with mean 1,
then
                                                      (1вИТќ±)/ќ±
                       sin(ќ±V )       cos((1 вИТ ќ±)V )
                    (cos(V ))1/ќ±            W
has a symmetric ќ±-stable distribution; see p.42 of Samorodnitsky and Taqqu
[316] for a proof. As noted there, this reduces to the Box-Muller method
(see Section 2.3.2) when ќ± = 2. Chambers, Mallows, and Stuck [79] develop
simulation procedures based on this representation and additional transfor-
mations. Samorodnitsky and Taqqu [316], pp.46-49, provide computer code
for sampling from an arbitrary stable distribution, based on Chambers et al.
[79].
    Feller [119], p.336, notes that the LeћБvy process generated by a symmet-
ric stable distribution can be constructed through a random time change of
Brownian motion. This also follows from the observation in Samorodnitsky
and Taqqu [316], p.21, that a symmetric stable random variable can be gener-
ated as the product of a normal random variable and a positive stable random
variable, a construction similar to (3.86).


3.6 Forward Rate Models: Continuous Rates
The distinguishing feature of the models considered in this section and the
next is that they explicitly describe the evolution of the full term structure of
interest rates. This contrasts with the approach in Sections 3.3 and 3.4 based
on modeling the dynamics of just the short rate r(t). In a setting like the
Vasicek model or the Cox-Ingersoll-Ross model, the current value of the short
rate determines the current value of all other term structure quantities вАФ
forward rates, bond prices, etc. In these models, the state of the world is com-
pletely summarized by the value of the short rate. In multifactor extensions,
like those described in Section 3.3.3, the state of the world is summarized by
the current values of a пђБnite number (usually small) of underlying factors;
from the values of these factors all term structure quantities are determined,
at least in principle.
    In the framework developed by Heath, Jarrow, and Morton [174] (HJM),
the state of the world is described by the full term structure and not necessarily
by a пђБnite number of rates or factors. The key contribution of HJM lies in
identifying the restriction imposed by the absence of arbitrage on the evolution
of the term structure.
    At any point in time the term structure of interest rates can be described
in various equivalent ways вАФ through the prices or yields of zero-coupon
150    3 Generating Sample Paths

bonds or par bonds, through forward rates, and through swap rates, to name
just a few examples. The HJM framework models the evolution of the term
structure through the dynamics of the forward rate curve. It could be argued
that forward rates provide the most primitive description of the term structure
(and thus the appropriate starting point for a model) because bond prices and
yields reпђВect averages of forward rates across maturities, but it seems diпђГcult
to press this point too far.
    From the perspective of simulation, this section represents a departure
from the previous topics of this chapter. Thus far, we have focused on models
that can be simulated exactly, at least at a пђБnite set of dates. In the gener-
ality of the HJM setting, some discretization error is usually inevitable. HJM
simulation might therefore be viewed more properly as a topic for Chapter 6;
we include it here because of its importance and because of special simulation
issues it raises.

3.6.1 The HJM Framework

The HJM framework describes the dynamics of the forward rate curve
{f (t, T ), 0 вЙ§ t вЙ§ T вЙ§ T вИЧ } for some ultimate maturity T вИЧ (e.g., 20 or 30
years from today). Think of this as a curve in the maturity argument T for
each value of the time argument t; the length of the curve shrinks as time ad-
vances because t вЙ§ T вЙ§ T вИЧ . Recall that the forward rate f (t, T ) represents the
instantaneous continuously compounded rate contracted at time t for riskless
borrowing or lending at time T вЙ• t. This is made precise by the relation
                                                      
                                                  T
                       B(t, T ) = exp вИТ               f (t, u) du
                                              t


between bond prices and forward rates, which implies
                                           вИВ
                          f (t, T ) = вИТ      log B(t, T ).                 (3.87)
                                          вИВT
The short rate is r(t) = f (t, t). Figure 3.14 illustrates this notation and the
evolution of the forward curve.
   In the HJM setting, the evolution of the forward curve is modeled through
an SDE of the form

                    df (t, T ) = ¬µ(t, T ) dt + ѕГ(t, T ) dW (t).           (3.88)

In this equation and throughout, the diпђАerential df is with respect to time t
and not maturity T . The process W is a standard d-dimensional Brownian
motion; d is the number of factors, usually equal to 1, 2, or 3. Thus, while the
forward rate curve is in principle an inпђБnite-dimensional object, it is driven by
a low-dimensional Brownian motion. The coeпђГcients ¬µ and ѕГ in (3.88) (scalar
and d -valued, respectively) could be stochastic or could depend on current
                                 3.6 Forward Rate Models: Continuous Rates             151


            f (t1, t2)
             f (0,t2)

             f (0,t1)
                                                     r (t2)
                                  r (t1)
                r (0)




                         0             t1                 t2                T*

Fig. 3.14. Evolution of forward curve. At time 0, the forward curve f (0, ¬Ј) is deпђБned
for maturities in [0, T вИЧ ] and the short rate is r(0) = f (0, 0). At t > 0, the forward
curve f (t, ¬Ј) is deпђБned for maturities in [t, T вИЧ ] and the short rate is r(t) = f (t, t).


and past levels of forward rates. We restrict attention to the case in which ¬µ
and ѕГ are deterministic functions of t, T вЙ• t, and the current forward curve
{f (t, u), t вЙ§ u вЙ§ T вИЧ }. Subject to technical conditions, this makes the evolution
of the curve Markovian. We could make this more explicit by writing, e.g.,
ѕГ(f, t, T ), but to lighten notation we omit the argument f . See Heath, Jarrow,
and Morton [174] for the precise conditions needed for (3.88).
    We interpret (3.88) as modeling the evolution of forward rates under the
risk-neutral measure (meaning, more precisely, that W is a standard Brownian
motion under that measure). We know that the absence of arbitrage imposes a
condition on the risk-neutral dynamics of asset prices: the price of a (dividend-
free) asset must be a martingale when divided by the numeraire
                                            t
                             ќ≤(t) = exp        r(u) du .
                                              0

Forward rates are not, however, asset prices, so it is not immediately clear
what restriction the absence of arbitrage imposes on the dynamics in (3.88).
To пђБnd this restriction we must start from the dynamics of asset prices, in
particular bonds. Our account is informal; see Heath, Jarrow, and Morton
[174] for a rigorous development.
   To make the discounted bond prices B(t, T )/ќ≤(t) positive martingales, we
posit dynamics of the form
          dB(t, T )
                    = r(t) dt + ќљ(t, T ) dW (t),      0 вЙ§ t вЙ§ T вЙ§ T вИЧ.             (3.89)
          B(t, T )

The bond volatilities ќљ(t, T ) may be functions of current bond prices (equiva-
lently, of current forward rates since (3.87) makes a one-to-one correspondence

between the two). Through (3.87), the dynamics in (3.89) constrain the evo-
lution of forward rates. By ItoћВвАЩs formula,

         d log B(t, T ) = [r(t) вИТ 12 ќљ(t, T ) ќљ(t, T )] dt + ќљ(t, T ) dW (t).

If we now diпђАerentiate with respect to T and then interchange the order of

To eliminate ќљ(t, T ) entirely, notice that
                                                 T
                        ќљ(t, T ) = вИТ                  ѕГ(t, u) du + constant.

this is the risk-neutral drift imposed by the absence of arbitrage. Substituting

                                           
                                            T

                                        t

This equation characterizes the arbitrage-free dynamics of the forward curve
under the risk-neutral measure; it is the centerpiece of the HJM framework.
    Using a subscript j = 1, . . . , d to indicate vector components, we can write
(3.91) as
                                T              
                
                d                                      d
   df (t, T ) =     ѕГj (t, T )      ѕГj (t, u) du dt +      ѕГj (t, T ) dWj (t). (3.92)
               j=1                  t                                 j=1
                               3.6 Forward Rate Models: Continuous Rates      153

This makes it evident that each factor contributes a term to the drift and that
the combined drift is the sum of the contributions of the individual factors.
    In (3.91), the drift is determined once ѕГ is speciпђБed. This contrasts with the
dynamics of the short rate models in Sections 3.3 and 3.4 where parameters
of the drift could be speciпђБed independent of the diпђАusion coeпђГcient without
introducing arbitrage. Indeed, choosing parameters of the drift is essential in
calibrating short rate models to an observed set of bond prices. In contrast,
an HJM model is automatically calibrated to an initial set of bond prices
B(0, T ) if the initial forward curve f (0, T ) is simply chosen consistent with
these bond prices through (3.87). Put slightly diпђАerently, calibrating an HJM
model to an observed set of bond prices is a matter of choosing an appropriate
initial condition rather than choosing a parameter of the model dynamics. The
eпђАort in calibrating an HJM model lies in choosing ѕГ to match market prices
of interest rate derivatives in addition to matching bond prices.
    We illustrate the HJM framework with some simple examples.
Example 3.6.1 Constant ѕГ. Consider a single-factor (d = 1) model in which
ѕГ(t, T ) вЙ° ѕГ for some constant ѕГ. The interpretation of such a model is that
each increment dW (t) moves all points on the forward curve {f (t, u), t вЙ§ u вЙ§
T вИЧ } by an equal amount ѕГ dW (t); the diпђАusion term thus introduces only
parallel shifts in the forward curve. But a model in which the forward curve
makes only parallel shifts admits arbitrage opportunities: one can construct
a costless portfolio of bonds that will have positive value under every parallel
shift. From (3.90) we пђБnd that an HJM model with constant ѕГ has drift
                                          T
                        ¬µ(t, T ) = ѕГ           ѕГ du = ѕГ 2 (T вИТ t).
                                          t

In particular, the drift will vary (slightly, because ѕГ 2 is small) across maturi-
ties, keeping the forward curve from making exactly parallel movements. This
small adjustment to the dynamics of the forward curve is just enough to keep
the model arbitrage-free. In this case, we can solve (3.91) to пђБnd
                                          t
               f (t, T ) = f (0, T ) +         ѕГ 2 (T вИТ u) du + ѕГW (t)
                                          0
                       = f (0, T ) + 12 ѕГ 2 [T 2 вИТ (T вИТ t)2 ] + ѕГW (t).

   In essentially any model, the identity r(t) = f (t, t) implies

Comparing this with (3.50), we пђБnd that an HJM model with constant ѕГ
coincides with a Ho-Lee model with calibrated drift. 2
Example 3.6.2 Exponential ѕГ. Another convenient parameterization takes
ѕГ(t, T ) = ѕГ exp(вИТќ±(T вИТ t)) for some constants ѕГ, ќ± > 0. In this case, the
diпђАusion term ѕГ(t, T ) dW (t) moves forward rates for short maturities more
than forward rates for long maturities. The drift is given by
                             T
                 2 вИТќ±(T вИТt)                    ѕГ 2  вИТ2ќ±(T вИТt)
     ¬µ(t, T ) = ѕГ e             eвИТќ±(T вИТu) du =      e          вИТ eвИТќ±(T вИТt) .
                             t                 ќ±
An argument similar to the one used in Example 3.6.1 shows that the short
rate in this case is described by the Vasicek model with time-varying drift
parameters.
   This example and the one that precedes it may be misleading. It would be
incorrect to assume that the short rate process in an HJM setting will always
have a convenient description. Indeed, such examples are exceptional. 2
Example 3.6.3 Proportional ѕГ. It is tempting to consider a speciпђБcation of
the form ѕГ(t, T ) = ѕГћГ(t, T )f (t, T ) for some deterministic ѕГћГ depending only on
t and T . This would make ѕГћГ(t, T ) the volatility of the forward rate f (t, T )
and would suggest that the distribution of f (t, T ) is approximately lognor-
mal. However, Heath et al. [174] note that this choice of ѕГ is inadmissible: it
produces forward rates that grow to inпђБnity in пђБnite time with positive prob-
ability. The diпђГculty, speaking loosely, is that if ѕГ is proportional to the level
of rates, then the drift is proportional to the rates squared. This violates the
linear growth condition ordinarily required for the existence and uniqueness of
solutions to SDEs (see Appendix B.2). Market conventions often presuppose
the existence of a (proportional) volatility for forward rates, so the failure
of this example could be viewed as a shortcoming of the HJM framework.
We will see in Section 3.7 that the diпђГculty can be avoided by working with
simple rather than continuously compounded forward rates. 2

Forward Measure
Although the HJM framework is usually applied under the risk-neutral mea-
sure, only a minor modiпђБcation is necessary to work in a forward measure. Fix
a maturity TF and recall that the forward measure associated with TF corre-
sponds to taking the bond B(t, TF ) as numeraire asset. The forward measure
PTF can be deпђБned relative to the risk-neutral measure Pќ≤ through
                            dPTF            B(t, TF )ќ≤(0)
                                        =                 .
                             dPќ≤    t       ќ≤(t)B(0, TF )
From the bond dynamics in (3.89), we пђБnd that this ratio is given by
                 t                           t

                    ќљ(u, TF ) ќљ(u, TF ) du +     ќљ(u, TF ) dW (u) .

By the Girsanov Theorem, the process W TF deпђБned by

                       dW TF (t) = вИТќљ(t, TF ) dt + dW (t)

is therefore a standard Brownian motion under PTF . Recalling that ќљ(t, T ) is
the integral of вИТѕГ(t, u) from u = t to u = T , we пђБnd that the forward rate
dynamics (3.91) become

      df (t, T ) = вИТѕГ(t, T ) ќљ(t, T ) dt + ѕГ(t, T ) [ќљ(t, TF ) dt + dW TF (t)]
               = вИТѕГ(t, T ) [ќљ(t, T ) вИТ ќљ(t, TF )] dt + ѕГ(t, T ) dW TF (t)

 for t вЙ§ T вЙ§ TF . Thus, the HJM dynamics under the forward measure are
similar to the dynamics under the risk-neutral measure, but where we previ-
ously integrated ѕГ(t, u) from t to T , we now integrate вИТѕГ(t, u) from T to TF .
Notice that f (t, TF ) is a martingale under PTF , though none of the forward
rates is a martingale under the risk-neutral measure.

3.6.2 The Discrete Drift

Except under very special choices of ѕГ, exact simulation of (3.91) is infeasible.
Simulation of the general HJM forward rate dynamics requires introducing a
discrete approximation. In fact, each of the two arguments of f (t, T ) requires
discretization. For the пђБrst argument, пђБx a time grid 0 = t0 < t1 < ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј <
tM . Even at a пђБxed time ti , it is generally not possible to represent the full
forward curve f (ti , T ), ti вЙ§ T вЙ§ T вИЧ , so instead we пђБx a grid of maturities
and approximate the forward curve by its value for just these maturities. In
principle, the time grid and the maturity grid could be diпђАerent; however,
assuming that the two sets of dates are the same greatly simpliпђБes notation
with little loss of generality.
    We use hats to distinguish discretized variables from their exact continuous-
time counterparts. Thus, fЋЖ(ti , tj ) denotes the discretized forward rate for ma-
turity tj as of time ti , j вЙ• i, and BћВ(ti , tj ) denotes the corresponding bond
price,                                                            
                                        
                                        jвИТ1
                   BћВ(ti , tj ) = exp вИТ     fЋЖ(ti , t )[t +1 вИТ t ] .         (3.94)
                                           =i
    To avoid introducing any more discretization error than necessary, we
would like the initial values of the discretized bonds BћВ(0, tj ) to coincide with
the exact values B(0, tj ) for all maturities tj on the discrete grid. Comparing
(3.94) with the equation that precedes (3.87), we see that this holds if

                        =i                                                   =i

 This is the discrete version of the HJM drift; it ensures that the discretized
discounted bond prices are martingales.
    To see the connection between this expression and the continuous-time

approximated by an integral. Dividing both sides of (3.97) by tj+1 вИТ tj = h,
we пђБnd that for small h the discrete drift is approximately

where the Zi = (Zi1 , . . . , Zid ), i = 1, . . . , M , are independent N (0, I) random

    We derived (3.97) by starting from the principle that the discretized dis-
counted bond prices should be martingales. But what are the practical impli-
cations of using some other approximation to the continuous drift instead of
this one? To appreciate the consequences, consider the following experiment.
Imagine simulating paths of fЋЖ as in (3.96) or (3.98). From a path of fЋЖ we may
extract a path

        rћВ(t0 ) = fЋЖ(t0 , t0 ),   rћВ(t1 ) = fЋЖ(t1 , t1 ),   ...   rћВ(tM ) = fЋЖ(tM , tM ),

of the discretized short rate rћВ. From this we can calculate a discount factor
                                                              
                                       
                                       jвИТ1
                     DћВ(tj ) = exp вИТ       rћВ(ti )[ti+1 вИТ ti ]            (3.99)
                                                 i=0

for each maturity tj . Imagine repeating this over n independent paths and let
DћВ(1) (tj ), . . . , DћВ(n) (tj ) denote discount factors calculated over these n paths.
A consequence of the strong law of large numbers, the martingale property,
and the initialization in (3.95) is that, almost surely,

                   1  (i)
                       n
                         DћВ (tj ) вЖТ E[DћВ(tj )] = BћВ(0, tj ) = B(0, tj ).
                   n i=1

This means that if we simulate using (3.97) and then use the simulation to
price a bond, the simulation price converges to the value to which the model
was ostensibly calibrated. With some other choice of discrete drift, the simu-
lation price would in general converge to something that diпђАers from B(0, tj ),
even if only slightly. Thus, the martingale condition is not simply a theoretical
feature вАФ it is a prerequisite for internal consistency of the simulated model.
Indeed, failure of this condition can create the illusion of arbitrage opportu-
nities. If E[DћВ(1) (tj )] = B(0, tj ), the simulation would be telling us that the
market has mispriced the bond.
    The errors (or apparent arbitrage opportunities) that may arise from using
a diпђАerent approximation to the continuous-time drift may admittedly be
quite small. But given that we have a simple way of avoiding such errors and
given that the form of the drift is the central feature of the HJM framework,
we may as well restrict ourselves to (3.97). This form of the discrete drift
appears to be in widespread use in the industry; it is explicit in Andersen
[11].

Forward Measure

Through an argument similar to the one leading to (3.97), we can пђБnd the ap-
propriate form of the discrete drift under the forward measure. In continuous

time, the forward measure for maturity TF is characterized by the require-

TF is the numeraire asset associated with this measure. In the discrete ap-
proximation, if we take tM = TF , then we require that BћВ(ti , tj )/BћВ(ti , tM ) be
a martingale in i for each j. This ratio is given by

3.6.3 Implementation

Once we have identiпђБed the discrete form of the drift, the main consideration
in implementing an HJM simulation is keeping track of indices. The notation
fЋЖ(ti , tj ) is convenient in describing the discretized model вАФ the пђБrst argument
shows the current time, the second argument shows the maturity to which
this forward rate applies. But in implementing the simulation we are not
interested in keeping track of an M √Ч M matrix of rates as the notation
fЋЖ(ti , tj ) might suggest. At each time step, we need only the vector of current
rates. To implement an HJM simulation we need to adopt some conventions
regarding the indexing of this vector.
     Recall that our time and maturity grid consists of a set of dates 0 =
t0 < t1 < ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј < tM . If we identify tM with the ultimate maturity T вИЧ in
the continuous-time model, then tM is the maturity of the longest-maturity
bond represented in the model. In light of (3.94), this means that the last
forward rate relevant to the model applies to the interval [tMвИТ1 , tM ]; this is
the forward rate with maturity argument tMвИТ1 . Thus, our initial vector of
forward rates consists of the M components fЋЖ(0, 0), fЋЖ(0, t1 ), . . . , fЋЖ(0, tMвИТ1 ),
which is consistent with the initialization (3.95). At the start of the simulation
we will represent this vector as (f1 , . . . , fM ). Thus, our пђБrst convention is to
use 1 rather than 0 as the lowest index value.
     As the simulation evolves, the number of relevant rates decreases. At time
ti , only the rates fЋЖ(ti , ti ), . . . , fЋЖ(ti , tMвИТ1 ) are meaningful. We need to specify
how these M вИТ i rates should be indexed, given that initially we had a vector
                                3.6 Forward Rate Models: Continuous Rates                161

of M rates: we can either pad the initial portion of the vector with irrelevant
data or we can shorten the length of the vector. We choose the latter and
represent the M вИТ i rates remaining at ti as the vector (f1 , . . . , fMвИТi ). Thus,
our second convention is to index forward rates by relative maturity rather
than absolute maturity. At time ti , fj refers to the forward rate fЋЖ(ti , ti+jвИТ1 ).
Under this convention f1 always refers to the current level of the short rate
because rћВ(ti ) = fЋЖ(ti , ti ).
    Similar considerations apply to ¬µћВ(ti , tj ) and ѕГћВk (ti , tj ), k = 1, . . . , d, and
we adopt similar conventions for the variables representing these terms. For
values of ¬µћВ we use variables mj and for values of ѕГћВk we use variables sj (k);
in both cases the subscript indicates a relative maturity and in the case of
sj (k) the argument k = 1, . . . , d refers the factor index in a d-factor model.
We design the indexing so that the simulation step from tiвИТ1 to ti indicated
in (3.98) becomes

                                    
                                    d
   fj вЖР fj+1 + mj [ti вИТ tiвИТ1 ] +          sj (k)   ti вИТ tiвИТ1 Zik ,   j = 1, . . . , M вИТ i.
                                    k=1

Thus, in advancing from tiвИТ1 to ti we want

                 mj = ¬µћВ(tiвИТ1 , ti+jвИТ1 ),     sj (k) = ѕГћВk (tiвИТ1 , ti+jвИТ1 ).        (3.101)

In particular, recall that ѕГћВ may depend on the current vector of forward rates;
as implied by (3.101), the values of all sj (k) should be determined before the
forward rates are updated.
    To avoid repeated calculation of the intervals between dates ti , we intro-
duce the notation
                         hi = ti вИТ tiвИТ1 , , i = 1, . . . , M.
These values do not change in the course of a simulation so we use the vector
(h1 , . . . , hM ) to represent these same values at all steps of the simulation.
    We now proceed to detail the steps in an HJM simulation. We separate
the algorithm into two parts, one calculating the discrete drift parameter at
a пђБxed time step, the other looping over time steps and updating the forward
curve at each step. Figure 3.16 illustrates the calculation of

    Figure 3.17 shows an algorithm for a single replication in an HJM simu-
