pendent replications. This algorithm calls the one in Figure 3.16 to calculate
the discrete drift for all remaining maturities at each time step. The two algo-
rithms could obviously be combined, but keeping them separate should help
clarify the various steps. In addition, it helps stress the point that in propa-
gating the forward curve from tiвИТ1 to ti , we пђБrst evaluate the sj (k) and mj
using the forward rates at step i вИТ 1 and then update the rates to get their
values at step i.
    To make this point a bit more concrete, suppose we speciпђБed a single-factor
model with ѕГћВ(ti , tj ) = ѕГћГ(i, j)fЋЖ(ti , tj ) for some пђБxed values ѕГћГ(i, j). This makes
each ѕГћВ(ti , tj ) proportional to the corresponding forward rate. We noted in Ex-
ample 3.6.3 that this type of diпђАusion term is inadmissible in the continuous-
time limit, but it can be (and often is) used in practice so long as the incre-
ments hi are kept bounded away from zero. In this model it should be clear
that in updating fЋЖ(tiвИТ1 , tj ) to fЋЖ(ti , tj ) we need to evaluate ѕГћГ(iвИТ1, j)fЋЖ(tiвИТ1 , tj )
before we update the forward rate.
    Since an HJM simulation is typically used to value interest rate derivatives,
we have included in Figure 3.17 a few additional generic steps illustrating how

a path of the forward curve is used both to compute and to discount the pay-

the placeholder вАЬcashпђВow at ti .вАЭ This cashпђВow is discounted through mul-
tiplication by D, which is easily seen to contain the simulated value of the
discount factor DћВ(ti ) as deпђБned in (3.99). (When D is updated in Figure 3.17,
before the forward rates are updated, f1 records the short rate for the interval
[tiвИТ1 , ti ].) To make the pricing application more explicit, we consider a few
examples.
Example 3.6.4 Bonds. There is no reason to use an HJM simulation to price
bonds вАФ if properly implemented, the simulation will simply return prices
that could have been computed from the initial forward curve. Nevertheless,
we consider this example to help пђБx ideas. We discussed the pricing of a zero-
coupon bond following (3.99); in Figure 3.17 this corresponds to setting P вЖР 1
at the maturity of the bond and P вЖР 0 at all other dates. For a coupon paying
bond with a face value of 100 and a coupon of c, we would set P вЖР c at the
coupon dates and P вЖР 100 + c at maturity. This assumes, of course, that the
coupon dates are among the t1 , . . . , tM . 2
Example 3.6.5 Caps. A caplet is an interest rate derivative providing pro-
tection against an increase in an interest rate for a single period; a cap is
a portfolio of caplets covering multiple periods. A caplet functions almost
like a call option on the short rate, which would have a payoпђА of the form
164     3 Generating Sample Paths

(r(T ) вИТ K)+ for some strike K and maturity T . In practice, a caplet diпђАers
from this in some small but important ways. (For further background, see
Appendix C.)
    In contrast to the instantaneous short rate r(t), the underlying rate in a
caplet typically applies over an interval and is based on discrete compound-
ing. For simplicity, suppose the interval is of the form [ti , ti+1 ]. At ti , the
continuously compounded rate for this interval is fЋЖ(ti , ti ); the corresponding
discretely compounded rate FћВ satisпђБes
                                    1                    ЋЖ
                                                  = eвИТf(ti ,ti )[ti+1 вИТti ] ;
                       1 + FћВ (ti )[ti+1 вИТ ti ]

i.e.,
                                       1     

                      FћВ (ti ) =              ef (ti ,ti )[ti+1 вИТti ] вИТ 1 .
                                   ti+1 вИТ ti

of this). Moreover, this payment is ordinarily made at the end of the interval,

Example 3.6.6 Swaptions. Consider, next, an option to swap пђБxed-rate pay-
ments for пђВoating-rate payments. (See Appendix C for background on swaps
and swaptions.) Suppose the underlying swap begins at tj0 with payments to
be exchanged at dates tj1 , . . . , tjn . If we denote the пђБxed rate in the swap by
R, then the пђБxed-rate payment at tjk is 100R[tjk вИТ tjkвИТ1 ], assuming a principal
or notional amount of 100. As explained in Section C.2 of Appendix C, the
value of the swap at tj0 is
                                     3.7 Forward Rate Models: Simple Rates             165
                                                                                
                            
                            n
        VћВ (tj0 ) = 100 R        BћВ(tj0 , tj )[tj вИТ tjвИТ1 ] + BћВ(tj0 , tjn ) вИТ 1 .
                            =1


The bond prices BћВ(tj0 , tj ) can be computed from the forward rates at tj0
using (3.94).
     The holder of an option to enter this swap will exercise the option if
VћВ (tj0 ) > 0 and let it expire otherwise. (For simplicity, we are assuming
that the option expires at tj0 though similar calculations apply for an op-
tion to enter into a forward swap, in which case the option expiration date
would be prior to tj0 .) Thus, we may view the swaption as having a payoпђА of
max{0, VћВ (tj0 )} at tj0 . In a simulation, we would therefore simulate the for-
ward curve to the option expiration date tj0 ; at that date, calculate the prices
of the bonds BћВ(tj0 , tj ) maturing at the payment dates of the swaps; from
the bond prices calculate the value of the swap VћВ (tj0 ) and thus the swap-
tion payoпђА max{0, VћВ (tj0 )}; record this as the cashпђВow P in the algorithm of
Figure 3.17 and discount it as in the algorithm.
     This example illustrates a general feature of the HJM framework that
contrasts with models based on the short rate as in Sections 3.3 and 3.4.
Consider valuing a 5-year option on a 20-year swap. This instrument involves
maturities as long as 25 years, so valuing it in a model of the short rate could
involve simulating paths over a 25-year horizon. In the HJM framework, if the
initial forward curve extends for 25 years, then we need to simulate only for
5 years; at the expiration of the option, the remaining forward rates contain
all the information necessary to value the underlying swap. Thus, although
the HJM setting involves updating many more variables at each time step, it
may also require far fewer time steps. 2


3.7 Forward Rate Models: Simple Rates
The models considered in this section are closely related to the HJM frame-
work of the previous section in that they describe the arbitrage-free dynamics
of the term structure of interest rates through the evolution of forward rates.
But the models we turn to now are based on simple rather than continu-
ously compounded forward rates. This seemingly minor shift in focus has
surprisingly far-reaching practical and theoretical implications. This model-
ing approach has developed primarily through the work of Miltersen, Sand-
mann, and Sondermann [268], Brace, Gatarek, and Musiela [56], Musiela and
Rutkowski [274], and Jamshidian [197]; it has gained rapid acceptance in the
пђБnancial industry and stimulated a growing stream of research into what are
often called LIBOR market models.
166    3 Generating Sample Paths

3.7.1 LIBOR Market Model Dynamics

The basic object of study in the HJM framework is the forward rate curve
{f (t, T ), t вЙ§ T вЙ§ T вИЧ }. But the instantaneous, continuously compounded for-
ward rates f (t, T ) might well be considered mathematical idealizations вАФ
they are not directly observable in the marketplace. Most market interest
rates are based on simple compounding over intervals of, e.g., three months
or six months. Even the instantaneous short rate r(t) treated in the models
of Sections 3.3 and 3.4 is a bit of a mathematical пђБction because short-term
rates used for pricing are typically based on periods of one to three months.
The term вАЬmarket modelвАЭ is often used to describe an approach to interest
rate modeling based on observable market rates, and this entails a departure
from instantaneous rates.
    Among the most important benchmark interest rates are the London Inter-
Bank OпђАered Rates or LIBOR. LIBOR is calculated daily through an average
of rates oпђАered by banks in London. Separate rates are quoted for diпђАerent
maturities (e.g., three months and six months) and diпђАerent currencies. Thus,
each day new values are calculated for three-month Yen LIBOR, six-month
US dollar LIBOR, and so on.
    LIBOR rates are based on simple interest. If L denotes the rate for an
accrual period of length ќі (think of ќі as 1/4 or 1/2 for three months and six
months respectively, with time measured in years), then the interest earned
on one unit of currency over the accrual period is ќіL. For example, if three-
month LIBOR is 6%, the interest earned at the end of three months on a
principal of 100 is 0.25 ¬Ј 0.06 ¬Ј 100 = 1.50.
    A forward LIBOR rate works similarly. Fix ќі and consider a maturity T .
The forward rate L(0, T ) is the rate set at time 0 for the interval [T, T + ќі].
If we enter into a contract at time 0 to borrow 1 at time T and repay it
with interest at time T + ќі, the interest due will be ќіL(0, T ). As shown in
Appendix C (speciпђБcally equation (C.5)), a simple replication argument leads
to the following identity between forward LIBOR rates and bond prices:
                                    B(0, T ) вИТ B(0, T + ќі)
                      L(0, T ) =                           .                (3.103)
                                        ќіB(0, T + ќі)
This further implies the relation
                                         T +ќі                         
                            1
                 L(0, T ) =     exp              f (0, u) du       вИТ1       (3.104)
                            ќі            T

between continuous and simple forwards, though it is not necessary to intro-
duce the continuous rates to build a model based on simple rates.
   It should be noted that, as is customary in this literature, we treat the
forward LIBOR rates as though they were risk-free rates. LIBOR rates are
based on quotes by banks which could potentially default and this risk is pre-
sumably reпђВected in the rates. US Treasury bonds, in contrast, are generally
                                       3.7 Forward Rate Models: Simple Rates         167

considered to have a negligible chance of default. The argument leading to
(3.103) may not hold exactly if the bonds on one side and the forward rate
on the other reпђВect diпђАerent levels of creditworthiness. We will not, however,
attempt to take account of these considerations.
    Although (3.103) and (3.104) apply in principle to a continuum of matu-
rities T , we consider a class of models in which a пђБnite set of maturities or
tenor dates
                       0 = T0 < T1 < ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј < TM < TM+1
are пђБxed in advance. As argued in Jamshidian [197], many derivative securities
tied to LIBOR and swap rates are sensitive only to a пђБnite set of maturities
and it should not be necessary to introduce a continuum to price and hedge
these securities. Let

                             ќіi = Ti+1 вИТ Ti ,    i = 0, . . . , M,

Spot Measure

We seek a model in which the evolution of the forward LIBOR rates is de-
scribed by a system of SDEs of the form

   dLn (t)
           = ¬µn (t) dt + ѕГn (t) dW (t),            0 вЙ§ t вЙ§ Tn ,       n = 1, . . . , M,   (3.108)
   Ln (t)

with W a d-dimensional standard Brownian motion. The coeпђГcients ¬µn and
ѕГn may depend on the current vector of rates (L1 (t), . . . , LM (t)) as well as the
                                   3.7 Forward Rate Models: Simple Rates        169

current time t. Notice that in (3.108) ѕГn is the (proportional) volatility because
we have divided by Ln on the left, whereas in the HJM setting (3.91) we took
ѕГ(t, T ) to be the absolute level of volatility. At this point, the distinction is
purely one of notation rather than scope because we allow ѕГn (t) to depend
on the current level of rates.
    Recall that in the HJM setting we derived the form of the drift of the
forward rates from the absence of arbitrage. More speciпђБcally, we derived the
drift from the condition that bond prices be martingales when divided by
the numeraire asset. The numeraire we  2 t used is the usual one associated with
the risk-neutral measure, ќ≤(t) = exp( 0 r(u) du). But introducing a short-rate
process r(t) would undermine our objective of developing a model based on
the simple (and thus more realistic) rates Ln (t). We therefore avoid the usual
risk-neutral measure and instead use a numeraire asset better suited to the
tenor dates Ti .
    A simply compounded counterpart of ќ≤(t) works as follows. Start with 1
unit of account at time 0 and buy 1/B1 (0) bonds maturing at T1 . At time T1 ,
reinvest the funds in bonds maturing at time T2 and proceed this way, at each
Ti putting all funds in bonds maturing at time Ti+1 . This trading strategy
earns (simple) interest at rate Li (Ti ) over each interval [Ti , Ti+1 ], just as in
the continuously compounded case a savings account earns interest at rate
r(t) at time t. The initial investment of 1 at time 0 grows to a value of

                                             ,
                                            ќЈ(t)вИТ1
                      B вИЧ (t) = BќЈ(t) (t)          [1 + ќіj Lj (Tj )]
                                             j=0

at time t. Following Jamshidian [197], we take this as numeraire asset and call
the associated measure the spot measure.

Forward Measure

    These observations raise the question of whether we may in fact take the

in the HJM setting, essentially because it makes the HJM drift quadratic in
the current level of rates. To see what happens with simple compounding,
rewrite (3.112) as

                
                n
                  ќіj Lj (t)Ln (t)ѕГn (t) ѕГj (t)

and consider the case of deterministic ѕГi . The numerators in the drift are

                              -               -

                              -               -
                              - 1 + ќіj Lj (t) - вЙ§ 1,

the drift is linearly bounded in Ln (t), making deterministic ѕГi admissible.
This feature is lost in the limit as the compounding period ќіj decreases to
zero. Thus, the distinction between continuous and simple forward rates turns
out to have important mathematical as well as practical implications.

3.7.2 Pricing Derivatives

We have noted two important features of LIBOR market models: they are
based on observable market rates, and (in contrast to the HJM framework)
they admit deterministic volatilities ѕГj . A third important and closely related
feature arises in the pricing of interest rate caps.
    Recall from Example 3.6.5 (or Appendix C.2) that a cap is a collection
of caplets and that each caplet may be viewed as a call option on a simple
forward rate. Consider, then, a caplet for the accrual period [Tn , Tn+1 ]. The
underlying rate is Ln and the value Ln (Tn ) is пђБxed at Tn . With a strike of K,
the capletвАЩs payoпђА is ќіn (Ln (Tn ) вИТ K)+ ; think of the caplet as refunding the
amount by which interest paid at rate Ln (Tn ) exceeds interest paid at rate
K. This payoпђА is made at Tn+1 .
    Let Cn (t) denote the price of this caplet at time t; we know the terminal
value Cn (Tn+1 ) = ќіn (Ln (Tn ) вИТ K)+ and we want to пђБnd the initial value
Cn (0). Under the spot measure, the deпђВated price Cn (t)/B вИЧ (t) must be a
martingale, so                                             
                                         ќіn (Ln (Tn ) вИТ K)+
                    Cn (0) = B вИЧ (0)EвИЧ                        ,
                                              B вИЧ (Tn+1 )
where we have written EвИЧ for expectation under the spot measure. Through
B вИЧ (Tn+1 ), this expectation involves the joint distribution of L1 (T1 ), . . . ,
Ln (Tn ), making its value diпђГcult to discern. In contrast, under the forward
                                     3.7 Forward Rate Models: Simple Rates            173

measure Pn+1 associated with maturity Tn+1 , the martingale property applies
to Cn (t)/Bn+1 (t). We may therefore also write
                                                           
                                         ќіn (Ln (Tn ) вИТ K)+
                 Cn (0) = Bn+1 (0)En+1                        ,
                                             Bn+1 (Tn+1 )
with En+1 denoting expectation under Pn+1 . Conveniently, Bn+1 (Tn+1 ) вЙ° 1,
so this expectation depends only on the marginal distribution of Ln (Tn ). If
we take ѕГn to be deterministic, then Ln (Tn ) has the lognormal distribution
LN (вИТѕГћДn2 (Tn )/2, ѕГћДn2 (Tn )), using the notation in (3.115). In this case, the caplet
price is given by the Black formula (after Black [49]),

                 Cn (0) = BC(Ln (0), ѕГћДn (Tn ), Tn , K, ќіn Bn+1 (0)),

with

       BC(F, ѕГ, T, K, b) =
                 log(F/K) + ѕГ 2 T /2                 log(F/K) вИТ ѕГ 2 T /2
        b Fќ¶              вИЪ                вИТ Kќ¶             вИЪ                    (3.117)

and ќ¶ the cumulative normal distribution. Thus, under the assumption of de-
terministic volatilities, caplets are priced in closed form by the Black formula.
    This formula is frequently used in the reverse direction. Given the market
price of a caplet, one can solve for the вАЬimplied volatilityвАЭ that makes the
formula match the market price. This is useful in calibrating a model to market
data, a point we return to in Section 3.7.4.
    Consider, more generally, a derivative security making a payoпђА of g(L(Tn ))
at Tk , with L(Tn ) = (L1 (T1 ), . . . , LnвИТ1 (TnвИТ1 ), Ln (Tn ), . . . , LM (Tn )) and k вЙ•

    As a further illustration, we consider the pricing of a swaption as described
in Example 3.6.6 and Appendix C.2. Suppose the underlying swap begins at
Tn with пђБxed- and пђВoating-rate payments exchanged at Tn+1 , . . . , TM+1 . From
equation (C.7) in Appendix C, we пђБnd that the forward swap rate at time t

Using (3.107) and noting that BќЈ(t) (t) cancels from the numerator and de-
nominator, this swap rate can be expressed purely in terms of forward LIBOR
rates.
   Consider, now, an option expiring at time Tk вЙ§ Tn to enter into the swap
over [Tn , TM+1 ] with пђБxed rate T . The value of the option at expiration can
be expressed as (cf. equation (C.11))

                          
                          M+1
                                 ќіj Bj (Tk )(R вИТ Sn (Tk ))+ .
                         j=n+1

This can be written as a function g(L(Tk )) of the LIBOR rates. The price
at time zero can therefore be expressed as an expectation using the general
expressions above.
    By applying ItoћВвАЩs formula to the swap rate (3.118), it is not diпђГcult to
conclude that if the forward LIBOR rates have deterministic volatilities, then
the forward swap rate cannot also have a deterministic volatility. In particu-
lar, then, the forward swap rate cannot be geometric Brownian motion under
any equivalent measure. Brace et al. [56] nevertheless use a lognormal ap-
proximation to the swap rate to develop a method for pricing swaptions; their
approximation appears to give excellent results. An alternative approach has
been developed by Jamshidian [197]. He develops a model in which the term
structure is described through a vector (S0 (t), . . . , SM (t)) of forward swap
rates. He shows that one may choose the volatilities of the forward swap rates
to be deterministic, and that in this case swaption prices are given by a variant
of the Black formula. However, in this model, the LIBOR rates cannot also
have deterministic volatilities, so caplets are no longer priced by the Black
formula. One must therefore choose between the two pricing formulas.

3.7.3 Simulation

Pricing derivative securities in LIBOR market models typically requires sim-
ulation. As in the HJM setting, exact simulation is generally infeasible and
some discretization error is inevitable. Because the models of this section deal
with a пђБnite set of maturities from the outset, we need only discretize the
time argument, whereas in the HJM setting both time and maturity required
discretization.
    We пђБx a time grid 0 = t0 < t1 < ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј < tm < tm+1 over which to simulate. It
is sensible to include the tenor dates T1 , . . . , TM+1 among the simulation dates.
In practice, one would often even take ti = Ti so that the simulation evolves
directly from one tenor date to the next. We do not impose any restrictions
on the volatilities ѕГn , though the deterministic case is the most widely used.
The only other speciпђБc case that has received much attention takes ѕГn (t) to
be the product of a deterministic function of time and a function of Ln (t) as
proposed in Andersen and Andreasen [13]. For example, one may take ѕГn (t)
proportional to a power of Ln (t), resulting in a CEV-type of volatility. In either
                                         3.7 Forward Rate Models: Simple Rates            175

this extension or in the case of deterministic volatilities, it often suпђГces to
restrict the dependence on time to piecewise constant functions that change
values only at the Ti . We return to this point in Section 3.7.4.
    Simulation of forward LIBOR rates is a special case of the general prob-
lem of simulating a system of SDEs. One could apply an Euler scheme or a
higher-order method of the type discussed in Chapter 6. However, even if we
restrict ourselves to Euler schemes (as we do here), there are countless alter-
natives. We have many choices of variables to discretize and many choices of
probability measure under which to simulate. Several strategies are compared
both theoretically and numerically in Glasserman and Zhao [151], and the
discussion here draws on that investigation.
    The most immediate application of the Euler scheme under the spot mea-
sure discretizes the SDE (3.116), producing

                LћВn (ti+1 ) = LћВn (ti ) + ¬µn (LћВ(ti ), ti )LћВn (ti )[ti+1 вИТ ti ]
                               + LћВn (ti )     ti+1 вИТ ti ѕГn (ti ) Zi+1              (3.119)

with
                                          
                                          n
                                            ќіj LћВj (ti )ѕГn (ti ) ѕГj (ti )
                   ¬µn (LћВ(ti ), ti ) =
                                         j=ќЈ(ti )
                                                      1 + ќіj LћВj (ti )

and Z1 , Z2 , . . . independent N (0, I) random vectors in d . Here, as in Sec-
tion 3.6.2, we use hats to identify discretized variables. We assume that we
are given an initial set of bond prices B1 (0), . . . , BM+1 (0) and initialize the
simulation by setting

                               Bn (0) вИТ Bn+1 (0)
                   LћВn (0) =                     ,        n = 1, . . . , M,
                                  ќіn Bn+1 (0)

in accordance with (3.105).
    An alternative to (3.119) approximates the LIBOR rates under the spot
measure using

  LћВn (ti+1 ) = LћВn (ti )√Ч
         0                               1
    exp ¬µn (LћВ(ti ), ti ) вИТ 12 ѕГn (ti )2 [ti+1 вИТ ti ] +        ti+1 вИТ ti ѕГn (ti ) Zi+1 .

This is equivalent to applying an Euler scheme to log Ln ; it may also be viewed
as approximating Ln by geometric Brownian motion over [ti , ti+1 ], with drift
and volatility parameters пђБxed at ti . This method seems particularly attractive
in the case of deterministic ѕГn , since then Ln is close to lognormal. A further
property of (3.120) is that it keeps all LћВn positive, whereas (3.119) can produce
negative rates.
    For both of these algorithms it is important to note that our deпђБnition
of ќЈ makes ќЈ right-continuous. For the original continuous-time processes we
176     3 Generating Sample Paths

could just as well have taken ќЈ to be left-continuous, but the distinction is
important in the discrete approximation. If ti = Tk , then ќЈ(ti ) = k + 1 and the
sum in each ¬µn (LћВ(ti ), ti ) starts at k + 1. Had we taken ќЈ to be left-continuous,
we would have ќЈ(Ti ) = k and thus an additional term in each ¬µn . It seems
intuitively more natural to omit this term as time advances beyond Tk since
Lk ceases to be meaningful after Tk . Glasserman and Zhao [151] and Sidenius
[330] both пђБnd that omitting it (i.e., taking ќЈ right-continuous) results in
smaller discretization error.
    Both (3.119) and (3.120) have obvious counterparts for simulation under
the forward measure PM+1 . The only modiпђБcation necessary is to replace
¬µn (LћВ(ti ), ti ) with

                                          
                                          M

Notice that ¬µM вЙ° 0. It follows that if the ѕГM is deterministic and constant
between the ti (for example, constant between tenor dates), then the log Euler
scheme (3.120) with ¬µM = 0 simulates LM without discretization error under
the forward measure PM+1 . None of the Ln is simulated without discretiza-
tion error under the spot measure, but we will see that the spot measure is
nevertheless generally preferable for simulation.

Martingale Discretization

In our discussion of simulation in the HJM setting, we devoted substantial at-
tention to the issue of choosing the discrete drift to keep the model arbitrage-
free even after discretization. It is therefore natural to examine whether an
analogous choice of drift can be made in the LIBOR rate dynamics. In the
HJM setting, we derived the discrete drift from the condition that the dis-
cretized discounted bond prices must be martingales. In the LIBOR market
model, the corresponding requirement is that

Pricing Derivatives

The pricing of a derivative security in a simulation proceeds as follows. Using
any of the methods considered above, we simulate paths of the discretized
variables LћВ1 , . . . , LћВM . Suppose we want to price a derivative with a payoпђА of
g(L(Tn )) at time Tn . Under the spot measure, we simulate to time Tn and
then calculate the deпђВated payoпђА

                                          ,
                                          nвИТ1
                                                        1
                           g(LћВ(Tn )) ¬Ј                          .
                                          j=0 1 + ќіj LћВj (Tj )

Averaging over independent replications produces an estimate of the deriva-
tiveвАЩs price at time 0. If we simulate under the forward measure, the estimate
consists of independent replications of
180    3 Generating Sample Paths

                                            ,
                                            nвИТ1
                    g(LћВ(Tn )) ¬Ј BM+1 (0)         (1 + ќіj LћВj (Tj )).
                                            j=1

    Glasserman and Zhao [151] compare various simulation methods based, in
part, on their discretization error in pricing caplets. For the case of a caplet
over [TnвИТ1 , Tn ], take g(x) = ќіnвИТ1 (x вИТ K)+ in the expressions above. If the ѕГj
are deterministic, the caplet price is given by the Black formula, as explained
in Section 3.7.2. However, because of the discretization error, the simulation
price will not in general converge exactly to the Black price as the number
of replications increase. The bias in pricing caplets serves as a convenient
indication of the magnitude of the discretization error.
    Figure 3.19, reproduced from Glasserman and Zhao [151], graphs biases
in caplet pricing as a function of caplet maturity for various simulation meth-
ods. The horizontal line through the center of each panel corresponds to zero
bias. The error bars around each curve have halfwidths of one standard error,
indicating that the apparent biases are statistically signiпђБcant. Details of the
parameters used for these experiments are reported in Glasserman and Zhao
[151] along with several other examples.
    These and other experiments suggest the following observations. The
smallest biases are achieved by simulating the diпђАerences of deпђВated bond
prices (the Vn in the spot measure and the Xn in the forward measure) using
an Euler scheme for the logarithms of these variables. (See Glasserman and
Zhao [151] for an explanation of the modiпђБed Vn method.) An Euler scheme
for log Dn is nearly indistinguishable from an Euler scheme for Ln . Under the
forward measure PM+1 , the пђБnal caplet is priced without discretization error
by the Euler schemes for log Xn and log Ln ; these share the feature that they
make the discretized rate LћВM lognormal.
    The graphs in Figure 3.19 compare discretization biases but say noth-
ing about the relative variances of the methods. Glasserman and Zhao [151]
пђБnd that simulating under the spot measure usually results in smaller vari-
ance than simulating under the forward measure, especially at high levels of
volatility. An explanation for this is suggested by the expressions (3.109) and
(3.113) for the deпђВated bond prices under the two measures: whereas (3.109)
always lies between 0 and 1, (3.113) can take arbitrarily large values. This
aпђАects derivatives pricing through the discounting of payoпђАs.

3.7.4 Volatility Structure and Calibration

In our discussion of LIBOR market models we have taken the volatility factors
ѕГn (t) as inputs without indicating how they might be speciпђБed. In practice,
these coeпђГcients are chosen to calibrate a model to market prices of actively
traded derivatives, especially caps and swaptions. (The model is automatically
calibrated to bond prices through the relations (3.105) and (3.106).) Once the
model has been calibrated to the market, it can be used to price less liquid

instruments for which market prices may not be readily available. Accurate
and eпђГcient calibration is a major topic in its own right and we can only touch
on the key issues. For a more extensive treatment, see James and Webber [194]
and Rebonato [303]. Similar considerations apply in both the HJM framework
and in LIBOR market models; we discuss calibration in the LIBOR setting
because it is somewhat simpler. Indeed, convenience in calibration is one of
the main advantages of this class of models.
    The variables ѕГn (t) are the primary determinants of both the level of
volatility in forward rates and the correlations between forward rate. It is
often useful to distinguish these two aspects and we will consider the overall
182    3 Generating Sample Paths

level of volatility пђБrst. Suppose we are given the market price of a caplet for
the interval [Tn , Tn+1 ] and from this price we calculate an implied volatility
vn by inverting the Black formula (3.117). (We can assume that the other
parameters of the formula are known.) If we choose ѕГn to be any deterministic
d -valued function satisfying
                                 Tn
                             1
                                     ѕГn (t)2 dt = vn2 ,
                            Tn 0
then we know from the discussion in Section 3.7.2 that the model is calibrated
to the market price of this caplet, because the modelвАЩs caplet price is given
by the Black formula with implied volatility equal to the square root of the
expression on the left. By imposing this constraint on all of the ѕГj , we ensure
that the model is calibrated to all caplet prices. (As a practical matter, it
may be necessary to infer the prices of individual caplets from the prices of
caps, which are portfolios of caplets. For simplicity, we assume caplet prices
are available.)
    Because LIBOR market models do not specify interest rates over accrual
periods shorter than the intervals [Ti , Ti+1 ], it is natural and customary to
restrict attention to functions ѕГn (t) that are constant between tenor dates.
We take each ѕГn to be right-continuous and thus denote by ѕГn (Ti ) its value
over the interval [Ti , Ti+1 ). Suppose, for a moment, that the model is driven
by a scalar Brownian motion, so d = 1 and each ѕГn is scalar valued. In this
case, it is convenient to think of the volatility structure as specifed through a
lower-triangular matrix of the following form:

    The potential value of a multifactor model lies in capturing correlations
between forward rates of diпђАerent maturities. For example, from the Euler

These correlations are often chosen to match market prices of swaptions
(which, unlike caps, are sensitive to rate correlations) or to match histori-
cal correlations.
    In the stationary case, we can visualize the volatility factors by graphing
them as functions of time to maturity. This can be useful in interpreting the
correlations they induce. Figure 3.20 illustrates three hypothetical factors in
a model with M = 15. Because the volatility is assumed stationary, we may
write ѕГn (Ti ) = ѕГ(n вИТ i) for some vectors ѕГ(1), . . . , ѕГ(M ). In a three-factor
model, each ѕГ(i) has three components. The three curves in Figure 3.20 are
graphs of the three components as functions of time to maturity. If we пђБx a
time to maturity on the horizontal axis, the total volatility at that point is
given by the sums of squares of the three components; the inner products of
these three-dimensional vectors at diпђАerent times determine the correlations
between the forward rates.
    Notice that the пђБrst factor in Figure 3.20 has the same sign for all matu-
rities; regardless of the sign of the increment of the driving Brownian motion,
this factor moves all forward rates in the same direction and functions approx-
imately as a parallel shift. The second factor has values of opposite signs at
short and long maturities and will thus have the eпђАect of tilting the forward
curve (up if the increment in the second component of the driving Brownian
motion is positive and down if it is negative). The third factor bends the for-
ward curve by moving intermediate maturities in the opposite direction of long
and short maturities, the direction depending on the sign of the increment of
the third component of the driving Brownian motion.

   The hypothetical factors in Figure 3.20 are the пђБrst three principal com-
ponents of the matrix

                 0.122 exp((вИТ0.8 |i вИТ j|)),           i, j = 1, . . . , 15.

More precisely, they are the пђБrst three eigenvectors of this matrix as ranked by
their eigenvalues, scaled to have length equal to their eigenvalues. It is common
in practice to use the principal components of either the covariance matrix
or the correlation matrix of changes in forward rates in choosing a factor
structure. Principal components analysis typically produces the qualitative
features of the hypothetical example in Figure 3.20; see, e.g., the examples in
James and Webber [194] or Rebonato [304].
    An important feature of LIBOR market models is that a good deal of
calibration can be accomplished through closed form expressions or eпђАective
approximations for the prices of caps and swaptions. This makes calibration
fast. In the absence of formulas or approximations, calibration is an iterative
procedure requiring repeated simulation at various parameter values until the
model price matches the market. Because each simulation can be quite time
consuming, calibration through simulation can be onerous.
4
Variance Reduction Techniques




This chapter develops methods for increasing the eпђГciency of Monte Carlo
simulation by reducing the variance of simulation estimates. These meth-
ods draw on two broad strategies for reducing variance: taking advantage of
tractable features of a model to adjust or correct simulation outputs, and
reducing the variability in simulation inputs. We discuss control variates,
antithetic variates, stratiпђБed sampling, Latin hypercube sampling, moment
matching methods, and importance sampling, and we illustrate these meth-
ods through examples. Two themes run through this chapter:
вЧ¶ The greatest gains in eпђГciency from variance reduction techniques result
  from exploiting speciпђБc features of a problem, rather than from generic
  applications of generic methods.
вЧ¶ Reducing simulation error is often at odds with convenient estimation of the
  simulation error itself; in order to supplement a reduced-variance estimator
  with a valid conпђБdence interval, we sometimes need to sacriпђБce some of the
  potential variance reduction.
The second point applies, in particular, to methods that introduce dependence
across replications in the course of reducing variance.


4.1 Control Variates
4.1.1 Method and Examples

The method of control variates is among the most eпђАective and broadly ap-
plicable techniques for improving the eпђГciency of Monte Carlo simulation.
It exploits information about the errors in estimates of known quantities to
reduce the error in an estimate of an unknown quantity.
    To describe the method, we let Y1 , . . . , Yn be outputs from n replications
of a simulation. For example, Yi could be the discounted payoпђА of a derivative
security on the ith simulated path. Suppose that the Yi are independent and
186     4 Variance Reduction Techniques

identically distributed and that our objective is to estimate E[Yi ]. The usual
estimator is the sample mean YћД = (Y1 +¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј+Yn )/n. This estimator is unbiased
and converges with probability 1 as n вЖТ вИЮ.
    Suppose, now, that on each replication we calculate another output Xi
along with Yi . Suppose that the pairs (Xi , Yi ), i = 1, . . . , n, are i.i.d. and that
the expectation E[X] of the Xi is known. (We use (X, Y ) to denote a generic
pair of random variables with the same distribution as each (Xi , Yi ).) Then
for any пђБxed b we can calculate

                             Yi (b) = Yi вИТ b(Xi вИТ E[X])

from the ith replication and then compute the sample mean

                                              1
                                                 n
             YћД (b) = YћД вИТ b(XћД вИТ E[X]) =           (Yi вИТ b(Xi вИТ E[X])).          (4.1)
                                              n i=1

This is a control variate estimator; the observed error XћД вИТ E[X] serves as a
control in estimating E[Y ].
   As an estimator of E[Y ], the control variate estimator (4.1) is unbiased
because                                       
                E[YћД (b)] = E YћД вИТ b(XћД вИТ E[X]) = E[YћД ] = E[Y ]
and it is consistent because, with probability 1,

                   1                 1
                      n                 n
                lim      Yi (b) = lim      (Yi вИТ b(Xi вИТ E[X]))
               nвЖТвИЮ n             nвЖТвИЮ n
                     i=1               i=1
                                   = E [Y вИТ b(X вИТ E[X])]
                                   = E[Y ].

Each Yi (b) has variance

                Var[Yi (b)] = Var [Yi вИТ b(Xi вИТ E[X])]
                            = ѕГY2 вИТ 2bѕГX ѕГY ѕБXY + b2 ѕГX
                                                      2
                                                        вЙ° ѕГ 2 (b),                (4.2)

where ѕГX2
          = Var[X], ѕГY2 = Var[Y ], and ѕБXY is the correlation between X and
Y . The control variate estimator YћД (b) has variance ѕГ 2 (b)/n and the ordinary
sample mean YћД (which corresponds to b = 0) has variance ѕГY2 /n. Hence, the
control variate estimator has smaller variance than the standard estimator if
b2 ѕГX < 2bѕГY ѕБXY .
    The optimal coeпђГcient bвИЧ minimizes the variance (4.2) and is given by
                                   ѕГY       Cov[X, Y ]
                            bвИЧ =      ѕБXY =            .                          (4.3)
                                   ѕГX        Var[X]
Substituting this value in (4.2) and simplifying, we пђБnd that the ratio of
the variance of the optimally controlled estimator to that of the uncontrolled
estimator is
                                                     4.1 Control Variates     187

                     Var[YћД вИТ bвИЧ (XћД вИТ E[X])]
                                              = 1 вИТ ѕБ2XY .                  (4.4)
                             Var[YћД ]
A few observations follow from this expression:
вЧ¶ With the optimal coeпђГcient bвИЧ , the eпђАectiveness of a control variate, as mea-
  sured by the variance reduction ratio (4.4), is determined by the strength of
  the correlation between the quantity of interest Y and the control X. The
  sign of the correlation is irrelevant because it is absorbed in bвИЧ .
вЧ¶ If the computational eпђАort per replication is roughly the same with and
  without a control variate, then (4.4) measures the computational speed-up
  resulting from the use of a control. More precisely, the number of replications
  of the Yi required to achieve the same variance as n replications of the
  control variate estimator is n/(1 вИТ ѕБ2XY ).
вЧ¶ The variance reduction factor 1/(1 вИТ ѕБ2XY ) increases very sharply as |ѕБXY |
  approaches 1 and, accordingly, it drops oпђА quickly as |ѕБXY | decreases away
  from 1. For example, whereas a correlation of 0.95 produces a ten-fold speed-
  up, a correlation of 0.90 yields only a пђБve-fold speed-up; at |ѕБXY | = 0.70
  the speed-up drops to about a factor of two. This suggests that a rather
  high degree of correlation is needed for a control variate to yield substantial
  beneпђБts.
    These remarks and equation (4.4) apply if the optimal coeпђГcient bвИЧ is
known. In practice, if E[Y ] is unknown it is unlikely that ѕГY or ѕБXY would
be known. However, we may still get most of the beneпђБt of a control variate
using an estimate of bвИЧ . For example, replacing the population parameters in
(4.3) with their sample counterparts yields the estimate
                               $n
                                   (Xi вИТ XћД)(Yi вИТ YћД )
                         bћВn = i=1$n                   .                (4.5)
                                    i=1 (Xi вИТ XћД)
                                                 2

Dividing numerator and denominator by n and applying the strong law of
large numbers shows that bћВn вЖТ bвИЧ with probability 1. This suggests using
the estimator YћД (bћВn ), the sample mean of Yi (bћВn ) = Yi вИТ bћВn (Xi вИТ E[X]), i =
1, . . . , n. Replacing bвИЧ with bћВn introduces some bias; we return to this point
in Section 4.1.3.
     The expression in (4.5) is the slope of the least-squares regression line
through the points (Xi , Yi ), i = 1, . . . , n. The link between control variates
and regression is useful in the statistical analysis of control variate estimators
and also permits a graphical interpretation of the method. Figure 4.1 shows
a hypothetical scatter plot of simulation outputs (Xi , Yi ) and the estimated
regression line for these points, which passes through the point (XћД, YћД ). In the
пђБgure, XћД < E[X], indicating that the n replications have underestimated E[X].
If the Xi and Yi are positively correlated, this suggests that the simulation
estimate YћД likely underestimates E[Y ]. This further suggests that we should
adjust the estimator upward. The regression line determines the magnitude of
the adjustment; in particular, YћД (bћВn ) is the value пђБtted by the regression line
at the point E[X].

Fig. 4.1. Regression interpretation of control variate method. The regression line
through the points (Xi , Yi ) has slope bћВn and passes through (XћД, YћД ). The control
variate estimator YћД (bћВn ) is the value пђБtted by the line at E[X]. In the пђБgure, the
sample mean XћД underestimates E[X] and YћД is adjusted upward accordingly.


Examples

To make the method of control variates more tangible, we now illustrate it
with several examples.
Example 4.1.1 Underlying assets. In derivative pricing simulations, under-
lying assets provide a virtually universal source of control variates. We know
from Section 1.2.1 that the absence of arbitrage is essentially equivalent to
the requirement that appropriately discounted asset prices be martingales.
Any martingale with a known initial value provides a potential control vari-
ate precisely because its expectation at any future time is its initial value. To
be concrete, suppose we are working in the risk-neutral measure and suppose
the interest rate is a constant r. If S(t) is an asset price, then exp(вИТrt)S(t) is
a martingale and E[exp(вИТrT )S(T )] = S(0). Suppose we are pricing an option
on S with discounted payoпђА Y , some function of {S(t), 0 вЙ§ t вЙ§ T }. From
independent replications Si , i = 1, . . . , n, each a path of S over [0, T ], we can
form the control variate estimator

                         1
                            n
                               (Yi вИТ b[Si (T ) вИТ erT S(0)]),
                         n i=1

or the corresponding estimator with b replaced by bћВn . If Y = eвИТrT (S(T )вИТK)+ ,
so that we are pricing a standard call option, the correlation between Y and
S(T ) and thus the eпђАectiveness of the control variate depends on the strike
K. At K = 0 we would have perfect correlation; for an option that is deep
out-of-the-money (i.e., with large K), the correlation could be quite low. This
                                                           4.1 Control Variates   189

is illustrated in Table 4.1 for the case of S вИЉ GBM(r, ѕГ 2 ) with parameters
r = 5%, ѕГ = 30%, S(0) = 50, and T = 0.25. This example shows that the
eпђАectiveness of a control variate can vary widely with the parameters of a
problem. 2


           K     40      45      50         55       60       65      70
            ѕБћВ   0.995   0.968   0.895      0.768    0.604    0.433   0.286
           ѕБћВ2   0.99    0.94    0.80       0.59     0.36     0.19    0.08
Table 4.1. Estimated correlation ѕБћВ between S(T ) and (S(T ) вИТ K)+ for various
values of K, with S(0) = 50, ѕГ = 30%, r = 5%, and T = 0.25. The third row
measures the fraction of variance in the call option payoпђА eliminated by using the
underlying asset as a control variate.




Example 4.1.2 Tractable options. Simulation is sometimes used to price
complex options in a model in which simpler options can be priced in
closed form. For example, even under Black-Scholes assumptions, some path-
dependent options require simulation for pricing even though formulas are
available for simpler options. A tractable option can sometimes provide a
more eпђАective control than the underlying asset.
    A particularly eпђАective example of this idea was suggested by Kemna and
Vorst [209] for the pricing of Asian options. Accurate pricing of an option on
the arithmetic average
                                      1
                                         n
                               SћДA =        S(ti )
                                      n i=1
requires simulation, even if S is geometric Brownian motion. In contrast, calls
and puts on the geometric average
                                                    1/n
                                      ,
                                      n
                             SћДG =          S(ti )
                                      i=1

can be priced in closed form, as explained in Section 3.2.2. Thus, options on
SћДG can be used as control variates in pricing options on SћДA .
    Figure 4.2 shows scatter plots of simulated values of (SћДA вИТ K)+ against
the terminal value of the underlying asset S(T ), a standard call payoпђА
(S(T ) вИТ K)+ , and the geometric call payoпђА (SћДG вИТ K)+ . The пђБgures are based
on K = 50 and thirteen equally spaced averaging dates; all other parameters
are as in Example 4.1.1. The leftmost panel shows that the weak correlation
between SћДA and S(T ) is further weakened by applying the call option payoпђА
to SћДA , which projects negative values of SћДA вИТ K to zero; the resulting corre-
lation is approximately 0.79. The middle panel shows the eпђАect of applying
the call option payoпђА to S(T ) as well; in this case the correlation increases
190    4 Variance Reduction Techniques

to approximately 0.85. The rightmost panel illustrates the extremely strong
relation between the payoпђАs on the arithmetic and geometric average call op-
tions. The correlation in this case is greater then 0.99. A similar comparison
is made in Broadie and Glasserman [67]. 2

Example 4.1.3 Bond prices. In a model with stochastic interest rates, bond
prices often provide a convenient source of control variates. As emphasized in
Sections 3.3вАУ3.4 and Sections 3.6вАУ3.7, an important consideration in imple-
menting an interest rate simulation is ensuring that the simulation correctly
prices bonds. While this is primarily important for consistent pricing, as a by-
product it makes bonds available as control variates. Bonds may be viewed as
the underlying assets of an interest rate model, so in a sense this example is
a special case of Example 4.1.1.
    In a model of the short rate r(t), a bond maturing at time T has initial
price
                                                             4.1 Control Variates   191
                                                  T             
                    B(0, T ) = E exp вИТ                  r(u) du        ,
                                                    0

the expectation taken with respect to the risk-neutral measure. Since we may
assume that B(0, T ) is known, the quantity inside the expectation provides
a potential control variate. But even if r is simulated without discretization
error at dates t1 , . . . , tn = T , the diпђАerence
                                                   
                                       1
                                          n
                              exp вИТ          r(ti ) вИТ B(0, T )
                                       n i=1

will not ordinarily have mean 0 because of the error in approximating the
integral. Using this diпђАerence in a control variate estimator could therefore
introduce some bias, though the bias can be made as small as necessary by
taking a suпђГciently пђБne partition of [0, T ]. In our discussion of the Vasicek
model in Section 3.3, we detailed the exact joint simulation of r(ti ) and its
time integral                               ti
                             Y (ti ) =            r(u) du.
                                         0
This provides a bias-free control variate because

                          E [exp(вИТY (T ))] = B(0, T ).

    Similar considerations apply to the forward rate models of Sections 3.6
and 3.7. In our discussion of the Heath-Jarrow-Morton framework, we devoted
considerable attention to deriving the appropriate discrete drift condition.
Using this drift in a simulation produces unbiased bond price estimates and
thus makes bonds available as control variates. In our discussion of LIBOR
market models, we noted that discretizing the system of SDEs for the LIBOR
rates Ln would not produce unbiased bond estimates; in contrast, the methods
in Section 3.7 based on discretizing deпђВated bonds or their diпђАerences do
produce unbiased estimates and thus allow the use of bonds as control variates.
    Comparison of this discussion with the one in Section 3.7.3 should make
clear that the question of whether or not asset prices can be used as control
variates is closely related to the question of whether a simulated model is
arbitrage-free. 2
Example 4.1.4 Tractable dynamics. The examples discussed thus far are all
based on using one set of prices in a model as control variates for some other
price in the same model. Another strategy for developing eпђАective control
variates uses prices in a simpler model. We give two illustrations of this idea.
    Consider, пђБrst, the pricing of an option on an asset whose dynamics are
modeled by

Example 4.1.5 Hedges as controls. There is a close link between the se-
lection of control variates and the selection of hedging instruments. If Y is
a discounted payoпђА and we are estimating E[Y ], then any instrument that
serves as an eпђАective hedge for Y also serves as an eпђАective control variate if
it can be easily priced. Indeed, the calculation of the optimal coeпђГcient bвИЧ is
identical to the calculation of the optimal hedge ratio in minimum-variance
hedging (see, e.g., Section 2.9 of Hull [189]).
    Whereas a static hedge (a пђБxed position in another asset) may provide
signiпђБcant variance reduction, a dynamic hedge can, in principle, remove all
194    4 Variance Reduction Techniques

variance вАФ at least under the assumptions of a complete market and continu-
ous trading as discussed in Section 1.2.1. Using the notation of Section 1.2.1,
let V (t) denote the value at time t of a price process that can be replicated
through a self-пђБnancing trading strategy applied to a set of underlying assets
Sj , j = 1, . . . , d. As in Section 1.2.1, under appropriate conditions we have

                                                  вИВV (t)
                     V (T ) = V (0) +                    dSj (t).

                                                   вИВSj

In other words, V is replicated through a delta-hedging strategy that holds
вИВV /вИВSj shares of asset Sj at each instant. This suggests that V (T ) should be
highly correlated with

control variate by using a rough approximation to the вИВV /вИВSj ; for example,
one might calculate these deltas as though the underlying asset prices followed
geometric Brownian motions, even if their actual dynamics are more complex.
See Clewlow and Carverhill [87] for examples of this.
    Using an expression like (4.9) as a control variate is somewhat similar to
using all the increments Sj (ti ) вИТ Sj (tiвИТ1 ) as controls or, more conveniently,
the increments of the discounted asset prices since these have mean zero. The
main diпђАerence is that the coeпђГcients вИВV /вИВSj in (4.9) will not in general be
constants but will depend on the Sj themselves. We may therefore interpret
(4.9) as using the Sj (ti ) вИТ Sj (tiвИТ1 ) as nonlinear control variates. We discuss
nonlinear controls more generally in Section 4.1.4. 2
Example 4.1.6 Primitive controls. In the examples above, we have stressed
the use of special features of derivative pricing models in identifying potential
control variates. Indeed, signiпђБcant variance reduction is usually achieved only
by taking advantage of special properties of a model. It is nevertheless worth
mentioning that many generic (and thus typically not very eпђАective) control
variates are almost always available in a simulation. For example, most of
the models discussed in Chapter 3 are simulated from a sequence Z1 , Z2 , . . .
of independent standard normal random variables. We know that E[Zi ] = 0
and Var[Zi ] = 1, so the sample mean and sample variance of the Zi are
available control variates. At a still more primitive level, most simulations
start from a sequence U1 , U2 , . . . of independent Unif[0,1] random variables.
Sample moments of the Ui can also be used as controls. 2
    Later in this chapter we discuss other techniques for reducing variance. In
a sense, all of these can be viewed as strategies for selecting control variates.
                                                        4.1 Control Variates     195

For suppose we want to estimate E[Y ] and in addition to the usual sample
mean YћД we have available an alternative unbiased estimator YћГ . The diпђАerence
(YћД вИТ YћГ ) has (known) expectation zero and can thus be used to form a control
variate estimate of the form
                                YћД вИТ b(YћД вИТ YћГ ).
The special cases b = 0 and b = 1 correspond to using just one of the two
estimators; by optimizing b, we obtain a combined estimator that has lower
variance than either of the two.

Output Analysis

In analyzing variance reduction techniques, along with the eпђАectiveness of a
technique it is important to consider how the technique aпђАects the statisti-
cal interpretation of simulation outputs. So long as we deal with unbiased,
independent replications, computing conпђБdence intervals for expectations is
a simple matter, as noted in Section 1.1 and explained in Appendix A. But
we will see that some variance reduction techniques complicate interval esti-
mation by introducing dependence across replications. This issue arises with
control variates if we use the estimated coeпђГcient bћВn in (4.5). It turns out
that in the case of control variates the dependence can be ignored in large
samples; a more careful consideration of small-sample issues will be given in
Section 4.1.3.
    For any пђБxed b, the control variate estimator YћД (b) in (4.1) is the sample
mean of independent replications Yi (b), i = 1, . . . , n. Accordingly, an asymp-
totically valid 1 вИТ ќі conпђБdence interval for E[Y ] is provided by
                                            ѕГ(b)
                               YћД (b) ¬± zќі/2 вИЪ ,                               (4.10)
                                              n
where zќі/2 is the 1 вИТ ќі/2 quantile from the normal distribution (ќ¶(zќі/2 ) =
1 вИТ ќі/2) and ѕГ(b) is the standard deviation per replication, as in (4.2).
   In practice, ѕГ(b) is typically unknown but can be estimated using the
sample standard deviation
                             
                             
                              1     n
                      s(b) =            (Yi (b) вИТ YћД (b))2 .
                               n вИТ 1 i=1

The conпђБdence interval (4.10) remains asymptotically valid if we replace ѕГ(b)
with s(b), as a consequence of the limit in distribution
                            YћД (b) вИТ E[Y ]
                                    вИЪ      вЗТ N (0, 1)
                               ѕГ(b)/ n
and the fact that s(b)/ѕГ(b) вЖТ 1; see Appendix A.2. If we use the estimated
coeпђГcient bћВn in (4.5), then the estimator
196     4 Variance Reduction Techniques

                                     1
                                        n
                       YћД (bћВn ) =         (Yi вИТ bћВn (Xi вИТ E[X]))
                                     n i=1

is not quite of the form YћД (b) because we have replaced the constant b with
the random quantity bћВn . Nevertheless, because bћВn вЖТ bвИЧ , we have
     вИЪ                                        вИЪ
       n(YћД (bћВn ) вИТ YћД (bвИЧ )) = (bћВn вИТ bвИЧ ) ¬Ј n(XћД вИТ E[X]) вЗТ 0 ¬Ј N (0, ѕГX

so YћД (bћВn ) satisпђБes the same central limit theorem as YћД (bвИЧ ). This means that
YћД (bћВn ) is asymptotically as precise as YћД (bвИЧ ). Moreover, the central limit theo-
rem applies in the form

                              YћД (bћВn ) вИТ E[Y ]
                                         вИЪ      вЗТ N (0, 1),
                                 s(bћВn )/ n

with s(bћВn ) the sample standard deviation of the Yi (bћВn ), i = 1, . . . , n, because
s(bћВn )/ѕГ(bвИЧ ) вЖТ 1. In particular, the conпђБdence interval (4.10) remains asymp-
totically valid if we replace YћД (b) and ѕГ(b) with YћД (bћВn ) and s(bћВn ), and conпђБdence
intervals estimated using bћВn are asymptotically no wider than conпђБdence in-
tervals estimated using the optimal coeпђГcient bвИЧ .
    We may summarize this discussion as follows. It is a simple matter to
estimate asymptotically valid conпђБdence intervals for control variate estima-
tors. Moreover, for large n, we get all the beneпђБt of the optimal coeпђГcient
bвИЧ by using the estimate bћВn . However, for пђБnite n, there may still be costs to
using an estimated rather than a пђБxed coeпђГcient; we return to this point in
Section 4.1.3.

4.1.2 Multiple Controls

We now generalize the method of control variates to the case of multiple
controls. Examples 4.1.1вАУ4.1.6 provide ample motivation for considering this
extension.
вЧ¶ If eвИТrt S(t) is a martingale, then eвИТrt1 S(t1 ), . . . , eвИТrtd S(td ) all have expec-
  tation S(0) and thus provide d controls on each path.
вЧ¶ If the simulation involves d underlying assets, the terminal values of all
  assets may provide control variates.
вЧ¶ Rather than use a single option as a control variate, we may want to use
  options with multiple strikes and maturities.
вЧ¶ In an interest rate simulation, we may choose to use d bonds of diпђАerent
  maturities as controls.
    Suppose, then, that each replication i of a simulation produces outputs
Yi and Xi = (Xi , . . . , Xi ) and suppose the vector of expectations E[X]
                (1)        (d)

Variance Decomposition

In looking for eпђАective control variates, it is useful to understand what part of
the variance of an ordinary estimator is removed through the use of controls.
We now address this point.
    Let (X, Y ) be any random vector with Y scalar and X d-dimensional. Let
(X, Y ) have the partitioned covariance matrix in (4.11). For any b, we can
write
                         Y = E[Y ] + b (X вИТ E[X]) + ,
simply by deпђБning  so that equality holds. If b = bвИЧ = ќ£вИТ1
                                                         X ќ£XY , then in fact
 is uncorrelated with X; i.e., Y вИТ bвИЧ (X вИТ E[X]) is uncorrelated with X so

         Var[Y ] = Var[bвИЧ X] + Var[] = Var[bвИЧ X] + Var[Y вИТ bвИЧ X].

In this decomposition, the part of Var[Y ] eliminated by using X as a control
is Var[bвИЧ X] and the remaining variance is Var[].
    The optimal vector bвИЧ makes bвИЧ (X вИТ E[X]) the projection of Y вИТ E[Y ]
onto X вИТ E[X]; the residual  may be interpreted as the part of Y вИТ E[Y ]
orthogonal to X вИТ E[X], orthogonal here meaning uncorrelated. The smaller
this orthogonal component (as measured by its variance), the greater the
variance reduction achieved by using X as a control for Y . If, in particular,
Y is a linear combination of the components of X, then using X as a control
eliminates all variance. Of course, in this case E[Y ] is a linear combination of
the (known) components of E[X], so simulation would be unnecessary.
                                                       4.1 Control Variates      199

    Consider, again, the examples with which we opened this section. If we
use multiple path values S(ti ) of an underlying asset as control variates, we
eliminate all variance in estimating the expected value of any instrument
whose payoпђА is a linear combination of the S(ti ). (In particular, each E[S(ti )]
is trivially estimated without error if we use S(ti ) as a control.) The variance
that remains in estimating an expected payoпђА while using the S(ti ) as controls
is attributable to the part of the payoпђА that is uncorrelated with the S(ti ).
Similarly, if we use bond prices as control variates in pricing an interest rate
derivative, the remaining variance is due to the part of the derivativeвАЩs payoпђА
that is uncorrelated with any linear combination of bond prices.

Control Variates and Weighted Monte Carlo

In introducing the idea of a control variate in Section 4.1.1, we explained that
the observed error in estimating a known quantity could be used to adjust
an estimator of an unknown quantity. But the technique has an alternative
interpretation as a method for assigning weights to replications. This alterna-
tive perspective is sometimes useful, particularly in relating control variates
to other methods.
    For simplicity, we start with the case of a single control; thus, Yi and Xi
are scalars and the pairs (Xi , Yi ) are i.i.d. The control variate estimator with
estimated optimal coeпђГcient bћВn is YћД (bћВn ) = YћД вИТ bћВn (XћД вИТ E[X]). As in (4.5), the
estimated coeпђГcient is given by
                               $n
                                      (Xi вИТ XћД)(Yi вИТ YћД )
                         bћВn = i=1  $n                      .
                                       i=1 (Xi вИТ XћД)
                                                     2


By substituting this expression into YћД (bћВn ) and simplifying, we arrive at
                      
                      n
                            1  (XћД вИТ Xi )(XћД вИТ E[X])          
                                                              n
        YћД (bћВn ) =           + $n                     Yi вЙ°         wi Yi .   (4.19)
                                    i=1 (Xi вИТ XћД)
                            n                    2
                      i=1                                     i=1

In other words, the control variate estimator is a weighted average of the
replications Y1 , . . . , Yn . The weights wi are completely determined by the ob-
servations X1 , . . . , Xn of the control.
    A similar representation applies with multiple controls. Using the esti-
mated vector of coeпђГcients in (4.16), the sample covariance matrix SX in
(4.17) and simplifying, we get

                      

                            n nвИТ1

which is again a weighted average of the Yi . Here, as before, Xi denotes the
vector of controls (Xi , . . . , Xi ) from the ith replication, and XћД and E[X]
                     (1)          (d)

denote the sample mean and expectation of the Xi , respectively.
200    4 Variance Reduction Techniques

    The representation in (4.20) is a special case of a general feature of regres-
sion вАФ namely, that a пђБtted value of Y is a weighted average of the observed
values of the Yi with weights determined by the Xi . One consequence of this
representation is that if we want to estimate multiple quantities (e.g., prices
of various derivative securities) from the same set of simulated paths using
the same control variates, the weights can be calculated just once and then
applied to all the outputs. Hesterberg and Nelson [178] also show that (4.20)
is useful in applying control variates to quantile estimation. They indicate
that although it is possible for some of the weights in (4.19) and (4.20) to
be negative, the probability of negative weights is small in a sense they make
precise.

4.1.3 Small-Sample Issues

In our discussion (following (4.10)) of output analysis with the method of
control variates, we noted that because bћВn converges to bвИЧ , we obtain an
asymptotically valid conпђБdence interval if we ignore the randomness of bћВn
and the dependence it introduces among Yi (bћВn ), i = 1, . . . , n. Moreover, we
noted that as n вЖТ вИЮ, the variance reduction achieved using bћВn approaches
the variance reduction that would be achieved if the optimal bвИЧ were known.
      In this section, we supplement these large-sample properties with a dis-
cussion of statistical issues that arise in analyzing control variate estimators
based on a пђБnite number of samples. We note that stronger distributional
assumptions on the simulation output lead to conпђБdence intervals valid for
all n. Moreover, it becomes possible to quantify the loss in eпђГciency due to
estimating bвИЧ . This oпђАers some guidance in deciding how many control vari-
ates to use in a simulation. This discussion is based on results of Lavenberg,
Moeller, and Welch [221] and Nelson [277].
      For any пђБxed b, the control variate estimator YћД (b) is unbiased. But using
bћВn , we have

            Bias(YћД (bћВn )) = E[YћД (bћВn )] вИТ E[Y ] = вИТE[bћВ
                                                          n (XћД вИТ E[X])],

which need not be zero because bћВn and XћД are not independent. A simple way to
eliminate bias is to use n1 replications to compute an estimate bћВn1 and to then
apply this coeпђГcient with the remaining n вИТ n1 replications of (Xi , Yi ). This
makes the coeпђГcient estimate independent of XћД and thus makes E[bћВ       n1 XћД] =
                                                            вИЧ
E[bћВn1 ]E[XћД]. In practice, the bias produced by estimating b is usually small so
the cost of estimating coeпђГcients through separate replications is unattractive.
                                                                              вИЪ
Indeed, the bias is typically O(1/n), whereas the standard error is O(1/ n).
    Lavenberg, Moeller, and Welch [221] and Nelson [277] note that even if
bћВn is estimated from the same replications used to compute YћД and XћД, the
control variate estimator is unbiased if the regression of Y on X is linear.
More precisely, if
                                                             4.1 Control Variates       201

 E[Y |X] = c0 +c1 X (1) +¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј+cd X (d)     for some constants c0 , c1 , . . . , cd , (4.21)

then E[YћД (bћВn )] = E[Y ].
   Under the additional assumption that Var[Y |X] does not depend on X,

                   2

                         n вИТ d вИТ 1 i=1
                        1   1               вИТ1
                   √Ч      +   (XћД вИТ E[X]) SX  (XћД вИТ E[X]) .

The пђБrst factor in this expression is the sample variance of the regression
residuals, the denominator n вИТ d вИТ 1 reпђВecting the loss of d + 1 degrees of
freedom in estimating the regression coeпђГcients in (4.21). The second factor
inпђВates the variance estimate when XћД is far from E[X].
    As in Lavenberg et al. [221] and Nelson [277], we now add a пђБnal as-
sumption that (X, Y ) has a multivariate normal distribution, from which two
important consequences follow. The пђБrst is that this provides an exact conпђБ-
dence interval for E[Y ] for all n: the interval

covers E[Y ] with probability 1 вИТ ќі, where tnвИТdвИТ1,ќі/2 denotes the 1 вИТ ќі/2
quantile of the t distribution with nвИТdвИТ1 degrees of freedom. This conпђБdence
interval may have better coverage than the crude interval (4.18), even if the
assumptions on which it is based do not hold exactly.
    A second important conclusion that holds under the added assumption of
normality is an exact expression for the variance of YћД (bћВn ). With d controls
and n > d + 2 replications,
                                           nвИТ2           ѕГ2
                       Var[YћД (bћВn )] =         (1 вИТ R2 ) Y .                       (4.23)
                                          nвИТdвИТ2           n
Here, as before, ѕГY2 = Var[Y ] is the variance per replication without controls
and R2 is the squared multiple correlation coeпђГcient deпђБned in (4.14). As
noted in (4.15), (1 вИТ R2 )ѕГY2 is the variance per replication of the control
variate estimator with known optimal coeпђГcient. We may thus write (4.23)
as
                                         nвИТ2
                       Var[YћД (bћВn )] =       Var[YћД (bвИЧ )].              (4.24)
                                        nвИТdвИТ2
In light of this relation, Lavenberg et al. [221] call (n вИТ 2)/(n вИТ d вИТ 2) the
loss factor measuring the loss in eпђГciency due to using the estimate bћВn rather
than the exact value bвИЧ .
    Both (4.22) and (4.24) penalize the use of too many controls вАФ more
precisely, they penalize the use of control variates that do not provide a suf-
пђБciently large reduction in variance. In (4.22), a larger d results in a loss of
202     4 Variance Reduction Techniques

degrees of freedom, a larger multiplier tnвИТdвИТ1,ќі/2 , and thus a wider conпђБdence
interval unless the increase in d is oпђАset by a suпђГcient decrease in sћВn . In (4.24),
a larger d results in a larger loss factor and thus a greater eпђГciency cost from
using estimated coeпђГcients. In both cases, the cost from using more controls
is eventually overwhelmed by increasing the sample size n; what constitutes
a reasonable number of control variates thus depends in part on the intended
number of replications.
     The validity of the conпђБdence interval (4.22) and the loss factor in (4.24)
depends on the distributional assumptions on (X, Y ) introduced above leading
up to (4.22); in particular, these results depend on the assumed normality of
(X, Y ). (Loh [239] provides extensions to more general distributions but these
seem diпђГcult to use in practice.) In pricing applications, Y would often be the
discounted payoпђА of an option contract and thus highly skewed and distinctly
non-normal. In this case, application of (4.22) and (4.24) lacks theoretical
support.
     Nelson [277] analyzes the use of various remedies for control variate estima-
tors when the distributional assumptions facilitating their statistical analysis
fail to hold. Among the methods he examines is batching. This method groups
the replications (Xi , Yi ), i = 1, . . . , n, into k disjoint batches of n/k replica-
tions each. It then calculates sample means of the (Xi , Yi ) within each batch
and applies the usual control variate procedure to the k sample means of the
k batches. The appeal of this method lies in the fact that the batch means
should be more nearly normally distributed than the original (Xi , Yi ). The
cost of batching lies in the loss of degrees of freedom: it reduces the eпђАective
sample size from n to k. Based on a combination of theoretical and exper-
imental results, Nelson [277] recommends forming 30 to 60 batches if up to
пђБve controls are used. With a substantially larger number of controls, the cost
of replacing the number of replications n with k = 30вАУ60 in (4.22) and (4.24)
would be more signiпђБcant; this would argue in favor of using a larger number
of smaller batches.
     Another strategy for potentially improving the performance of control vari-
ate estimators replaces the estimated covariance matrix SX with its true value
ќ£X in estimating bвИЧ . This is feasible if ќ£X is known, which would be the case
in at least some of the examples introduced in Section 4.1.1. Nelson [277] and
Bauer, Venkatraman, and Wilson [40] analyze this alternative; perhaps sur-
prisingly, they пђБnd that it generally produces estimators inferior to the usual
method based on bћВn .

4.1.4 Nonlinear Controls

Our discussion of control variates has thus far focused exclusively on linear
controls, meaning estimators of the form

                                YћД вИТ b (XћД вИТ E[X]),                           (4.25)
                                                         4.1 Control Variates   203

with the vector b either known or estimated. There are, however, other ways
one might use the discrepancy between XћД and E[X] to try to improve the
estimator YћД in estimating E[Y ]. For example, in the case of scalar X, the
estimator
                                      E[X]
                                   YћД
                                       XћД
adjusts YћД upward if 0 < XћД < E[X], downward if 0 < E[X] < XћД, and thus may
be attractive if Xi and Yi are positively correlated. Similarly, the estimator
YћД XћД/E[X] may have merit if the Xi and Yi are negatively correlated. Other
estimators of this type include
                                    
                    YћД exp XћД вИТ E[X]      and YћД (XћД/E[X]) .

In each case, the convergence of XћД to E[X] ensures that the adjustment to
YћД vanishes as the sample size increases, just as in (4.25). But for any пђБnite
number of replications, the variance of the adjusted estimator could be larger
or smaller than that of YћД .
    These are examples of nonlinear control variate estimators. They are all
special cases of estimators of the form h(XћД, YћД ) for functions h satisfying

                             h(E[X], y) = y for all y.

The diпђАerence between the controlled estimator h(XћД, YћД ) and YћД thus depends
on the deviation of XћД from E[X].
    Although the introduction of nonlinear controls would appear to substan-
tially enlarge the class of candidate estimators, it turns out that in large
samples, a nonlinear control variate estimator based on a smooth h is equiv-
alent to an ordinary linear control variate estimator. This was demonstrated
in Glynn and Whitt [159], who note a related observation in Cheng and Feast
[84]. We present the analysis leading to this conclusion and then discuss its
implications.

Delta Method

The main tool for the large-sample analysis of nonlinear control variate esti-
mators is the delta method. This is a result providing a central limit theorem
for functions of sample means. To state it generally, we let ќЊi , i = 1, 2, . . . be
i.i.d. random vectors in k with mean vector ¬µ and covariance matrix ќ£. The
sample mean ќЊћД of ќЊ1 , . . . , ќЊn satisпђБes the central limit theorem
                                 вИЪ
                                   n[ќЊ¬ѓ вИТ ¬µ] вЗТ N (0, ќ£).

Now let h : k вЖТ  be continuously diпђАerentiable in a neighborhood of ¬µ and
suppose the partial derivatives of h at ¬µ are not all zero. For suпђГciently large
n, a Taylor approximation gives
                            ¬ѓ = h(¬µ) + вИЗh(ќґn )[ќЊ¬ѓ вИТ ¬µ],

nonlinear control variate estimator is asymptotically normal with
                       вИЪ

(recall that h(E[X], E[Y ]) = E[Y ]) and
                         2
                    вИВh                 вИВh
            ѕГh2 =            ѕГY2 + 2        вИЗx hќ£XY + вИЗx hќ£X вИЗx h ,

with вИЗx h denoting the gradient of h with respect to the elements of X and
