                    ѕГh2 = ѕГY2 + 2вИЗx hќ£XY + вИЗx hќ£X вИЗx h .

But this is precisely the variance of

                                Yi вИТ b (Xi вИТ E[X])

with b = вИТвИЗx h(E[X], E[Y ]); see (4.12). Thus, the distribution of the nonlinear
control variate estimator using XћД is asymptotically the same as the distrib-
ution of an ordinary linear control variate estimator using XћД and a speciпђБc
vector of coeпђГcients b. In particular, the limiting variance parameter ѕГh2 can
be no smaller than the optimal variance that would be derived from using the
optimal vector bвИЧ .
    A negative reading of this result leads to the conclusion that nonlinear con-
trols add nothing beyond what can be achieved using linear controls. A some-
what more positive and more accurate interpretation would be that whatever
advantages a nonlinear control variate estimator may have must be limited to
small samples. вАЬSmallвАЭ may well include all relevant sample sizes in speciпђБc
                                                       4.2 Antithetic Variates       205

applications. The delta method tells us that asymptotically only the linear
part of h matters, but if h is highly nonlinear a very large sample may be
required for this asymptotic conclusion to be relevant. For пђБxed n, each of the
examples with which we opened this section may perform rather diпђАerently
from a linear control.
    It should also be noted that in the linear control variate estimator to which
any nonlinear control variate estimator is ultimately equivalent, the coeпђГcient
b is implicitly determined by the function h. In particular, using a nonlinear
control does not entail estimating this coeпђГcient. In some cases, a nonlinear
control may be eпђАective because вИТвИЗx h is close to optimal but need not be
estimated.


4.2 Antithetic Variates
The method of antithetic variates attempts to reduce variance by introduc-
ing negative dependence between pairs of replications. The method can take
various forms; the most broadly applicable is based on the observation that
if U is uniformly distributed over [0, 1], then 1 вИТ U is too. Hence, if we gen-
erate a path using as inputs U1 , . . . , Un , we can generate a second path using
1 вИТ U1 , . . . , 1 вИТ Un without changing the law of the simulated process. The
variables Ui and 1 вИТ Ui form an antithetic pair in the sense that a large value
of one is accompanied by a small value of the other. This suggests that an
unusually large or small output computed from the пђБrst path may be balanced
by the value computed from the antithetic path, resulting in a reduction in
variance.
    These observations extend to other distributions through the inverse trans-
form method: F вИТ1 (U ) and F вИТ1 (1 вИТ U ) both have distribution F but are an-
tithetic to each other because F вИТ1 is monotone. For a distribution symmetric
about the origin, F вИТ1 (1 вИТ u) and F вИТ1 (u) have the same magnitudes but op-
posite signs. In particular, in a simulation driven by independent standard
normal random variables, antithetic variates can be implemented by pairing a
sequence Z1 , Z2 , . . . of i.i.d. N (0, 1) variables with the sequence вИТZ1 , вИТZ2 , . . .
of i.i.d. N (0, 1) variables, whether or not they are sampled through the in-
verse transform method. If the Zi are used to simulate the increments of a
Brownian path, then the вИТZi simulate the increments of the reпђВection of the
path about the origin. This again suggests that running a pair of simulations
using the original path and then its reпђВection may result in lower variance.
    To analyze this approach more precisely, suppose our objective is to esti-
mate an expectation E[Y ] and that using some implementation of antithetic
sampling produces a sequence of pairs of observations (Y1 , YћГ1 ), (Y2 , YћГ2 ), . . . ,
(Yn , YћГn ). The key features of the antithetic variates method are the following:
вЧ¶ the pairs (Y1 , YћГ1 ), (Y2 , YћГ2 ), . . . , (Yn , YћГn ) are i.i.d.;
вЧ¶ for each i, Yi and YћГi have the same distribution, though ordinarily they are
  not independent.
206        4 Variance Reduction Techniques

We use Y generically to indicate a random variable with the common distri-
bution of the Yi and YћГi .
    The antithetic variates estimator is simply the average of all 2n observa-
tions,                                                      
                      1                     1  Yi + YћГi
                            n        n           n
              YћВAV =           Yi +     YћГi =                  .        (4.27)
                     2n i=1         i=1
                                              n i=1      2

The rightmost representation in (4.27) makes it evident that YћВAV is the sample

                                                         
                  Y1 + YћГ1      Y2 + YћГ2          Yn + YћГn
                             ,             ,...,             .            (4.28)
                     2             2                 2

The central limit theorem therefore applies and gives

As usual, this limit in distribution continues to hold if we replace ѕГAV with

where 1 вИТ ќ¶(zќі/2 ) = ќі/2.
     Under what conditions is an antithetic variates estimator to be preferred
to an ordinary Monte Carlo estimator based on independent replications? To
make this comparison, we assume that the computational eпђАort required to
generate a pair (Yi , YћГi ) is approximately twice the eпђАort required to generate
Yi . In other words, we ignore any potential computational savings from, for
example, пђВipping the signs of previously generated Z1 , Z2 , . . . rather than
generating new normal variables. This is appropriate if the computational cost
of generating these inputs is a small fraction of the total cost of simulating Yi .
Under this assumption, the eпђАort required to compute YћВAV is approximately
that required to compute the sample mean of 2n independent replications, and
it is therefore meaningful to compare the variances of these two estimators.
Using antithetics reduces variance if
                                                      
                                0    1         1 
                                                  2n
                            Var YћВAV < Var           Yi ;
                                              2n i=1

i.e., if
                                                           4.2 Antithetic Variates    207
                                   0        1
                              Var Yi + YћГi < 2Var[Yi ].
The variance on the left can be written as
                  0        1
              Var Yi + YћГi = Var[Yi ] + Var[YћГi ] + 2Cov[Yi , YћГi ]
                                 = 2Var[Yi ] + 2Cov[Yi , YћГi ],

using the fact that Yi and YћГi have the same variance if they have the same
distribution. Thus, the condition for antithetic sampling to reduce variance
becomes                           0        1
                               Cov Yi , YћГi < 0.                      (4.29)
    Put succinctly, this condition requires that negative dependence in the
inputs (whether U and 1 вИТ U or Z and вИТZ) produce negative correlation
between the outputs of paired replications. A simple suпђГcient condition en-
suring this is monotonicity of the mapping from inputs to outputs deпђБned by
a simulation algorithm. To state this precisely and to give a general formu-
lation, suppose the inputs to a simulation are independent random variables
X1 , . . . , Xm . Suppose that Y is an increasing function of these inputs and YћГ
is a decreasing function of the inputs; then

                                 E[Y YћГ ] вЙ§ E[Y ]E[YћГ ].

This is a special case of more general properties of associated random vari-
ables, in the sense of Esary, Proschan, and Walkup [113]. Observe that if
Y = f (U1 , . . . , Ud ) or Y = f (Z1 , . . . , Zd ) for some increasing function f , then
YћГ = f (1 вИТ U1 , . . . , 1 вИТ Ud) and YћГ = f (вИТZ1 , . . . , вИТZd ) are decreasing functions
of (U1 , . . . , Ud ) and (Z1 , . . . , Zd ), respectively. The requirement that the sim-
ulation map inputs to outputs monotonically is rarely satisпђБed exactly, but
provides some qualitative insight into the scope of the method.
    The antithetic pairs (U, 1 вИТ U ) with U вИЉ Unif[0,1] and (Z, вИТZ) with Z вИЉ
N (0, 1) share an additional relevant property: in each case, the average of the
paired values is the population mean, because
                   U + (1 вИТ U )                      Z + (вИТZ)
                                = 1/2        and              = 0.
                        2                                2
It follows that if the output Y is a linear function of inputs (U1 , . . . , Ud ) or
(Z1 , . . . , Zd ), then antithetic sampling results in a zero-variance estimator. Of
course, in the linear case simulation would be unnecessary, but this observation
suggests that antithetic variates will be very eпђАective if the mapping from
inputs to outputs is close to linear.

Variance Decomposition

Antithetic variates eliminate the variance due to the antisymmetric part of an
integrand, in a sense we now develop. For simplicity, we restrict attention to
208    4 Variance Reduction Techniques

the case of standard normal inputs, but our observations apply equally well
to any other distribution symmetric about the origin and apply with minor
modiпђБcations to uniformly distributed inputs.
   Suppose, then, that Y = f (Z) with Z = (Z1 , . . . , Zd ) вИЉ N (0, I). DeпђБne
the symmetric and antisymmetric parts of f , respectively, by
                       f (z) + f (вИТz)                         f (z) вИТ f (вИТz)
            f0 (z) =                        and    f1 (z) =                  .
                              2                                      2
Clearly, f = f0 + f1 ; moreover, this gives an orthogonal decomposition of f
in the sense that f0 (Z) and f1 (Z) are uncorrelated:
                                           1
                       E[f0 (Z)f1 (Z)] =     E[f 2 (Z) вИТ f 2 (вИТZ)]
                                           4
                                          =0
                                          = E[f0 (Z)]E[f1 (Z)].

It follows that
                        Var[f (Z)] = Var[f0 (Z)] + Var[f1 (Z)].                  (4.30)
The пђБrst term on the right is the variance of an estimate of E[f (Z)] based on
an antithetic pair (Z, вИТZ). Thus, antithetic sampling eliminates all variance
if f is antisymmetric (f = f1 ) and it eliminates no variance if f is symmetric
(f = f0 ).
    Fox [127] advocates the use of antithetic sampling as the пђБrst step of a more
elaborate framework, in order to eliminate the variance due to the linear (or,
more generally, the antisymmetric) part of f .

Systematic Sampling

Antithetic sampling pairs a standard normal vector Z = (Z1 , . . . , Zd ) with its
reпђВection вИТZ = (вИТZ1 , . . . , вИТZd ), but it is natural to consider other vectors
formed by changing the signs of the components of Z. Generalizing still further
leads us to consider transformations T : d вЖТ d (such as multiplication
by an orthogonal matrix) with the property that T Z вИЉ N (0, I) whenever
Z вИЉ N (0, I). This property implies that the iterated transformations T 2 Z,
T 3 Z, . . . will also have standard normal distributions. Suppose that T k is
the identity for some k. The usual antithetic transformation has k = 2, but
by considering other rotations and reпђВections of d , it is easy to construct
examples with larger values of k.
    DeпђБne

                        1
                           k
             f0 (z) =         f (T i Z)     and    f1 (z) = f (z) вИТ f0 (z).
                        k i=1

We clearly have E[f0 (Z)] = E[f (Z)]. The estimator f0 (Z) generalizes the an-
tithetic variates estimator; in the survey sampling literature, methods of this
                                                      4.3 StratiпђБed Sampling    209

type are called systematic sampling because after the initial random drawing
of Z, the k вИТ 1 subsequent points are obtained through deterministic trans-
formations of Z.
   The representation f (Z) = f0 (Z) + f1 (Z) again gives an orthogonal de-
composition. To see this, пђБrst observe that
                                     п£Ѓ                               п£є
                             1 k
                                                  1 k
               E[f0 (Z)2 ] =       E п£∞f (T i Z) ¬Ј       f (T j T i Z)п£ї
                             k i=1                k j=1

                              1                           
                                 k
                          =         E f (T i Z) ¬Ј f0 (T i Z)
                              k i=1
                          = E[f (Z)f0 (Z)],

so
                 E[f0 (Z)f1 (Z)] = E[f0 (Z)(f (Z) вИТ f0 (Z))] = 0.
Thus, (4.30) continues to hold under the new deпђБnitions of f0 and f1 . Assum-
ing the f (T i Z), i = 1, . . . , k, require approximately equal computing times,
the estimator f0 (Z) beats ordinary Monte Carlo if
                                            1
                            Var[f0 (Z)] <     Var[f (Z)].
                                            k
The steps leading to (4.29) generalize to the requirement

                           

                                 Cov[f (Z), f (T j Z)] < 0.
                           j=1

This condition is usually at least as diпђГcult to satisfy as the simple version
(4.29) for ordinary antithetic sampling.
    For a more general formulation of antithetic sampling and for historical
remarks, see Hammersley and Handscomb [169]. Boyle [52] is an early ap-
plication in пђБnance. Other work on antithetic variates includes Fishman and
Huang [122] and Rubinstein, Samorodnitsky, and Shaked [312].


4.3 StratiпђБed Sampling

4.3.1 Method and Examples

StratiпђБed sampling refers broadly to any sampling mechanism that constrains
the fraction of observations drawn from speciпђБc subsets (or strata) of the
sample space. Suppose, more speciпђБcally, that our goal is to estimate E[Y ]
with Y real-valued, and let A1 , . . . , AK be disjoint subsets of the real line for
which P (Y вИИ вИ™i Ai ) = 1. Then
210    4 Variance Reduction Techniques

                  
                  K                                       
                                                          K
        E[Y ] =         P (Y вИИ Ai )E[Y |Y вИИ Ai ] =              pi E[Y |Y вИИ Ai ]    (4.31)
                  i=1                                     i=1

with pi = P (Y вИИ Ai ). In random sampling, we generate independent
Y1 , . . . , Yn having the same distribution as Y . The fraction of these samples
falling in Ai will not in general equal pi , though it would approach pi as the
sample size n increased. In stratiпђБed sampling, we decide in advance what
fraction of the samples should be drawn from each stratum Ai ; each observa-
tion drawn from Ai is constrained to have the distribution of Y conditional
on Y вИИ Ai .
     The simplest case is proportional sampling, in which we ensure that the
fraction of observations drawn from stratum Ai matches the theoretical prob-
ability pi = P (Y вИИ Ai ). If the total sample size is n, this entails generating
ni = npi samples from Ai . (To simplify the discussion, we ignore rounding
and assume npi is an integer instead of writing npi .) For each i = 1, . . . , K,
let Yij , j = 1, . . . , ni be independent draws from the conditional distribution
of Y given Y вИИ Ai . An unbiased estimator of E[Y |Y вИИ Ai ] is provided by the
sample mean (Yi1 + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + Yini )/ni of observations from the ith stratum. It
follows from (4.31) that an unbiased estimator of E[Y ] is provided by

                               
                               K
                                            1 
                                                ni
                                                         1 
                                                            K ni
                        YћВ =         pi ¬Ј          Yij =           Yij .            (4.32)
                               i=1
                                            ni j=1       n i=1 j=1

This estimator should be contrasted with the usual sample mean YћД = (Y1 +
¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + Yn )/n of a random sample of size n. Compared with YћД , the stratiпђБed
estimator YћВ eliminates sampling variability across strata without aпђАecting
sampling variability within strata.
      We generalize this formulation in two simple but important ways. First,
we allow the strata to be deпђБned in terms of a second variable X. This stratiпђБ-
cation variable could take values in an arbitrary set; to be concrete we assume
it is d -valued and thus take the strata Ai to be disjoint subsets of d with
P (X вИИ вИ™i Ai ) = 1. The representation (4.31) generalizes to

                 
                 K                                        
                                                          K
       E[Y ] =          P (X вИИ Ai )E[Y |X вИИ Ai ] =              pi E[Y |X вИИ Ai ],   (4.33)
                  i=1                                     i=1

where now pi = P (X вИИ Ai ). In some applications, Y is a function of X (for
example, X may be a discrete path of asset prices and Y the discounted payoпђА
of a derivative security), but more generally they may be dependent without
either completely determining the other. To use (4.33) for stratiпђБed sampling,
we need to generate pairs (Xij , Yij ), j = 1, . . . , ni , having the conditional
distribution of (X, Y ) given X вИИ Ai .
     As a second extension of the method, we allow the stratum allocations
n1 , . . . , nK to be arbitrary (while summing to n) rather than proportional to
                                                       4.3 StratiпђБed Sampling     211

p1 , . . . , pK . In this case, the пђБrst representation in (4.32) remains valid but

By minimizing the variance of this estimator over the qi , we can пђБnd an
allocation rule that is at least as eпђАective as a proportional allocation. We
return to this point later in this section.
    From this introduction it should be clear that the use of stratiпђБed sampling
involves consideration of two issues:
вЧ¶ choosing the stratiпђБcation variable X, the strata A1 , . . . , AK , and the allo-
  cation n1 , . . . , nK ;
вЧ¶ generating samples from the distribution of (X, Y ) conditional on X вИИ Ai .
In addressing the пђБrst issue we will see that stratiпђБed sampling is most eпђАective
when the variability of Y within each stratum is small. Solutions to the second
issue are best illustrated through examples.
Example 4.3.1 Stratifying uniforms. Perhaps the simplest application of
stratiпђБed sampling stratiпђБes the uniformly distributed random variables that
drive a simulation. Partition the unit interval (0, 1) into the n strata
                                       
                      1            1 2                    nвИТ1
            A1 = 0,       , A2 =    ,     , . . . , An =        ,1 .
                      n            n n                      n

Each of these intervals has probability 1/n under the uniform distribution, so
in a proportional allocation we should draw one sample from each stratum.
(The sample size n and the number of strata K are equal in this example.) Let
U1 , . . . , Un be independent and uniformly distributed between 0 and 1 and let
                               i вИТ 1 Ui
                        Vi =        + ,       i = 1, . . . , n.                 (4.35)
                                 n   n
Each Vi is uniformly distributed between (i вИТ 1)/n and i/n, which is to say
that Vi has the conditional distribution of U given U вИИ Ai for U вИЉ Unif[0,1].
Thus, V1 , . . . , Vn constitute a stratiпђБed sample from the uniform distribution.
(In working with the unit interval and the subintervals Ai , we are clearly free
to deпђБne these to be open or closed on the left or right; in each setting, we
adopt whatever convention is most convenient.)
    Suppose Y = f (U ) so that E[Y ] is simply the integral of f over the unit
interval. Then the stratiпђБed estimator

is similar to the deterministic midpoint integration rule

based on the value of f at the midpoints of the Ai . A feature of the random-

    This example easily generalizes to partitions of (0, 1) into intervals of un-
equal lengths. If Ai = (ai , bi ], then the conditional distribution of U given
U вИИ Ai is uniform between ai and bi ; we can sample from this conditional
distribution by setting V = ai + U (bi вИТ ai ). 2
Example 4.3.2 Stratifying nonuniform distributions. Let F be a cumulative

                           F вИТ1 (u) = inf{x : F (x) вЙ§ u}

summing to 1, deпђБne a0 = вИТвИЮ,

a1 = F вИТ1 (p1 ), a2 = F вИТ1 (p1 + p2 ), . . . , aK = F вИТ1 (p1 + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + pK ) = F вИТ1 (1).

DeпђБne strata

              A1 = (a0 , a1 ], A2 = (a1 , a2 ], . . . , AK = (aKвИТ1 , aK ]

or with AK = (aKвИТ1 , aK ) if aK = вИЮ. By construction, each stratum Ai has
probability pi under F ; for if Y has distribution F , then

                       P (Y вИИ Ai ) = F (ai ) вИТ F (aiвИТ1 ) = pi .

Thus, deпђБning strata for F with speciпђБed probabilities is straightforward,
provided one can пђБnd the quantiles ai . Figure 4.4 displays ten equiprobable
(pi = 1/K) strata for the standard normal distribution.
    To use the sets A1 , . . . , AK for stratiпђБed sampling, we need to be able to
generate samples of Y conditional on Y вИИ Ai . As demonstrated in Exam-

then
                               V = aiвИТ1 + U (ai вИТ aiвИТ1 )
is uniformly distributed between aiвИТ1 and ai and then F вИТ1 (V ) has the distri-

    Figure 4.5 illustrates the diпђАerence between stratiпђБed and random sam-
pling from the standard normal distribution. The left panel is a histogram of
500 observations, пђБve from each of 100 equiprobable strata; the right panel is
a histogram of 500 independent draws from the normal distribution. StratiпђБ-
cation clearly produces a better approximation to the underlying distribution.
                                                                   4.3 StratiпђБed Sampling        213

Fig. 4.4. A partition of the real line into ten intervals of equal probability under the

is 1/10.


    How might we use stratiпђБed samples from the normal distribution in sim-
ulating paths of a stochastic process? It would not be legitimate to use one
value from each of 100 strata to generate 100 steps of a single Brownian path:
the increments of Brownian motion are independent but the stratiпђБed values
are not, and ignoring this dependence would produce nonsensical results. In
contrast, we could validly use the stratiпђБed values to generate the пђБrst incre-
ment of 100 replications of a single Brownian path (or the terminal values of
the paths, as explained in Section 4.3.2). In short, in using stratiпђБed sampling
or any other variance reduction technique, we are free to introduce dependence
across replications but not within replications. 2

Fig. 4.5. Comparison of stratiпђБed sample (left) and random sample (right). The
stratiпђБed sample uses 100 equiprobable strata with пђБve samples from each stratum;
the random sample consists of 500 independent draws from the normal distribution.
Both histograms use 25 bins.
214     4 Variance Reduction Techniques

Example 4.3.3 StratiпђБcation through acceptance-rejection. A crude but al-
most universally applicable method for generating samples conditional on a
stratum generates unconditional samples and keeps those that fall in the target
set. This is the method described in Example 2.2.8 for conditional sampling,
and may be viewed as a form of acceptance-rejection in which the acceptance
probability is always 0 or 1.
    To describe this in more detail, we use the notation of (4.33). Our goal is
to generate samples of the pair (X, Y ) using strata A1 , . . . , AK for X, with
ni samples to be generated conditional on X вИИ Ai , i = 1, . . . , K. Given
a mechanism for generating unconditional samples from the distribution of
(X, Y ), we can repeatedly generate such samples until we have produced ni
samples with X вИИ Ai for each i = 1, . . . , K; any extra samples generated from
a stratum are simply rejected.
    The eпђГciency of this method depends on the computational cost of gen-
erating pairs (X, Y ) and determining the stratum in which X falls. It also
depends on the stratum probabilities: if P (X вИИ Ai ) is small, a large number
of candidates may be required to produce ni samples from Ai . These com-
putational costs must be balanced against the reduction in variance achieved
through stratiпђБcation. Glasserman, Heidelberger, and Shahabuddin [143] an-
alyze the overhead from rejected samples in this method based on a Poisson
approximation to the arrival of samples from each stratum. 2
Example 4.3.4 Stratifying the unit hypercube. The methods described in Ex-
amples 4.3.1 and 4.3.2 extend, in principle, to multiple dimensions. Using the
inverse transform method, a vector (X1 , . . . , Xd ) of independent random vari-
ables can be represented as (F1вИТ1 (U1 ), . . . , FdвИТ1 (Ud )) with Fi the distribution
of Xi and U1 , . . . , Ud independent and uniform over [0, 1). In this sense, it suf-
пђБces to consider the uniform distribution over the d-dimensional hypercube
[0, 1)d . (In the case of dependent X1 , . . . , Xd , replace Fi with the conditional
distribution of Xi given X1 , . . . , XiвИТ1 .) In stratifying the unit hypercube with
respect to the uniform distribution, it is convenient to take the strata to be
products of intervals because the probability of such a set is easily calculated
and because it is easy to sample uniformly from such a set by applying a
transformation like (4.35) to each coordinate.
     Suppose, for example, that we stratify the jth coordinate of the hypercube
into Kj intervals of equal length. Each stratum of the hypercube has the form

                      ,d 
                           ij вИТ 1 ij
                                 ,       ,     ij вИИ {1, . . . , Kj }
                      j=1
                             Kj Kj

and has probability 1/(K1 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј Kd ). To generate a vector V uniformly distrib-
uted over this set, generate U1 , . . . , Ud independently from Unif[0,1) and deпђБne
the jth coordinate of V to be
                               ij вИТ 1 + Uj
                        Vj =               ,     j = 1, . . . , d.            (4.36)
                                   Kj
                                                            4.3 StratiпђБed Sampling     215

   In this example, the total number of strata is K1 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј Kd . Generating at least
one point from each stratum therefore requires a sample size at least this large.
Unless the Kj are quite small (in which case stratiпђБcation may provide little
beneпђБt), this is likely to be prohibitive for d larger than 5, say. In Section 4.4
and Chapter 5, we will see methods related to stratiпђБed sampling that are
better suited to higher dimensions. 2

     In this formulation, each YћВj may be thought of as a batch with sample size
k and the original estimator YћВ as the sample mean of m independent batches.
Given a total sample size n, is it preferable to have at least m observations
                                                                     4.3 StratiпђБed Sampling          217

from each stratum, as in this setting, or to increase the number of strata so
that only one observation is drawn from each? A larger m should improve our
estimate of ѕГ(q) and the accuracy of the normal approximation implicit in the
conпђБdence intervals above. However, we will see below (cf. (4.46)) that taking
пђБner strata reduces variance. Thus, as is often the case, we face a tradeoпђА
between reducing variance and accurately measuring variance.

Variance Decomposition

The preceding discussion considers the allocation of samples to given strata.
In order to consider the question of how strata should be selected in the пђБrst
place, we now examine what part of the variance of Y is removed through
stratiпђБcation of X.
                                                        4.3 StratiпђБed Sampling     219

   As before, let A1 , . . . , AK be strata for X. Let ќЈ вЙ° ќЈ(X) вИИ {1, . . . , K}
denote the index of the stratum containing X, so that X вИИ AќЈ . We can
always write
                                  Y = E[Y |ќЈ] +                         (4.44)
simply by deпђБning the residual  so that equality holds. It is immediate that
E[|ќЈ] = 0 and that  is uncorrelated with E[Y |ќЈ] because

                            E[ (E[Y |ќЈ] вИТ E[Y ])] = 0,

as can be seen by пђБrst conditioning on ќЈ. Because (4.44) decomposes Y into
the sum of uncorrelated terms, we have

                         Var[Y ] = Var[E[Y |ќЈ]] + Var[].

We will see that stratiпђБed sampling with proportional allocation eliminates
the пђБrst term on the right, leaving only the variance of the residual term and
thus guaranteeing a variance reduction.
   The residual variance is E[2 ] because E[] = 0. Also,
                                               
                  E[2 |ќЈ] = E (Y вИТ E[Y |ќЈ])2 |ќЈ = Var[Y |ќЈ].

We thus arrive at the familiar decomposition

                      Var[Y ] = Var[E[Y |ќЈ]] + E [Var[Y |ќЈ]] .                   (4.45)

The conditional expectation of Y given ќЈ = i is ¬µi , and the probability that
ќЈ = i is pi . The пђБrst term on the right side of (4.45) is thus
                                                                    2
                                     
                                     K                
                                                      K
                    Var[E[Y |ќЈ]] =         pi ¬µ2i вИТ         pi ¬µi        .
                                     i=1              i=1

Comparing this with (4.43), we conclude from (4.44) and (4.45) that

                                                      
                                                      K
                        Var[] = E [Var[Y |ќЈ]] =            pi ѕГi2 ,
                                                      i=1

which is precisely the variance parameter in (4.42) for stratiпђБed sampling
with proportional allocation. This conпђБrms that the variance parameter of
the stratiпђБed estimator is the variance of the residual of Y after conditioning
on ќЈ.
    Consider now the eпђАect of alternative choices of strata. The total variance
Var[Y ] in (4.45) is constant, so making the residual variance small is equivalent
to making Var[E[Y |ќЈ]] large вАФ i.e., to making Var[¬µќЈ ] large. This indicates that
we should try to choose strata to achieve a high degree of variability across the
stratum means ¬µ1 , . . . , ¬µK and low variability within each stratum. Indeed,
220     4 Variance Reduction Techniques

from (4.45) we пђБnd that stratiпђБcation eliminates inter-stratum variability,
leaving only intra-stratum variability.
    Another consequence of (4.45) is that further stratiпђБcation results in fur-
ther variance reduction. More precisely, suppose the partition {AћГ1 , . . . , AћГKћГ }
reпђБnes the partition {A1 , . . . , AK }, in the sense that the stratum index ќЈћГ of
the new partition completely determines ќЈ. Then E[Y |ќЈ] = E[E[Y |ќЈћГ]|ќЈ] and
JensenвАЩs inequality yields
                            Var[E[Y |ќЈ]] вЙ§ Var[E[Y |ќЈћГ]],                    (4.46)
from which it follows that the residual variance from the reпђБned strata cannot
exceed the residual variance from the original strata.
    The decomposition (4.44) invites a comparison between stratiпђБed sampling
and control variates. Consider the case of real-valued X. Using the method of
Example 4.3.2, we can in principle stratify X using an arbitrarily large number
of equiprobable intervals. As we reпђБne the stratiпђБcation, it is reasonable to
expect that E[Y |ќЈ] will approach E[Y |X]. (For a speciпђБc result of this type
see Lemma 4.1 of Glasserman, Heidelberger, and Shahabuddin [139].) The
decomposition (4.44) becomes
                          Y = E[Y |X] +  = g(X) + ,
with g(x) = E[Y |X = x]. If g is linear, then the variance removed through
(inпђБnitely пђБne) stratiпђБcation of X is precisely the same as the variance that
would be removed using X as a control variate. But in the general case, using
X as a control variate would remove only the variance associated with the
linear part of g near E[X]; see the discussion in Section 4.1.4. In contrast,
inпђБnitely пђБne stratiпђБcation of X removes all the variance of g(X) leaving only
the variance of the residual . In this sense, using X as a stratiпђБcation variable
is more eпђАective than using it as a control variate. However, it should also be
noted that using X as a control requires knowledge only of E[X] and not the
full distribution of X; moreover, it is often easier to use X as a control than
to generate samples from the conditional law of (X, Y ) given the stratum
containing X.

4.3.2 Applications
This section illustrates the application of stratiпђБed sampling in settings some-
what more complex than those in Examples 4.3.1вАУ4.3.4. As noted in Exam-
ple 4.3.4, fully stratifying a random vector becomes infeasible in high dimen-
sions. We therefore focus primarily on methods that stratify a scalar projection
in a multidimensional setting. This can be eпђАective in valuing a derivative se-
curity if its discounted payoпђА is highly variable along the selected projection.

Terminal StratiпђБcation
In the pricing of options, the most important feature of the path of an under-
lying asset is often its value at the option expiration; much of the variability
                                                   4.3 StratiпђБed Sampling              221

in the optionвАЩs payoпђА can potentially be eliminated by stratifying the termi-
nal value. As a step in this direction, we detail the stratiпђБcation of Brownian
motion along its terminal value. In the special case of an asset described
by geometric Brownian motion with constant volatility, this is equivalent to
stratifying the terminal value of the asset price itself.
       Suppose, then, that we need to generate a discrete Brownian path W (t1 ),
. . . , W (tm ) and that we want to stratify the terminal value W (tm ). We can
accomplish this through a variant of the Brownian bridge construction of
Brownian motion presented in Section 3.1. Using the inverse transform method
as in Example 4.3.2 we can stratify W (tm ), and then conditional on each value
of W (tm ) we can generate the intermediate values W (t1 ), . . . , W (tmвИТ1 ).
       Consider, in particular, the case of K equiprobable strata and a propor-
tional allocation. Let U1 , . . . , UK be independent Unif[0,1] random variables
and set

with t0 = 0 and W (0) = 0.

Stratifying a Linear Projection

Generating (W (t1 ), . . . , W (tm )) stratiпђБed along W (tm ) or (4.47) or (4.48) are
all special cases of the problem of generating a multivariate normal random
vector stratiпђБed along some projection. We now turn to this general formula-
tion of the problem.
    Suppose, then, that ќЊ вИЉ N (¬µ, ќ£) in d and that we want to generate ќЊ with
X вЙ° v  ќЊ stratiпђБed for some пђБxed vector v вИИ d . Suppose the d √Ч d matrix ќ£
has full rank. We may take ¬µ to be the zero vector because stratifying v  ќЊ is
equivalent to stratifying v  (ќЊ вИТ ¬µ) since v  ¬µ is a constant. Also, stratifying
X is equivalent to stratifying any multiple of X; by scaling v if necessary, we
may therefore assume that v  ќ£v = 1. Thus,

                       X = v  ќЊ вИЉ N (0, v  ќ£v) = N (0, 1),

so we know how to stratify X using the method in Example 4.3.1.
   The next step is to generate ќЊ conditional on the value of X. First observe
that ќЊ and X are jointly normal with

                          ќЊ                     ќ£ ќ£v
                                вИЉN     0,                    .
                          X                   v  ќ£ v  ќ£v

Using the Conditioning Formula (2.25), we пђБnd that

                           ќ£v              ќ£vv  ќ£                      
      (ќЊ|X = x) вИЉ N               x, ќ£ вИТ             = N ќ£vx, ќ£ вИТ ќ£vv  ќ£ .
                         v  ќ£v            v  ќ£v
Observe that the conditional covariance matrix does not depend on x; this is
important because it means that only a single factorization is required for the
conditional sampling. Let A be any matrix for which AA = ќ£ (such as the
one found by Cholesky factorization) and observe that

       (A вИТ ќ£vv  A)(A вИТ ќ£vv  A)
             = AA вИТ AA vv  ќ£ вИТ ќ£vv  AA + ќ£vv  ќ£vv  ќ£
               = ќ£ вИТ ќ£vv  ќ£,

again using the fact that v  ќ£v = 1. Thus, we can use the matrix A вИТ ќ£vv  A
to sample from the conditional distribution of ќЊ given X.
    The following algorithm generates K samples from N (0, ќ£) stratiпђБed along
the direction determined by v:
   for i = 1, . . . , K
       generate U вИЉ Unif[0,1]
       V вЖР (i вИТ 1 + U )/K
       X вЖР ќ¶вИТ1 (V )
       generate Z вИЉ N (0, I) in d
       ќЊ вЖР ќ£vX + (A вИТ ќ£vv  A)Z
224      4 Variance Reduction Techniques

By construction, of the K values of X generated by this algorithm, exactly one
will fall in each of K equiprobable strata for the standard normal distribution.
But observe that under this construction,

                    v  ќЊ = v  ќ£vX + v  (A вИТ ќ£vv  A)Z = X.

Thus, of the K values of ќЊ generated, exactly one has a projection v  ќЊ falling
into each of K equiprobable strata. In this sense, the algorithm generates
samples from N (0, ќ£) stratiпђБed along the direction determined by v.
    To apply this method to generate a Brownian path with the integral in
(4.47) stratiпђБed, take ќ£ to be the covariance matrix of the Brownian path
(ќ£ij = min(ti , tj ), as in (3.6)) and

      v вИЭ (ѕГ(0) вИТ ѕГ(1), ѕГ(1) вИТ ѕГ(2), . . . , ѕГ(m вИТ 2) вИТ ѕГ(m вИТ 1), ѕГ(m вИТ 1)) ,

normalized so that v  ќ£v = 1. To generate the path with its average (4.48)
stratiпђБed, take v to be the vector with all entries equal to the square root of
the sum of the entries of ќ£. Yet another strategy for choosing stratiпђБcation
directions is to use the principal components of ќ£ (cf. Section 2.3.3).
    Further simpliпђБcation is possible in stratifying a sample from the standard
multivariate normal distribution N (0, I). In this case, the construction above
becomes

              ќЊ = vX + (I вИТ vv  )Z,     X вИЉ N (0, 1),     Z вИЉ N (0, I),

with v now normalized so that v  v = 1. Since X = v  ќЊ, by stratifying X we
stratify the projection of ќЊ onto v. The special feature of this setting is that
the matrix-vector product (I вИТ vv  )Z can be evaluated as Z вИТ v(v  Z), which
requires O(d) operations rather than O(d2 ).
    This construction extends easily to allow stratiпђБcation along multiple di-
rections simultaneously. Let B denote a d √Ч m matrix, m вЙ§ d, whose columns
represent the stratiпђБcation directions. Suppose B has been normalized so that
B  ќ£B = I. If ќ£ itself is the identity matrix, this says that the m columns of
B form a set of orthonormal vectors in d . Where we previously stratiпђБed the
scalar projection X = v  ќЊ, we now stratify the m-vector X = B  ќЊ, noting
that X вИЉ N (0, I). For this, we пђБrst stratify the m-dimensional hypercube as
in Example 4.3.4 and then set Xj = ќ¶вИТ1 (Vj ), j = 1, . . . , m, with (V1 , . . . , Vm )
sampled from a stratum of the hypercube as in (4.36). This samples X from
the m-dimensional standard normal distribution with each of its components
stratiпђБed. We then set

                  ќЊ = ќ£BX + (A вИТ ќ£BB  A)Z,           Z вИЉ N (0, I),

for any d √Ч d matrix A satisfying AA = ќ£. The projection B  ќЊ of ќЊ onto
the columns of B returns X and is stratiпђБed by construction.
    To illustrate this method, we apply it to the LIBOR market model dis-
cussed in Section 3.7, using the notation and terminology of that section. We
                                                    4.3 StratiпђБed Sampling       225

use accrual intervals of length ќі = 1/2 and set all forward rates initially equal
to 6%. We consider a single-factor model (i.e., one driven by a scalar Brownian
motion) with a piecewise constant stationary volatility, meaning that ѕГn (t)
depends on n and t only through n вИТ ќЈ(t), the number of maturity dates re-
maining until Tn . We consider a model in which volatility decreases linearly
from 0.20 to 0.10 over a 20-year horizon, and a model in which all forward
rate volatilities are 0.15.
    Our simulation uses a time increment equal to ќі, throughout which volatil-
ities are constant. We therefore write
                      Tn                 вИЪ n

with Z1 , Z2 , . . . independent N (0, 1) variables. This suggests using the vec-
tor (ѕГn (0), ѕГn (T1 ), . . . , ѕГn (TnвИТ1 )) as the stratiпђБcation direction in sampling
(Z1 , . . . , Zn ) from the standard normal distribution in n .
    Table 4.2 reports estimated variance reduction ratios for pricing various
options in this model. Each entry in the table gives an estimate of the ratio
of the variance using ordinary Monte Carlo to the variance using a stratiпђБed
sample of equal size. The results are based on 40 strata (or simply 40 indepen-
dent samples for ordinary Monte Carlo); the estimated ratios are based 1000
replications, each replication using a sample size of 40. The 1000 replications
merely serve to make the ratio estimates reliable; the ratios themselves should
be interpreted as the variance reduction achieved by using 40 strata.
    The results shown are for a caplet with a maturity of 20 years, a caplet with
a maturity of 5 years, bonds with maturities 20.5 and 5.5 years, and a swaption
maturing in 5 years to enter into a 5-year, пђБxed-for-пђВoating interest rate swap.
The options are all at-the-money. The results are based on simulation in the
spot measure using the log-Euler scheme in (3.120), except for the last row
which applies to the forward measure for maturity 20.5. In each case, the
stratiпђБcation direction is based on the relevant portion of the volatility vector
вАФ forty components for a 20-year simulation, ten components for a 5-year
simulation. (Discounting a payment to be received at Tn+1 requires simulating
to Tn .)
    The results in the table indicate that the variance reduction achieved varies
widely but can be quite substantial. With notable exceptions, we generally see
greater variance reduction at shorter maturities, at least in part because of
the discount factor (see, e.g., (3.109)). The stratiпђБcation direction we use is
tailored to a particular rate Ln (through (4.49)), but not necessarily to the
discount factor. The discount factor becomes a constant under the forward
measure and, accordingly, we see a greater variance reduction in this case,
at the same maturity. Surprisingly, we пђБnd the greatest improvement in the
case of the swaption, even though the stratiпђБcation direction is not speciпђБcally
tailored to the swap rate.

Optimal Directions

In estimating E[f (ќЊ)] with ќЊ вИЉ N (¬µ, ќ£) and f a function from d to , it would
be convenient to know the stratiпђБcation direction v for which stratifying v  ќЊ
would produce the greatest reduction in variance. Finding this optimal v is
rarely possible; we give a few examples for which the optimal direction is
available explicitly.
    With no essential loss of generality, we restrict attention to the case of
E[f (Z)] with Z вИЉ N (0, I). From the variance decomposition (4.45) and the
surrounding discussion, we know that the residual variance after stratifying
a linear combination v  Z is E[Var[f (Z)|ќЈ]], where ќЈ is the (random) index
of the stratum containing v  Z. If we use equiprobable strata and let the
number of strata grow (with each new set of strata reпђБning the previous set),
this residual variance converges to E[Var[f (Z)|v  Z]] (cf. Lemma 4.1 of [139]).
We will therefore compare alternative choices of v through this limiting value.
    In the linear case f (z) = b z, it is evident that the optimal direction is
v = b. Next, let f (z) = z  Az for some d √Ч d matrix A. We may assume that
A is symmetric and thus that it has real eigenvalues ќї1 вЙ• ќї2 вЙ• ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј вЙ• ќїd and
associated orthonormal eigenvectors v1 , . . . , vd . Minimizing E[Var[f (Z)|v  Z]]
over vectors v for which v  v = 1 is equivalent to maximizing Var[E[f (Z)|v  Z]]
over the same set because the two terms sum to Var[f (Z)] for any v. In the
quadratic case, some matrix algebra shows that v  v = 1 implies

                        Var[E[Z  AZ|v  Z]] = (v  Av)2 .

This is maximized by v1 if ќї21 вЙ• ќї2d and by vd if ќї2d вЙ• ќї21 . In other words,
the optimal stratiпђБcation direction is an eigenvector of A associated with an
eigenvalue of largest absolute
                      $        value.
                                $ The eпђАect of optimal stratiпђБcation is to
reduce variance from i ќї2i to i ќї2i вИТ maxi ќї2i .
                                                  4.3 StratiпђБed Sampling      227

    As a пђБnal case, let f (z) = exp( 12 z  Az). For f (Z) to have пђБnite second
moment, we now require that ќї1 < 1/2. Theorem 4.1 of [139] shows that
the optimal stratiпђБcation direction in this case is an eigenvector vj вИЧ where j вИЧ
satisпђБes
                                 2                       2
                          ќїj вИЧ                     ќїi
                                   = max                   .              (4.50)
                        1 вИТ ќїj вИЧ      i=1,...,d 1 вИТ ќїi

As in the previous case, this criterion will always select either ќї1 or ќїd , but
it will not necessarily select the one with largest absolute value.
    Simulation is unnecessary for evaluation of E[f (Z)] in each of these exam-
ples. Nevertheless, a linear, quadratic, or exponential-quadratic function may
be useful as an approximation to a more general f and thus as a guide in
selecting stratiпђБcation directions. Fox [127] uses quadratic approximations for
related purposes in implementing quasi-Monte Carlo methods. Glasserman,
Heidelberger, and Shahabuddin [139] use an exponential-quadratic approxi-
mation for stratiпђБed sampling in option pricing; in their application, A is the
Hessian of the logarithm of an optionвАЩs discounted payoпђА. We discuss this
method in Section 4.6.2.

Radial StratiпђБcation

The symmetry of the standard multivariate normal distribution makes it
possible to draw samples from this distribution with stratiпђБed norm. For
Z вИЉ N (0, I) in d , let
                                X = Z12 + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + Zd2 ,
         вИЪ
so that X = Z is the radius of the sphere on which Z falls. The distribution
of X is chi-square with d degrees of freedom (abbreviated ѕЗ2d ) and is given
explicitly in (3.71). Section 3.4.2 discusses eпђГcient methods for sampling from
ѕЗ2d , but for stratiпђБcation it is more convenient to use the inverse transform
method as explained in Example 4.3.2. There is no closed-form expression for
the inverse of the ѕЗ2d distribution, but the inverse can be evaluated numerically
and methods for doing this are available in many statistical software libraries
(see, e.g., the survey in Section 18.5 of [201]). Hence, by generating a stratiпђБed
sample from Unif[0,1] and then applying the inverse of the ѕЗ2d distribution,
we can generate stratiпђБed values of X.
     The next step is to sample Z conditional on the value of the stratiпђБcation
variable X. Because of the symmetry of the normal distribution,
                                                             вИЪ       given X the
vector Z is uniformly distributed on the sphere of radius X. This is the basis
of the Box-Muller method (cf. Section 2.3) in dimension 2 but it holds вИЪ for all
dimensions d. To sample uniformly from the sphere of radius R = X in d ,
we can extend the Box-Muller construction as follows: sample U1 , . . . , UdвИТ1
independently from Unif[0,1] and set

            Z1 = R cos(2ѕАU1 )
            Z2 = R sin(2ѕАU1 ) cos(2ѕАU2 )

               .. ..       ..
                . .         .
          ZdвИТ1 = R sin(2ѕАU1 ) sin(2ѕАU2 ) ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј sin(2ѕАUdвИТ2 ) cos(2ѕАUdвИТ1 )
            Zd = R sin(2ѕАU1 ) sin(2ѕАU2 ) ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј sin(2ѕАUdвИТ2 ) sin(2ѕАUdвИТ1 ).

    Alternatively, given a method for generating standard normal random vari-
ables we can avoid the evaluation of sines and cosines. If ќЊ1 , . . . , ќЊd are indepen-
dent N (0, 1) random variables and ќЊ = (ќЊ1 , . . . , ќЊd ) , then ќЊ/ќЊ is uniformly

                                                 ќЊ

                                                ќЊ
is uniformly distributed over the sphere of radius of R.
    It should be noted that neither of these constructions extends easily to
stratiпђБed sampling from N (0, ќ£) for general ќ£. If ќґ вИЉ N (0, ќ£) and X = ќґќ£вИТ1 ќґ,
then X вИЉ ѕЗ2d and we can stratify X just as before; moreover, given X, ќґ is
uniformly distributed over the ellipsoid

                                HX = {x вИИ d : x ќ£вИТ1 x = X}.

The diпђГculty lies in sampling uniformly from the ellipsoid. Extending the
Box-Muller construction entails replacing the sines and cosines with elliptic
functions. The second вИЪ  construction does not appear to generalize at all: if
ќЊ вИЉ N (0, ќ£), the vector XќЊ/ ќЊ  ќ£вИТ1 ќЊ lies on the ellipsoid HX but is not
uniformly distributed over the ellipsoid.
    The construction does, however, generalize beyond the standard normal
to the class of spherically contoured distributions. The random vector Y is
said to have a spherically contoured distribution if its conditional distribution
given Y  is uniform over the sphere of radius Y ; see Fang, Kotz, and Ng
[114]. To stratify Y along its radius, we must therefore stratify X = Y ,
which will not be ѕЗ2d except in the normal case. Given X, we can sample Y
uniformly from the sphere of radius X using either of the methods described
above for the normal distribution.
    Radial stratiпђБcation is proposed and applied in [142] as a method for re-
ducing variance in estimating the risk in a portfolio of options for which losses
result from large moves of the underlying assets in any direction.

Stratifying a Poisson Process

In this example, we generate a Poisson process on [0, T ] with the total number
of jumps in this interval stratiпђБed. Let ќї denote the arrival rate for the Poisson
process and N the number of jumps in [0, T ]. Then N is a Poisson random
variable with distribution
                                           (ќїT )k
                       P (N = k) = eвИТќїT           ,   k = 0, 1, 2, . . . .
                                             k!
                                                                    4.3 StratiпђБed Sampling                  229

We can sample from this distribution using the inverse transform method, as in
Figure 3.9, and thus generate a stratiпђБed sample of values as in Example 4.3.2.
    For each value of N in the stratiпђБed sample, we need to generate the
arrival times of the jumps in [0, T ] conditional on the number of jumps N .
For this we use a standard property of the Poisson process: given N = k, the
arrival times of the jumps have the joint distribution of the order statistics of
k independent random variables uniformly distributed over [0, T ]. Thus, we
may start from Unif[0,1] random variables U1 , . . . , Uk , multiply them by T
to make them uniform over [0, T ], and then sort them in ascending order to
obtain the arrival times.
    An alternative to sorting, detailed in Fox [127], samples directly from the
joint distribution of the order statistics. Let V1 , . . . , Vk and U1 , . . . , Uk denote
independent Unif[0,1] random variables. Then
                 1/k    1/(kвИТ1)                               1/k    1/(kвИТ1)            1/k
               V1      V2         ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј Vk ,    ...,     V1         V2         ,    V1                (4.51)

have the joint distribution of the ascending order statistics of U1 , . . . , Uk . For
example,

   P (max(U1 , . . . , Uk ) вЙ§ x) = P (U1 вЙ§ x) ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј P (Uk вЙ§ x) = xk ,                         x вИИ [0, 1],

and the last term in (4.51) simply samples from this distribution by applying
its inverse to V1 . An induction argument veriпђБes correctness of the remaining
terms in (4.51). The products in (4.51) can be evaluated recursively from
right to left. (To reduce round-oпђА error, Fox [127] recommends recursively
                                       1/(kвИТi+1)

                                         1/k    1/(kвИТ1)              1/i

i.e., by rescaling from [0, 1] to [0, T ]. Because the terms in (4.51) are generated

this has the same distribution as (4.52) and allows generation of the arrival
times in a single pass. (Subtracting the values in (4.51) from 1 maps the ith
largest value to the ith smallest.)
    This method of stratiпђБcation extends, in principle, to inhomogeneous Pois-
son processes. The number of arrivals in [0, T ] continues to be a Poisson ran-
dom variable in this case. Conditional on the number of arrivals, the times of
the arrivals continue to be distributed as order statistics, but now of random
variables with a density proportional to the arrival rate ќї(t), t вИИ [0, T ], rather
than a uniform density.

Terminal StratiпђБcation in a Binomial Lattice

A binomial lattice provides a discrete-time, discrete-space approximation to
the evolution of a diпђАusion process. Each node in the lattice (see Figure 4.7)
is associated with a level of the underlying asset (or rate) S; over a single time
step, the movement of the asset is restricted to two successor nodes, usually
corresponding to a move up and a move down. By varying the spacing of the
nodes and the transition probabilities, it is possible to vary the conditional
mean and variance of the change in the underlying asset over a single time
step, and thus to approximate virtually any diпђАusion processes.




                                               p
                                        S
                                              1-p




Fig. 4.7. A four-step binomial lattice. Each node has an associated value S of the
underlying asset. Each node has two successor nodes, corresponding to a move up
and a move down.


    Binomial lattices are widely used for numerical option pricing. A typical
algorithm proceeds by backward induction: an option contract determines the
payoпђАs at the terminal nodes (which correspond to the option expiration); the
option value at any other node is determined by discounting the values at its
two successor nodes.
    Consider, for example, the pricing of a put with strike K. Each terminal
node corresponds to some level S of the underlying asset at expiration and
thus to an option value (K вИТ S)+ . A generic node in the lattice has an вАЬupвАЭ
successor node and a вАЬdownвАЭ successor node; suppose the option values Vu
and Vd , respectively, at the two successor nodes have already been calculated.
If the probability of a move up is p, and if the discount factor over a single
step is 1/(1 + R), then the value at the current node is
                               1
                        V =       (pVu + (1 вИТ p)Vd ) .
                              1+R
                                                  4.3 StratiпђБed Sampling     231

In pricing an American put, the backward induction rule is

                             1
                V = max         (pVu + (1 вИТ p)Vd ) , K вИТ S .
                            1+R

    Binomial option pricing is ordinarily a deterministic calculation, but it can
be combined with Monte Carlo. Some path-dependent options, for example,
are more easily valued through simulation than through backward induction.
In some cases, there are advantages to sampling paths through the binomial
lattice rather than sampling paths of a diпђАusion. For example, in an interest
rate lattice, it is possible to compute bond prices at every node. The avail-
ability of these bond prices can be useful in pricing path-dependent options
on, e.g., bonds or swaps through simulation.
    An ordinary simulation through a binomial lattice starts at the root node
and generates moves up or down using the appropriate probabilities for each
node. As a further illustration of stratiпђБed sampling, we show how to simulate
paths through a binomial lattice with the terminal value stratiпђБed.
    Consider, пђБrst, the case of a binomial lattice for which the probability of
an up move has the same value p at all nodes. In this case, the total number
of up moves N through an m-step lattice has the binomial distribution

                             m k
             P (N = k) =       p (1 вИТ p)mвИТk ,     k = 0, 1, . . . , m.
                             k

Samples from this distribution can be generated using the inverse transform
method for discrete distributions, as in Example 2.2.4, much as in the case
of the Poisson distribution in Figure 3.9. As explained in Example 4.3.2, it is
a simple matter to generate stratiпђБed samples from a distribution using the
inverse transform method. Thus, we have a mechanism for stratifying the total
number of up moves through the lattice. Since the terminal node is determined
by the diпђАerence N вИТ (m вИТ N ) = 2N вИТ m between the number of up and down
moves, stratifying N is equivalent to stratifying the terminal node.
    The next step is to sample a path through the lattice conditional on the
terminal node вАФ equivalently, conditional on the number of up moves N . The
key observation for this procedure is that, given N , all paths through the
lattice with N up moves (hence m вИТ N down moves) are equally likely. Gen-
erating a path conditional on N is simply a matter of randomly distributing
N вАЬupsвАЭ among m moves. At each step, the probability of a move up is the
ratio of the number of remaining up moves to the number of remaining steps.
The following algorithm implements this idea:
   k вЖР N (total number of up moves to be made)
   for i = 0, . . . , m вИТ 1
       if k = 0 move down
       if k вЙ• m вИТ i move up
       if 0 < k < m вИТ i
232    4 Variance Reduction Techniques

           generate U вИЉ Unif[0,1]
           if (m вИТ i)U < k
               k вЖРkвИТ1
               move up
           else move down
The variable k records the number of remaining up moves and m вИТ i is the
number of remaining steps. The condition (m вИТ i)U < k is satisпђБed with
probability k/(m вИТ i). This is the ratio of the number of remaining up moves
to the number of remaining steps, and is thus the conditional probability of an
up move on the next step. Repeating this algorithm for each of the stratiпђБed
values of N produces a set of paths through the lattice with stratiпђБed terminal
node.
    This method extends to lattices in which the probability of an up move
varies from node to node, though this extension requires substantial addi-
tional computing. The пђБrst step is to compute the distribution of the termi-
nal node, which is no longer binomial. The probability of reaching a node can
be calculated using the lattice itself: this probability is the вАЬpriceвАЭ (without
discounting) of a security that pays 1 in that node and 0 everywhere else.
Through forward induction, the probabilities of all terminal nodes can be
found in O(m2 ) operations. Once these are computed, it becomes possible to
use the discrete inverse transform method to generate stratiпђБed samples from
the terminal distribution.
    The next step is to simulate paths through the lattice conditional on a
terminal node. For this, let p denote the unconditional probability of an up
move at the current node. Let hu denote the unconditional probability of
reaching the given terminal node from the up successor of the current node;
let hd denote the corresponding probability from the down successor. Then
the conditional probability of an up move at the current node (given the ter-
minal node) is phu /(phu +(1вИТp)hd ) and the conditional probability of a down
move is (1 вИТ p)hd /(phu + (1 вИТ p)hd). Once the hu and hd have been calculated
at every node, it is therefore a simple matter to simulate paths conditional
on a given terminal node by applying these conditional probabilities at each
step. Implementing this requires calculation of O(m) conditional probabili-
ties at every node, corresponding to the O(m) terminal nodes. These can be
calculated with a total eпђАort of O(m3 ) using backward induction.

4.3.3 PoststratiпђБcation

As should be evident from our discussion thus far, implementation of strat-
iпђБed sampling requires knowledge of stratum probabilities and a mechanism
for conditional sampling from strata. Some of the examples discussed in Sec-
tion 4.3.2 suggest that conditional sampling may be diпђГcult even when com-
puting stratum probabilities is not. PoststratiпђБcation combines knowledge of
stratum probabilities with ordinary independent sampling to reduce variance,
                                                            4.3 StratiпђБed Sampling     233

at least asymptotically. It can therefore provide an attractive alternative to
genuine stratiпђБcation when conditional sampling is costly.
    As before, suppose our objective is to estimate E[Y ]. We have a mech-
anism for generating independent replications (X1 , Y1 ), . . . , (Xn , Yn ) of the
pair (X, Y ); moreover, we know the probabilities pi = P (X вИИ Ai ) for strata
A1 , . . . , AK . As usual, we require that these be positive and sum to 1. For
i = 1, . . . , K, let
                                      n
                                Ni =     1{Xj вИИ Ai }
                                     j=1

denote the number of samples that fall in stratum i and note that this is now
a random variable. Let
                                    
                                    n
                             Si =         1{Xj вИИ Ai }Yj
                                    j=1

denote the sum of those Yj for which Xi falls in stratum i, for i = 1, . . . , K.
   The usual sample mean YћД = (Y1 + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + Yn )/n can be written as

                             S1 + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј + SK   K

                      YћД =                   =        ¬Ј   ,

at least if all Ni are nonzero. By the strong law of large numbers, Ni /n вЖТ pi
and Si /Ni вЖТ ¬µi , with probability 1, where ¬µi = E[Y |X вИИ Ai ] denotes the
stratum mean, as in (4.37). PoststratiпђБcation replaces the random fraction

                                          

Whereas the sample mean YћД assigns weight 1/n to every observation, the
poststratiпђБed estimator weights values falling in stratum i by the ratio pi /Ni .
Thus, values from undersampled strata (Ni < npi ) get more weight and values
from oversampled strata (Ni > npi ) get less weight. To cover the possibility
that none of the n replications falls in the ith stratum, we replace Si /Ni with
zero in (4.53) if Ni = 0.
    It is immediate from the almost sure convergence of Si /Ni to ¬µi that the
poststratiпђБed estimator YћВ is a consistent estimator of E[Y ]. Less clear are its
merits relative to the ordinary sample mean or a genuinely stratiпђБed estimator.
We will see that, asymptotically as the sample size grows, poststratiпђБcation
is as eпђАective as stratiпђБed sampling in reducing variance. To establish this
result, we пђБrst consider properties of ratio estimators more generally.

PoststratiпђБcation: Asymptotic Variance

We now apply this analysis of ratio estimators to derive a central limit theo-

estimators. A straightforward extension of the result for a single ratio gives

This is precisely the asymptotic variance for the stratiпђБed estimator based on
proportional allocation of samples to strata; see (4.42). It can be estimated
consistently by replacing each ѕГi2 with the sample variance of the observations
falling in the ith stratum.
     From this result we see that in the large-sample limit , we can extract all
the variance reduction of stratiпђБed sampling without having to sample condi-
tionally from the strata by instead weighting each observation according to its
stratum. How large the sample needs to be for the two methods to give similar
results depends in part on the number of strata and their probabilities. There
is no simple way to determine at what sample size this limit becomes rele-
vant without experimentation. But stratiпђБed sampling is generally preferable
and poststratiпђБcation is best viewed as an alternative for settings in which
conditional sampling from the strata is diпђГcult.
236     4 Variance Reduction Techniques

4.4 Latin Hypercube Sampling
Latin hypercube sampling is an extension of stratiпђБcation for sampling in
multiple dimensions. Recall from the discussion in Example 4.3.4 that strat-
iпђБed sampling in high dimensions is possible in principle but often infeasible
in practice. The diпђГculty is apparent even in the simple case of sampling
from the d-dimensional hypercube [0, 1)d . Partitioning each coordinate into
K strata produces K d strata for the hypercube, thus requiring a sample size
of at least K d to ensure that each stratum is sampled. For even moderately
large d, this may be prohibitive unless K is small, in which case stratiпђБcation
provides little beneпђБt. For this reason, in Section 4.3.2 we focused on methods
for stratifying a small number of important directions in multidimensional
problems.
    Latin hypercube sampling treats all coordinates equally and avoids the ex-
ponential growth in sample size resulting from full stratiпђБcation by stratifying
only the one-dimensional marginals of a multidimensional joint distribution.
The method, introduced by McKay, Conover, and Beckman [259] and further
analyzed in Stein [337], is most easily described in the case of sampling from
the uniform distribution over the unit hypercube. Fix a dimension d and a
sample size K. For each coordinate i = 1, . . . , d, independently generate a
                    (1)         (K)
stratiпђБed sample Vi , . . . , Vi    from the unit interval using K equiprobable
                (j)
strata; each Vi is uniformly distributed over [(j вИТ 1)/K, j/K). If we arrange
the d stratiпђБed samples in columns,
                                  (1)      (1)          (1)
                               V1    V2 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј Vd
                                 (2)    (2)      (2)
                               V1    V2 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј Vd
                                 ..    ..       ..
                                  .     .        .
                                  (K)      (K)          (K)
                               V1        V2      ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј Vd

then each row gives the coordinates of a point in [0, 1)d . The пђБrst row identiпђБes
a point in [0, 1/K)d, the second a point in [1/K, 2/K)d, and so on, correspond-
ing to K points falling in subcubes along the diagonal of the unit hypercube.
Now randomly permute the entries in each column of the array. More precisely,
let ѕА1 , . . . , ѕАd be permutations of {1, . . . , K}, drawn independently from the
distribution that makes all K! such permutations equally likely. Let ѕАj (i) de-
note the value to which i is mapped by the jth permutation. The rows of the
array
                                ѕА (1)   ѕА (1)             ѕА (1)
                              V1 1    V2 2        ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј Vd d
                                ѕА (2)   ѕА (2)             ѕА (2)
                              V1 1    V2 2        ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј Vd d
                                 ..      ..                ..                 (4.55)
                                  .       .                 .
                             ѕА (K)       ѕА (K)              ѕА (K)
                           V1 1         V2 2      ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј Vd d
continue to identify points in [0, 1)d , but they are no longer restricted to
the diagonal. Indeed, each row is a point uniformly distributed over the unit
                                                 4.4 Latin Hypercube Sampling        237

hypercube. The K points determined by the K rows are not independent: if
we project the K points onto their ith coordinates, the resulting set of values
    ѕА (1)         ѕА (K)                          (1)          (K)
{Vi i , . . . , Vi i } is the same as the set {Vi , . . . , Vi }, and thus forms
a stratiпђБed sample from the unit interval.
    The вАЬmarginalвАЭ stratiпђБcation property of Latin hypercube sampling is il-
lustrated in Figure 4.8. The пђБgure shows a sample of size K = 8 in dimension
d = 2. Projecting the points onto either of their two coordinates shows that
exactly one point falls in each of the eight bins into which each axis is par-
titioned. StratiпђБed sampling would require drawing a point from each square
and thus a sample size of 64.

                           = ќ¶вИТ1 (Vi ),

construction in (4.55) continues to apply, provided we have some mechanism
for stratifying the marginals: generate a stratiпђБed sample from each marginal
using K equiprobable strata for each, then randomly permute these d stratiпђБed
samples.
    This construction does rely crucially on the assumption of independent
marginals, and transforming variables to introduce dependence can aпђАect the
partial stratiпђБcation properties of Latin hypercube samples in complicated
ways. This is evident in the case of a multivariate normal distribution N (0, ќ£).
To sample from N (0, ќ£), we set X = AZ with Z вИЉ N (0, I) and AA = ќ£.
Replacing independently generated Zs with the Latin hypercube sample (4.57)
produces points X (j) = AZ (j) , j = 1, . . . , K; but the marginals of the X (j)
so constructed will not in general be stratiпђБed. Rather, the marginals of the
AвИТ1 X (j) are stratiпђБed.
Example 4.4.1 Brownian paths. As a speciпђБc illustration, consider the sim-
ulation of Brownian paths at times 0 = t0 < t1 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј < td . As in (4.57), let
Z (1) , . . . , Z (K) denote a Latin hypercube sample from N (0, I) in d dimen-
sions. From these K points in d , we generate K discrete Brownian paths
W (1) , . . . , W (K) by setting
                                 
                                 n
                                                 (j)
                 W (j) (tn ) =         ti вИТ tiвИТ1 Zi ,   n = 1, . . . , d.
                                 i=1

If we пђБx a time tn , n вЙ• 2, and examine the K values W (1) (tn ), . . . , W (K) (tn ),
these will not form a stratiпђБed sample from N (0, tn ). It is rather the incre-
ments of the Brownian paths that would be stratiпђБed.
    These K Brownian paths could be used to generate K paths of a process
driven by a single Brownian motion. It would not be appropriate to use
the K Brownian paths to generate a single path of a process driven by a
K-dimensional Brownian motion. Latin hypercube sampling introduces de-
pendence between elements of the sample, whereas the coordinates of a K-
dimensional (standard) Brownian motion are independent. Using (W (1) , . . . ,
W (K) ) in place of a K-dimensional Brownian motion would thus change the
law of the simulated process and could introduce severe bias. In contrast, the
marginal law of each W (j) coincides with that of a scalar Brownian motion.
Put succinctly, in implementing a variance reduction technique we are free to
introduce dependence across paths but not within paths. 2
Example 4.4.2 Paths through a lattice. To provide a rather diпђАerent exam-
ple, we apply Latin hypercube sampling to the problem of simulating paths
                                         4.4 Latin Hypercube Sampling      239

through a binomial lattice. (See Figure 4.7 and the surrounding discussion for
background.) Consider an m-step lattice with пђБxed probabilities p and 1 вИТ p.
The вАЬmarginalsвАЭ in this example correspond to the m time steps, so the di-
mension d equals m, and the sample size K is the number of paths. We encode
a move up as a 1 and a move down as a 0. For each i = 1, . . . , m we generate
a stratiпђБed sample of 1s and 0s: we вАЬgenerateвАЭ pK 1s and K вИТ pK 0s, and
if pK is not an integer, the Kth value in the sample is 1 with probability p
and 0 with probability 1 вИТ p. For example, with d = 4, K = 8, and p = 0.52,
we might get
                                    1111
                                    1111
                                    1111
                                    1111
                                    0000
                                    0000
                                    0000
                                    0100
the columns corresponding to the d = 4 stratiпђБed samples. Applying a random
permutation to each column produces, e.g.,

                                    0101
                                    0011
                                    1101
                                    1110
                                    0100
                                    1011
                                    0010
                                    1100

Each row now encodes a path through the lattice. For example, the last row
corresponds to two consecutive moves up followed by two consecutive moves
down. Notice that, for each time step i, the fraction of paths on which the
ith step is a move up is very nearly p. This is the property enforced by Latin
hypercube sampling.
    One could take this construction a step further to enforce the (nearly)
correct fraction of up moves at each node rather than just at each time step.
For simplicity, suppose Kpd is an integer. To the root node, assign Kp 1s and
K(1 вИТ p) 0s. To the node reached from the root by taking  steps up and k
steps down,  + k < d, assign Kp +1 (1 вИТ p)k ones and Kp (1 вИТ p)k+1 zeros.
Randomly and independently permute the ones and zeros at all nodes. The
result encodes K paths through the lattice with the fraction of up moves out
of every node exactly equal to p.
    Mintz [269] develops a simple way to implement essentially the same idea.
His implementation eliminates the need to precompute and permute the out-
comes at all nodes. Instead, it assigns to each node a counter that keeps track
240     4 Variance Reduction Techniques

of the number of paths that have left that node by moving up. For example,
consider again a node reached from the root by taking  steps up and k steps
down, +k < d. Let K be the total number of paths to be generated and again
suppose for simplicity that Kpd is an integer. The number of paths reaching
the designated node is
                            K k = Kp (1 вИТ p)k ,
so the counter at that node counts from zero to pK k , the number of paths
that exit that node by moving up. If a path reaches the node and пђБnds the
counter at i, it moves down with probability i/pK k and moves up with the
complementary probability. If it moves up, the counter is incremented to i + 1.
2

Output Analysis

Under various additional conditions on f , Loh [238], Owen [285], and Stein
[337] establish a central limit theorem for YћВ of the form
                            вИЪ
                             K[ќ±ћВf вИТ ќ±f ] вЗТ N (0, ѕГ2 ),
242     4 Variance Reduction Techniques

which in principle provides the basis for a large-sample conпђБdence interval for
ќ±f based on Latin hypercube sampling. In practice, ѕГ2 is neither known nor
easily estimated, making this approach diпђГcult to apply.
     A simpler approach to interval estimation generates i.i.d. estimators
ќ±ћВf (1), . . . , ќ±ћВf (n), each based on a Latin hypercube sample of size K. An
asymptotically (as n вЖТ вИЮ) valid 1 вИТ ќі conпђБdence interval for ќ±f is provided
by                                             
