             21

eliminates that term too. Notice that the correct weights to apply to XћВ h , XћВ 2h ,
and any other discretization depend on the weak order of convergence of the
scheme used.


6.3 Extensions
6.3.1 General Expansions

The derivations leading to the strong пђБrst-order scheme (6.10) and the weak
second-order schemes (6.28) and (6.32) generalize to produce approximations
that are, in theory, of arbitrarily high weak or strong order, under conditions
on the coeпђГcient functions. These higher-order methods can be cumbersome
to implement and are of questionable practical signiпђБcance; but they are of
considerable theoretical interest and help underscore a distinction between
weak and strong approximations.
    We consider a d-dimensional process X driven by an m-dimensional stan-
dard Brownian motion W through an SDE of the form

                                             
                                             d
                  dX(t) = b0 (X(t)) dt +           bj (X(t)) dW (t).
                                             j=1

We have written the drift coeпђГcient as b0 rather than a to allow more compact
notation in the expansions that follow. Let L0 be as in (6.29) but with ai
replaced by b0i and let Lk be as in (6.30), k = 1, . . . , m.
   For any n = 1, 2, . . ., and any j1 , j2 , . . . , jn вИИ {0, 1, . . . , m}, deпђБne the
multiple integrals
                            t+h      u3  u2
      I(j1 ,j2 ,...,jn ) =       ¬Ј¬Ј¬Ј           dWj1 (u1 ) dWj2 (u2 ) ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј dWjn (un ),
                       t          t     t
                                                                        6.3 Extensions     363

with the convention that dW0 (u) = du. These integrals generalize those used
in Section 6.2.2. Here, t and h are arbitrary positive numbers; as in Sec-
tion 6.2.2, our objective is to approximate an arbitrary increment from X(t)
to X(t + h).
    The general weak expansion of order ќ≤ = 1, 2, . . . takes the form
                              
                              ќ≤      
         X(t + h) вЙИ X(t) +                     Lj1 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј LjnвИТ1 bjn I(j1 ,...,jn ) ,      (6.44)
                              n=1 j1 ,...,jn

with each ji ranging over 0, 1, . . . , m. (This approximation applies to each
coordinate of the vectors X and bjn .) When ќ≤ = 2, this reduces to the second-
order scheme in (6.32). Kloeden and Platen [211] (Section 14.5) justify the
general case and provide conditions under which this approximation produces
a scheme with weak order ќ≤.
    In contrast to (6.44), the general strong expansion of order ќ≤ = 1/2, 1,
3/2,. . . takes the form (Kloeden and Platen [211], Section 14.5)
                                 
           X(t + h) вЙИ X(t) +              Lj1 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј LjnвИТ1 bjn I(j1 ,...,jn ) . (6.45)
                             (j1 ,...,jn )вИИAќ≤

The set Aќ≤ is deпђБned as follows. A vector of indices (j1 , . . . , jn ) is in Aќ≤ if
either (i) the number of indices n plus the number of indices that are 0 is less
than or equal to 2ќ≤, or (ii) n = ќ≤ + 12 and all n indices are 0. Thus, when
ќ≤ = 1, the weak expansion sums over j = 0 and j = 1 (the Euler scheme),
whereas the strong expansion sums over j = 0, j = 1, and (j1 , j2 ) = (1, 1) to
get (6.10). Kloeden and Platen [211], Section 10.6, show that (6.45) indeed
results in an approximation with strong order ќ≤.
    Both expansions (6.44) and (6.45) follow from repeated application of the
steps we used in (6.7) and (6.22)вАУ(6.23). The distinction between the two
expansions can be summarized as follows: the weak expansion treats terms
вИЖWj , j = 0, as having the same order as h, whereas the strong expansion
treats them as having order h1/2 . Thus, indices equal to zero (corresponding
to вАЬdtвАЭ terms rather than вАЬdWj вАЭ terms) count double in reckoning the number
of terms to include in the strong expansion (6.45).

6.3.2 Jump-DiпђАusion Processes
Let {N (t), t вЙ• 0} be a Poisson process, let {Y1 , Y2 , . . .} be i.i.d. random
vectors, W a standard multidimensional Brownian motion with N , W , and
{Y1 , Y2 , . . .} independent of each other. Consider jump-diпђАusion models of the
form
 dX(t) = a(X(tвИТ)) dt+ b(X(tвИТ)) dW (t)+ c(X(tвИТ), YN (tвИТ)+1 ) dN (t). (6.46)
Between jumps of the Poission process, X evolves like a diпђАusion with coeпђГ-
cient functions a and b; at the nth jump of the Poisson process, the jump in
X is
364    6 Discretization Methods

                        X(t) вИТ X(tвИТ) = c(X(tвИТ), Yn ),
a function of the state of X just before the jump and the random variable Yn .
We discussed a special case of this model in Section 3.5.1. Various processes
of this type are used to model the dynamics of underlying assets in pricing
derivative securities.
    Mikulevicius and Platen [265] extend the general weak expansion (6.44)
to processes of this type and analyze the discretization schemes that follow
from this expansion. Their method uses a pure-diпђАusion discretization method
between the jumps of the Poisson process and applies the function c to the dis-
cretized process to determine the jump in the discretized process at a jump of
the Poisson process. The jump magnitudes are thus computed exactly, condi-
tional on the value of the discretized process just before a jump. Mikulevicius
and Platen [265] show, in fact, that the weak order of convergence of this
method equals the order of the scheme used for the pure-diпђАusion part, under
conditions on the coeпђГcient functions a, b, and c.
    In more detail, this method supplements the original time grid 0, h, 2h, . . .
with the jump times of the Poisson process. Because the Poisson process is
independent of the Brownian motion, we can imagine generating all of these
jump times at the start of a simulation. (See Section 3.5 for a discussion of
the simulation of Poisson processes.) Let 0 = ѕД0 , ѕД1 , ѕД2 , . . . be the combined
time grid, including both the multiples of h and the Poisson jump times. The
discretization scheme proceeds by simulating XћВ from ѕДi to ѕДi+1 , i = 0, 1, . . ..
Given XћВ(ѕДi ), we apply an Euler scheme or higher-order scheme to generate
XћВ(ѕДi+1 вИТ), using the coeпђГcient functions a and b. If ѕДi+1 is a Poisson jump
time вАФ the nth, say вАФ we generate Yn and set

                   XћВ(ѕДi+1 ) = XћВ(ѕДi+1 вИТ) + c(XћВ(ѕДi+1 вИТ), Yn ).

If ѕДi+1 is not a jump time, we set XћВ(ѕДi+1 ) = XћВ(ѕДi+1 вИТ).
    Glasserman and Merener [145] apply this method to a version of the LI-
BOR market model with jumps. The jump processes they consider are more
general than Poisson processes, having arrival rates that depend on the cur-
rent level of forward rates. They extend the method to this setting by using
a bound on the state-dependent arrival rates to construct jumps by thinning
a Poisson process. This requires relaxing the smoothness conditions imposed
on c in Mikulevicius and Platen [265].
    Maghsoodi [245] provides various alternative discretization schemes for
jump-diпђАusion processes and considers both strong and weak error criteria.
He distinguishes jump-adapted methods (like the one described above) that
include the Poisson jump epochs in the time grid from those that use a пђБxed
grid. A jump-adapted method may become computationally burdensome if the
jump intensity is very high. Protter and Talay [301] analyze the Euler scheme
for stochastic diпђАerential equations driven by LeћБvy processes, which include
(6.46) as a special case. Among other results, they provide error expansions
in powers of h justifying the use of Richardson extrapolation.
                                                         6.3 Extensions     365

6.3.3 Convergence of Mean Square Error

The availability of discretization schemes of various orders poses a tradeoпђА:
using a higher-order scheme requires more computing time per path and thus
reduces the number of paths that can be completed in a пђБxed amount of time.
The number of paths completed aпђАects the standard error of any estimates we
compute, but has no eпђАect on discretization bias, which is determined by our
choice of scheme. Thus, we face a tradeoпђА between reducing bias and reducing
variance.
    We discussed this tradeoпђА in a more general setting in Section 1.1.3 and
the asymptotic conclusions reached there apply in the current setting. Here
we present a slightly diпђАerent argument to arrive at the same conclusion.
    We suppose that our objective is to minimize mean square error (MSE),
the sum of variance and squared bias. Using a discretization scheme of weak
order ќ≤, we expect
                                  Bias вЙИ c1 hќ≤
for some constant c1 . For the variance based on n paths we expect
                                               c2
                                Variance вЙИ
                                               n
for some constant c2 . The time step h would generally have some eпђАect on
variance; think of c2 as the limit as h decreases to zero of the variance per
replication.
     If we make the reasonable assumption that the computing time per path is
proportional to the number of steps per path, then it is inversely proportional
to h. The total computing time for n paths is then nc3 /h, for some constant
c3 .
     With these assumptions and approximations, we formulate the problem of
minimizing MSE subject to a computational budget s as follows:
                                c2                 nc3
                   min c21 h2ќ≤ +       subject to        = s.
                    n,h           n                  h
Using the constraint to eliminate a variable, we put this in the form
                                         c2 c3
                            min c21 h2ќ≤ +        ,
                              h            hs
which is minimized at                      1
                                 h = csвИТ 2ќ≤+1                             (6.47)
with c a constant. Substituting this back into our expressions for the squared
bias and the variance, we get
                                2ќ≤             2ќ≤        2ќ≤
                  MSE вЙИ c1 sвИТ 2ќ≤+1 + c2 sвИТ 2ќ≤+1 = c sвИТ 2ќ≤+1 ,

for some constants c , c1 , c2 . The optimal allocation thus balances variance
and squared bias. Also, the optimal root mean square error becomes
366       6 Discretization Methods
                                вИЪ                 ќ≤
                                     MSE вИЭ sвИТ 2ќ≤+1 .                   (6.48)

This is what we found in Section 1.1.3 as well.
   These calculations show how the order ќ≤ of a scheme aпђАects both the opti-
mal allocation of eпђАort and the convergence rate under the optimal allocation.
As the convergence order ќ≤ increases, the optimal convergence rate in (6.48)
approaches sвИТ1/2 , the rate associated with unbiased simulation. But for the
important cases of ќ≤ = 1 (пђБrst-order) and ќ≤ = 2 (second-order) we get rates of
sвИТ1/3 and sвИТ2/5 . This makes precise the notion that simulating a process for
which a discretization scheme is necessary is harder than simulating a solv-
able model. It also shows that when very accurate results are required (i.e.,
when s is large), a higher-order scheme will ultimately dominate a lower-order
scheme.
   DuпђГe and Glynn [100] prove a limit theorem that justiпђБes the convergence
rate implied by (6.48). They also report numerical results that are generally
consistent with their theoretical predictions.


6.4 Extremes and Barrier Crossings: Brownian
Interpolation
In Section 6.2.3 we showed that discretization methods can sometimes be ex-
tended to path-dependent payoпђАs through supplementary state variables. The
additional state variables remove dependence on the past; standard discretiza-
tion procedures can then be applied to the augmented state vector.
    In option pricing applications, path-dependence often enters through the
maximum or minimum of an underlying asset over the life of the option. This
includes, for example, options whose payoпђАs depend on whether or not an un-
derlying asset crosses a barrier. Here, too, path-dependence can be eliminated
by including the running maximum or minimum in the state vector. However,
this renders standard discretization procedures inapplicable because of the
singular dynamics of these supplementary variables. The running maximum,
for example, can increase only when it is equal to the underlying process.
    This issue arises even when the underlying process X is a standard Brown-
ian motion. Let
                               M (t) = max X(u)
                                          0вЙ§uвЙ§t

and let
                 MћВ h (n) = max{X(0), X(h), X(2h), . . . , X(nh)}.     (6.49)
           h
Then MћВ (n) is the maximum of the Euler approximation to X over [0, nh];
the Euler approximation to X is exact for X itself because X is Brownian
motion. Fix a time T and let h = T /n so that MћВ h (n) is the discrete-time
approximation of M (T ). Asmussen, Glynn, and Pitman [24] show that the
normalized error
              6.4 Extremes and Barrier Crossings: Brownian Interpolation       367

                             hвИТ1/2 [MћВ h (n) вИТ M (T )]
has a limiting distribution as h вЖТ 0. This result may be paraphrased as
stating that the distribution of MћВ h (n) converges to that of M (T ) at rate
h1/2 . It follows that the weak order of convergence (in the sense of (6.14))
cannot be greater than 1/2. In contrast, we noted in Section 6.1.2 that for
SDEs with smooth coeпђГcient functions the Euler scheme has weak order of
convergence 1. Thus, the singularity of the dynamics of the running maximum
leads to a slower convergence rate.
    In the case of Brownian motion, this diпђГculty can be circumvented by
sampling M (T ) directly, rather than through (6.49). We can sample from
the joint distribution of X(T ) and M (T ) as follows. First we generate X(T )
from N (0, T ). Conditional on X(T ) the process {X(t), 0 вЙ§ t вЙ§ T } becomes a
Brownian bridge, so we need to sample from the distribution of the maximum
of a Brownian bridge. We discussed how to do this in Example 2.2.3. Given
X(T ), set
                                X(T ) + X(T )2 вИТ 2T log U

with U вИЉ Unif[0,1] independent of X(T ). The pair (X(T ), M (T )) then has
the joint distribution of the terminal and maximum value of the Brownian

for more general processes. Suppose X is a diпђАusion satisfying the SDE (6.1)
with scalar coeпђГcient functions a and b. Let XћВ(i) = XћВ(ih), i = 0, 1, . . ., be
a discrete-time approximation to X, such as one deпђБned through an Euler

can expect to get a better approximation by interpolating over the interval
[ih, (i + 1)h) using a Brownian motion with пђБxed parameters ai = a(XћВ(i))
and bi = b(XћВ(i)). Given the endpoints XћВ(i) and XћВ((i + 1)), the maximum of

                                 

with U0 , U1 , . . . independent Unif[0,1] random variables. (The value of a(XћВ(i))

                           max{MћВ0 , MћВ1 , . . . , MћВnвИТ1 }.

Barrier Crossings

Similar ideas apply in pricing barrier options with continuously monitored
barriers. Suppose B > X(0) and let

                           ѕД = inf{t вЙ• 0 : X(t) > B}.

A knock-out option might have a payoпђА of the form

                            (K вИТ X(T ))+ 1{ѕД > T },                          (6.51)

with K a constant. This requires simulation of X(T ) and the indicator 1{ѕД >
T }.
    The simplest method sets

                              ѕДћВ = inf{i : XћВ(i) > B}

and approximates (X(T ), 1{ѕД > T }) by (XћВ(n), 1{ѕДћВ > n}) with h = T /n, for
some discretizaton XћВ. But even if we could simulate X exactly on the discrete
grid 0, h, 2h, . . . , this would not sample 1{ѕД > T } exactly: it is possible for X
to cross the barrier at some time t between grid points ih and (i + 1)h and
never be above the barrier at any of the dates 0, h, 2h, . . ..
    The method in (6.50) can be used to reduce discretization error in sampling
the survival indicator 1{ѕД > T }. Observe that the barrier is crossed in the
interval [ih, (i + 1)h) precisely if the maximum over this interval exceeds B.
Hence, we can approximate the survival indicator 1{ѕД > T } using

                                 ,
                                 nвИТ1
                                       1{MћВi вЙ§ B},                           (6.52)
                                 i=0

with nh = T and MћВi as in (6.50).
    This method can be simpliпђБed. Rather than generate MћВi , we can sample
the indicators 1{MћВi вЙ§ B} directly. Given XћВ(i) and XћВ(i + 1), this indicator
takes the value 1 with probability

For пђБxed U0 , U1 , . . . , UnвИТ1 , this has the same value as (6.52) but is slightly sim-

Thus, rather than generate the barrier-crossing indicators, we can just multi-
ply by the probabilities pћВi .
     Because (6.55) is the conditional expectation of (6.53), the two have the
same expectation and thus the same discretization bias. By JensenвАЩs inequal-
ity, the second moment of (6.53) is larger than the second moment of its condi-
tional expectation (6.55), so using (6.55) rather than (6.53) reduces variance.
(This is an instance of a more general strategy for reducing variance known
as conditional Monte Carlo, based on replacing an estimator with its con-
ditional expectation; see, Boyle et al. [53] for other applications in пђБnance.)
Using (6.53), we would stop simulating a path once some MћВi exceeds B. Using
(6.55), we never generate the MћВi and must therefore simulate every path for
n steps, unless some XћВ(i) exceeds B (in which case pћВi = 0). So, although
(6.55) has lower variance, it requires greater computational eпђАort per path.
A closely related tradeoпђА is investigated by Glasserman and Staum [146];
they consider estimators in which each transition of an underlying asset is
sampled conditional on not crossing a barrier. In their setting, products of
survival probabilities like those in (6.55) serve as likelihood ratios relating the
conditional and unconditional evolution of the process.
370    6 Discretization Methods

    Baldi, Caramellino, and Iovino [32] develop methods for reducing dis-
cretization error in a general class of barrier option simulation problems. They
consider single- and double-barrier options with time-varying barriers and de-
velop approximations to the one-step survival probabilities that reпђБne the pћВi
above. The pћВi are based on a single constant barrier and a Brownian approxi-
mation over a time interval of length h. Baldi et al. [32] derive asymptotics of
the survival probabilities as h вЖТ 0 for quite general diпђАusions based, in part,
on a linear approximation to upper and lower barriers.

Averages Revisited

As already noted, the simulation estimators based on (6.50) or (6.52) can be
viewed as the result of using Brownian motion to interpolate between the
points XћВ(i) and XћВ(i + 1) in a discretization scheme. The same idea can be
applied in simulating other path-dependent quantities besides extremes and
barrier-crossing indicators.
   As an example, consider simulation of the pair
                                               

XћВ(i), i = 0, 1, . . . , n, and uses Brownian interpolation to approximate the
integral. More explicitly, the approximation is

                                                    6.5 Changing Variables       371

6.5 Changing Variables
We conclude our discussion of discretization methods by considering the пђВexi-
bility to change variables through invertible transformations of a process. If X
is a d-dimensional diпђАusion and g : d вЖТ d is a smooth, invertible transfor-
mation, we can deпђБne a process Y (t) = g(X(t)), simulate a discretization YћВ ,
and deпђБne XћВ = g вИТ1 (YћВ ) to get a discretization of the original process X. Thus,
even if we restrict ourselves to a particular discretization method (an Euler or
higher-order scheme), we have a great deal of пђВexibility in how we implement
it. Changing variables has the potential to reduce bias and can also be useful
in enforcing restrictions (such as nonnegativity) on simulated values. There
is little theory available to guide such transformations; we illustrate the idea
with some examples.

Taking Logarithms

Many of the stochastic processes that arise in mathematical пђБnance take only
positive values. This property often results from specifying that the diпђАu-
sion term be proportional to the current level of the process, as in geo-
metric Brownian motion and in the LIBOR market model of Section 3.7.
If the coordinates of a d-dimensional process X are positive, we may deпђБne
Yi (t) = log Xi (t), i = 1, . . . , d, apply ItoћВвАЩs formula to derive an SDE satisпђБed
by Y = (Y1 , . . . , Yd ), simulate a discretization YћВ of Y , and then (if they are
needed) approximate the original Xi with XћВi = exp(YћВi ). We encountered this
idea in Section 3.7.3 in the setting of the LIBOR market model.
     Applying a logarithmic transformation can have several beneпђБts. First, it
ensures that the simulated XћВi are positive because they result from exponen-
tiation, whereas even a high-order scheme applied directly to the dynamics of
X will produce some negative values. Keeping the variables positive can be
important if the variables represent asset prices or interest rates.
     Second, a logarithmic transformation can enhance the numerical stability
of a discretization method, meaning that it can reduce the propagation of
round-oпђА error. A process with вАЬadditive noiseвАЭ can generally be simulated
with less numerical error than a process with вАЬmultiplicative noise.вАЭ Numerical
stability is discussed in greater detail in Kloeden and Platen [211].
     Third, a logarithmic transformation can reduce discretization bias. For
example, an Euler scheme applied to geometric Brownian motion becomes
exact if we пђБrst take logarithms. More generally, if the coeпђГcients of a diпђАusion
are nearly proportional to the level of the process, then the coeпђГcitions of the
log process are nearly constant.
     This idea is illustrated in Figure 6.3, which is similar to examples in
Glasserman and Merener [145] and is based on numerical results obtained
by Nicolas Merener. The пђБgure shows estimated biases in pricing a six-month
caplet maturing in 20 years using the LIBOR market model with the decreas-
ing volatility parameters used in Table 4.2. The largest practical time step in
372    6 Discretization Methods

this setting is the length of the accrual period; the пђБgure compares methods
using one, two, and four steps per accrual period with between four and 20
million replications per method. In this example, taking logarithms cuts the
absolute bias roughly in half for the Euler scheme. The пђБgure also shows re-
sults using a second-order method for rates (√Ч) and log rates (вЧ¶); even with
10 million replications, the mean errors in these methods are not statistically
distinguishable from zero. In a LIBOR market model with jumps, Glasserman
and Merener [145] пђБnd experimentally that a пђБrst-order scheme applied to log
rates is as accurate as a second-order scheme applied to the rates themselves.

                                                 Euler

Fig. 6.3. Estimated bias versus number of steps in caplet pricing. The √Ч and вЧ¶
correspond to second-order schemes for rates and log rates, respectively.


    As an aside, we note that the most time-consuming part of simulating
an Euler approximation to a LIBOR market model is evaluating the drift
coeпђГcient. To save computing time, one might use the same value of the drift
for multiple time steps, along the lines of Example 4.1.4, but with occasional
updating of the drift. A similar idea is found to be eпђАective in Hunter et
al. [192] as part of a predictor-corrector method (of the type discussed in
Chapter 15 of Kloeden and Platen [211]).

Imposing Upper and Lower Bounds

Taking logarithms enforces a lower bound at zero on a discretized process.
Suppose the coordinates of X are known to evolve in the interval (0, 1). How
could this be imposed on a discretization of X? Enforcing such a condition
could be important if, for example, the coordinates of X are zero-coupon
bond prices, which should always be positive and should never exceed their
face value of 1.
    Glasserman and Wang [149] consider the transformations

                                                           Xi
                   Yi = ќ¶вИТ1 (Xi )     and    Yi = log                ,           (6.56)

formula produces an SDE for Y from an SDE for X in either case; the result-

produce
                                             exp(YћВi )
                  XћВi = ќ¶(YћВi ) or XћВi =                 .
                                           1 + exp(YћВi )
  If the coordinates of X correspond to bonds of increasing maturities, we
may also want to enforce an ordering of the form

                      1 вЙ• X1 (t) вЙ• X2 (t) вЙ• ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј вЙ• Xd (t) вЙ• 1                   (6.57)

discretization (YћВ1 , . . . , YћВd ); applying the inverse of g produces a discretization
(XћВ1 , . . . , XћВd ) satsifying (6.57).

Constant DiпђАusion Transformation

Consider the SDE (6.1) in the case of scalar X and W . Suppose there exists

which g (x) = 1/b(x). With Y (t) = g(X(t)), ItoћВвАЩs formula gives
                                               
dY (t) = a(X(t))g (X(t)) + 12 b2 (X(t))g (X(t)) dt + g (X(t))b(X(t)) dW (t)
       = aћГ(Y (t)) dt + dW (t),

with aћГ(y) = a(f (y))g (f (y))+ 12 b2 (f (y))g (f (y)) and f the inverse of g. Chang-

coeпђГcient. This is potentially useful in reducing discretization error, though
the impact on the drift cannot be disregarded. Moving all state-dependence
from the diпђАusion to the drift is attractive in combination with a predictor-
corrector method, which improves accuracy by averaging current and future
levels of the drift coeпђГcient.
    To illustrate the constant diпђАusion transformation we apply it to the

for all i, j, k = 1, . . . , d. This condition implies the commutativity condition
(6.33). It follows that processes X that can be transformed to have a con-

comparatively easy to implement.

Martingale Discretization

The requirement that discounted asset prices be martingales is central to the
pricing of derivative securities. The martingale property is usually imposed in
a continuous-time model; but as we have noted at several points, it is desirable
to enforce the property on the simulated approximation as well. Doing so
extends the no-arbitrage property to prices computed from a simulation. It
also preserves the internal consistency of a simulated model by ensuring that
prices of underlying assets computed in a simulation coincide with those used
as inputs to the simulation; cf. the discussions in Sections 3.3.2, 3.6.2, 3.7.3,
Example 4.1.3, and Section 4.5.
    If the SDE (6.1) has drift coeпђГcient a identically equal to zero, then the
Euler and higher-order methods in Sections 6.1.1 and 6.2 all produce dis-
cretizations XћВ that are discrete-time martingales. In this sense, the martingale
property is almost trivially preserved by the discretization methods. However,
the variables simulated do not always coincide with the variables to which the
martingale property applies. For example, in pricing пђБxed-income derivatives
we often simulate interest rates but the martingale property applies to dis-
counted bonds and not to the interest rates themselves.
    The simulation method developed for the Heath-Jarrow-Morton frame-
work in Section 3.6.2 may be viewed as a nonstandard Euler scheme вАФ non-
standard because of the modiпђБed drift coeпђГcient. We modiпђБed the drift in
forward rates precisely so that the discretized discounted bond prices would
be martingales. In the LIBOR market models of Section 3.7, we argued that
an analogous drift modiпђБcation is infeasible. Instead, in Section 3.7.3 we dis-
cussed ways of preserving the martingale property through changes of vari-
ables. These methods include discretizing the discounted bond prices directly
or discretizing their diпђАerences.
    Preserving the martingale property is sometimes at odds with preserving
bounds on variables. If X is a positive martingale then the Euler approx-
imation to X is a martingale but not, in general, a positive process. Both
                                                6.6 Concluding Remarks      375

properties can usually be enforced by discretizing log X instead. If X is a
martingale taking values in (0, 1), then deпђБning Y as in (6.56), discretizing
Y , and then inverting the transformation preserves the bounds on X but not
the martingale property. Using the transformation ќ¶вИТ1 , a simple correction
to the drift preserves the martingale property as well as the bounds. As shown
in [149], when applied to the multidimensional constraint (6.57), this method
preserves the constraints and limits departures from the martingale property
to terms that are o(h), with h the simulation time step.


6.6 Concluding Remarks
The benchmark method for reducing discretization error is the combination of
the Euler scheme with two-point extrapolation, as in Section 6.2.4. This tech-
nique is easy to implement and is usually faster than a second-order expansion
for comparable accuracy, especially in high dimensions. The smoothness con-
ditions required on the coeпђГcient functions to ensure the validity of extrapo-
lation are not always satisпђБed in пђБnancial engineering applications, but similar
conditions underlie the theoretical support for second-order approximations.
    Second-order and higher-order schemes do have practical applications, but
they should always be compared with the simpler alternative of an extrapo-
lated Euler approximation. A theoretical comparison is usually diпђГcult, but
the magnitudes of the derivatives of the coeпђГcient functions can provide some
indication, with large derivatives unfavorable for higher-order methods. Ta-
lay and Tubaro [343] analyze speciпђБc models for which they derive explicit
expressions for error terms. They show, for example, that the reпђБnement in
(6.10) can produce larger errors than an Euler approximation.
    Of the many expansions discussed in this chapter, the most useful (in
addition to the extrapolated Euler scheme) are (6.28) for scalar processes and
(6.38) for vector processes.
    For speciпђБc applications in пђБnancial engineering, a technique that uses
information about the problem context is often more eпђАective than a general-
purpose expansion. For example, even a simple logarithmic change of vari-
ables can reduce discretization error, with the added beneпђБt of ensuring that
positive variables stay positive. The methods of Section 6.4 provide another
illustration: they oпђАer simple and eпђАective ways of reducing discretization er-
ror in pricing options sensitive to barrier crossings and extreme values of the
underlying assets. The simulation methods in Sections 3.6 and 3.7 also address
discretization issues for speciпђБc models.
    The literature on discretization methods for stochastic diпђАerential equa-
tions oпђАers many modiпђБcations of the basic schemes discussed in Sections 6.1.1,
6.2, and 6.3.1. Kloeden and Platen [211] provide a comprehensive treatment
of these methods. Another direction of research investigates the law of the
error of a discretization method, as in Jacod and Protter [193].
376    6 Discretization Methods

    This chapter has discussed the use of second-order and higher-order expan-
sions in simulation, but the same techniques are sometimes useful in deriving
approximations without recourse to simulation.
7
Estimating Sensitivities




Previous chapters have addressed various aspects of estimating expectations
with a view toward computing the prices of derivative securities. This chapter
develops methods for estimating sensitivities of expectations, in particular
the derivatives of derivative prices commonly referred to as вАЬGreeks.вАЭ From
the discussion in Section 1.2.1, we know that in an idealized setting of con-
tinuous trading in a complete market, the payoпђА of a contingent claim can
be manufactured (or hedged) through trading in underlying assets. The risk
in a short position in an option, for example, is oпђАset by a delta-hedging
strategy of holding delta units of each underlying asset, where delta is simply
the partial derivative of the option price with respect to the current price of
that underlying asset. Implementation of the strategy requires knowledge of
these price sensitivities; sensitivities with respect to other parameters are also
widely used to measure and manage risk. Whereas the prices themselves can
often be observed in the market, their sensitivites cannot, so accurate calcula-
tion of sensitivities is arguably even more important than calculation of prices.
We will see, however, that derivative estimation presents both theoretical and
practical challenges to Monte Carlo simulation.
    The methods for estimating sensitivities discussed in this chapter fall into
two broad categories: methods that involve simulating at two or more values
of the parameter of diпђАerentiation and methods that do not. The пђБrst cate-
gory вАФ пђБnite-diпђАerence approximations вАФ are at least superпђБcially easier to
understand and implement; but because they produce biased estimates their
use requires balancing bias and variance. Methods in the second category,
when applicable, produce unbiased estimates. They accomplish this by using
information about the simulated stochastic process to replace numerical dif-
ferentiation with exact calculations. The pathwise method diпђАerentiates each
simulated outcome with respect to the parameter of interest; the likelihood
ratio method diпђАerentiates a probability density rather than an outcome.
378      7 Estimating Sensitivities

7.1 Finite-DiпђАerence Approximations
Consider a model that depends on a parameter ќЄ ranging over some interval
of the real line. Suppose that for each value of ќЄ we have a mechanism for
generating a random variable Y (ќЄ), representing the output of the model at
parameter ќЄ. Let
                               ќ±(ќЄ) = E[Y (ќЄ)].
The derivative estimation problem consists of пђБnding a way to estimate ќ± (ќЄ),
the derivative of ќ± with respect to ќЄ.
    In the application to option pricing, Y (ќЄ) is the discounted payoпђА of an
option, ќ±(ќЄ) is its price, and ќЄ could be any of the many model or market
parameters that inпђВuence the price. When ќЄ is the initial price of an underlying
asset, then ќ± (ќЄ) is the optionвАЩs delta (with respect to that asset). The second
derivative ќ± (ќЄ) is the optionвАЩs gamma. When ќЄ is a volatility parameter,
ќ± (ќЄ) is often called вАЬvega.вАЭ For interest rate derivatives, the sensitivities of
prices to the initial term structure (as represented by, e.g., a yield curve or
forward curve) are important.

7.1.1 Bias and Variance

An obvious approach to derivative estimation proceeds as follows. Simulate
independent replications Y1 (ќЄ), . . . , Yn (ќЄ) of the model at parameter ќЄ and
n additional replications Y1 (ќЄ + h), . . . , Yn (ќЄ + h) at ќЄ + h, for some h > 0.
Average each set of replications to get YћДn (ќЄ) and YћДn (ќЄ + h) and form the
forward-diпђАerence estimator

                           ЋЖ F (n, h) = YћДn (ќЄ + h) вИТ YћДn (ќЄ) .

    The superior accuracy of a central-diпђАerence approximation compared
with a forward-diпђАerence approximation is illustrated in Figure 7.1. The curve
in the пђБgure plots the Black-Scholes formula against the price of the underly-
ing asset with volatility 0.30, an interest rate of 5%, a strike price of 100, and
0.04 years (about two weeks) to expiration. The пђБgure compares the tangent
line at 95 with a forward diпђАerence calculated from prices at 95 and 100 and a
central diпђАerence using prices at 90 and 100. The slope of the central-diпђАerence
line is clearly much closer to that of the tangent line.
    In numerical diпђАerentiation of functions evaluated through deterministic
algorithms, rounding errors resulting from small values of h limit the accuracy
of пђБnite-diпђАerence approximations. (See, for example, the discussion in Sec-
tion 5.7 of Press et al. [299].) In applications of Monte Carlo, the variability in
estimates of function values usually prevents us from taking very small values
of h. So while it is advisable to be aware of possible round-oпђА errors, this is
seldom the main obstacle to accurate estimation of derivatives in simulation.

Variance

The form of the bias of the forward- and central-diпђАerence estimators would
lead us to take ever smaller values of h to improve accuracy, at least if we
ignore the limits of machine precision. But the eпђАect of h on bias must be
weighed against its eпђАect on variance.
    The variance of the forward-diпђАerence estimator (7.1) is

numbers, the output Y (¬Ј) is continuous in the input ќЄ. We will have much
more to say about Case (iii) in Section 7.2.2.

7.1.2 Optimal Mean Square Error

Because decreasing h can increase variance while decreasing bias, minimiz-
ing mean square error (MSE) requires balancing these two considerations.
Increasing the number of replications n decreases variance with no eпђАect on
bias, whereas h aпђАects both bias and variance; our objective is to пђБnd the op-
timal relation between the two. This tradeoпђА is analyzed by Glynn [153], Fox
and Glynn [128], and Zazanis [358], and in early work by Frolov and Chentsov
[130].

with decreasing h. The digital option пђБts in Case (ii) so its relative error
explodes as h approaches zero. From the пђБgure we see that the relative error
for the digital option is minimized at a surprisingly large value of about 4;
the пђБgure also shows that the cost of taking h too large is much smaller than
the cost of taking it too small.

Extrapolation
For a smooth function ќ±(ќЄ) = E[Y (ќЄ)], the bias in пђБnite-diпђАerence estimates
can be reduced through extrapolation, much as in Section 6.2.4. This tech-
nique applies to all the пђБnite-diпђАerence estimators considered above; we illus-
trate it in the case of вИЖЋЖ C,ii , the central-diпђАerence estimator using common
random numbers.
    A Taylor expansion of ќ±(ќЄ) shows that
             ќ±(ќЄ + h) вИТ ќ±(ќЄ вИТ h)          1
                                 = ќ± (ќЄ) + ќ± (ќЄ)h2 + O(h4 );
                      2h                  6
odd powers of h are eliminated by the symmetry of the central-diпђАerence
estimator. Similarly,
             ќ±(ќЄ + 2h) вИТ ќ±(ќЄ вИТ 2h)                2
                                       = ќ± (ќЄ) + ќ± (ќЄ)h2 + O(h4 ).
                       4h                         3
It follows that the bias in the combined estimator
                          4ЋЖ               1ЋЖ
                            вИЖC,ii (n, h) вИТ вИЖ  C,ii (n, 2h)
                          3                3
is O(h4 ). Accordingly, the RMSE of this estimator is O(nвИТ4/9 ) if h is taken
to be O(nвИТ1/9 ). The ninth root of n varies little over practical sample sizes n,
so this estimator achieves a convergence rate of nearly nвИТ1/2 with hn nearly
constant.

Second Derivatives
The analysis leading to Table 7.1 extends, with evident modiпђБcations, to пђБnite-
diпђАerence estimators of second derivatives. Consider a central-diпђАerence esti-
mator of the form
                                               7.1 Finite-DiпђАerence Approximations                    385

                             YћДn (ќЄ + h) вИТ 2YћДn (ќЄ) + YћДn (ќЄ вИТ h)
                                                                  .                                 (7.12)
                                             h2
Its expectation is
                   ќ±(ќЄ + h) вИТ 2ќ±(ќЄ) + ќ±(ќЄ вИТ h)
                                               = ќ± (ќЄ) + O(h2 ),
                               h2
if ќ± is four times diпђАerentiable. The bias is then O(h2 ).
    If values at ќЄ and ќЄ ¬± h are simulated independently of each other, the
numerator in (7.12) has variance that is O(1) in h, so the variance of the
ratio is O(hвИТ4 ). By using common random numbers, we can often reduce
the variance of the numerator to O(h), but even in this case the estimator
variance is O(hвИТ3 ). With hn chosen optimally, this results in a covergence rate
of O(nвИТ2/7 ) for the RMSE. This makes precise the idea that estimating second
derivatives is fundamentally more diпђГcult than estimating пђБrst derivatives.
    For problems in which Case (iii) of (7.8) applies вАФ the most favorable case
for derivative estimation вАФ the estimator in (7.12) often has variance O(hвИТ1 ).
Because of the nondiпђАerentiability of option payoпђАs, there are no interesting
examples in which the variance of the numerator of (7.12) is smaller than
O(h3 ), and there are plenty of examples in which it is O(h).

Multiple Parameters

Suppose now that ќЄ is a vector of parameters (ќЄ1 , . . . , ќЄm ) and that we need
to estimate the sensitivity of ќ±(ќЄ) = E[Y (ќЄ)] to each of these parameters. A
straightforward approach selects an increment hi for each ќЄi and estimates
вИВќ±/вИВќЄi using
         1                                                                                    
            YћДn (ќЄ1 , . . . , ќЄi + hi , . . . , ќЄm ) вИТ YћДn (ќЄ1 , . . . , ќЄi вИТ hi , . . . , ќЄm ) ,
        2hi
or the corresponding forward-diпђАerence estimator. In addition to the prob-
lem of selecting an appropriate hi , this setting poses a further computational
challenge. It requires estimation of ќ±(ќЄ) at 2m + 1 values of ќЄ using central
diпђАerences and m + 1 values using forward diпђАerences. This diпђГculty becomes
even more severe for second derivatives. Finite-diпђАerence estimation of all sec-
ond derivatives вИВ 2 ќ±/вИВќЄi вИВќЄj requires simulation at O(m2 ) parameter values.
This may be onerous if m is large. In pricing interest rate derivatives, for ex-
ample, we may be interested in sensitivities with respect to all initial forward
rates or bond prices, in which case m could easily be 20 or more.
   Techniques from the design of experiments and response surface method-
ology are potentially useful in reducing the number of parameter values at
which one simulates. For this formulation, let вИЖY denote the change in Y
resulting from incrementing each ќЄi by hi , i = 1, . . . , m. The problem of esti-
mating sensitivities can be viewed as one of пђБtting a пђБrst-order model of the
form
386    7 Estimating Sensitivities

                                        
                                        m
                               вИЖY =            ќ≤i h i +                   (7.13)
                                        i=1

or a second-order model of the form
                            
                            m                
                                             m 
                                               m
                     вИЖY =         ќ≤i h i +             ќ≤ij hi hj + .      (7.14)
                            i=1              i=1 j=i

Each ќ≤i approximates the partial derivative of ќ± with respect to ќЄi , 2ќ≤ii ap-
proximates вИВ 2 ќ±/вИВќЄi2 , and each ќ≤ij , j = i, approximates вИВ 2 ќ±/вИВќЄi вИВќЄj . In both
cases,  represents a residual error.
    Given observations of Y at various values of ќЄ, the coeпђГcients ќ≤i , ќ≤ij can
be estimated using, e.g., least squares or weighted least squares. Response
surface methodology (as in, e.g., Khuri and Cornell [210]) provides guidance
on choosing the values of ќЄ at which to measure (i.e., simulate) Y .
    In the terminology of experimental design, simulating at all 2m points
deпђБned by adding or subtracting hi to each ќЄi is a full factorial design. Simu-
lating at fewer points may add bias to estimates of some coeпђГcients. But by
reducing the number of points at which we need to simulate, we can increase
the number of replications at each point and reduce variance. A design with
fewer points thus oпђАers a tradeoпђА between bias and variance.
    Most of the literature on response surface methodology assumes indepen-
dent residuals across observations. In simulation, we have the пђВexibility to
introduce dependence between observations through the assignment of the
output of the random number generator. Schruben and Margolin [323] ana-
lyze the design of simulation experiments in which the same random numbers
are used at multiple parameter values. They recommend a combination of
common random numbers and antithetic sampling across parameter values.


7.2 Pathwise Derivative Estimates
This section and the next develop alternatives to пђБnite-diпђАerence methods
that estimate derivatives directly, without simulating at multiple parameter
values. They do so by taking advantage of additional information about the
dynamics and parameter dependence of a simulated process.

7.2.1 Method and Examples

In our discussion of optimal mean square error in Section 7.1, we noted that
in Case (iii) of (7.8) the MSE decreases with the parameter increment h. This
suggests that we should let h decrease to zero and estimate the derivative of
ќ±(ќЄ) = E[Y (ќЄ)] using

                                        Y (ќЄ + h) вИТ Y (ќЄ)
                        Y (ќЄ) = lim                       .                (7.15)
                                    hвЖТ0         h
                                        7.2 Pathwise Derivative Estimates      387

This estimator has expectation E[Y (ќЄ)]. It is an unbiased estimator of ќ± (ќЄ)
if                                 
                             d           d
                        E      Y (ќЄ) =     E[Y (ќЄ)];                   (7.16)
                            dќЄ          dќЄ
i.e., if the interchange of diпђАerentiation and expectation is justiпђБed.
     Even before discussing the validity of (7.16), we need to clarify what we
mean by (7.15). Up until this point, we have not been explicit about what
dependence, if any, the random variable Y has on the parameter ќЄ. In our
discussion of пђБnite-diпђАerence estimators, the notation Y (ќЄ) indicated an out-
come of Y simulated at parameter value ќЄ, but did not entail a functional
relation between the two. Indeed, we noted that the relation between Y (ќЄ)
and Y (ќЄ +h) could depend on whether the two outcomes were simulated using
common random numbers.
     To make (7.15) precise, we need a collection of random variables {Y (ќЄ), ќЄ вИИ
ќШ} deпђБned on a single probability space (вД¶, F , P ). In other words, Y (ќЄ) is a
stochastic process indexed by ќЄ вИИ ќШ. Take ќШ вКЖ  to be an interval. We can
пђБx ѕЙ вИИ вД¶ and think of the mapping ќЄ вЖТ Y (ќЄ, ѕЙ) as a random function on
ќШ. We can then interpret Y (ќЄ) = Y (ќЄ, ѕЙ) as the derivative of the random
function with respect to ќЄ with ѕЙ held пђБxed. In (7.15), we implicitly assume
that the derivative exists with probability one, and when this holds, we call
Y (ќЄ) the pathwise derivative of Y at ќЄ.
     As a practical matter, we usually think of each ѕЙ as a realization of the
output of an ideal random number generator. Each Y (ќЄ, ѕЙ) is then the output
of a simulation algorithm at parameter ќЄ with random number stream ѕЙ. Each
Y (ќЄ, ѕЙ) is the derivative of the simulation output with respect to ќЄ with the
random numbers held пђБxed. The value of this derivative depends, in part, on
how we implement a simulation algorithm.
     We will see through examples that the existence with probability 1 of
the pathwise derivative Y (ќЄ) at each ќЄ typically holds. This is not to say
that, with probability 1, the mapping ќЄ вЖТ Y (ќЄ) is a diпђАerentiable function
on ќШ. The distinction lies in the order of quantiпђБcation: the exceptional set
of probability 0 on which (7.15) fails to exist can and often does depend on
ќЄ. The union of these exceptional sets for ќЄ ranging over ќШ may well have
positive probability.
     There is a large literature on pathwise derivative estimation in the discrete-
event simulation literature, where it is usually called inпђБnitesimal perturbation
analysis. This line of work stems primarily from Ho and Cao [186] and Suri and
Zazanis [339]; a general framework is developed in Glasserman [138]. Broadie
and Glasserman [64] apply the method to option pricing and we use some
of their examples here. Chen and Fu [80] develop applications to mortgage-
backed securities.
     To illustrate the derivation and scope of pathwise derivative estimators,
we now consider some examples.
388    7 Estimating Sensitivities

Example 7.2.1 Black-Scholes delta. The derivative of the Black-Scholes for-

the Black-Scholes delta does not require simulation, but nevertheless provides
a useful example through which to introduce the pathwise method.
   Let

    This example illustrates a general point about diпђАerentiability made at the
beginning of this section. Consider the discounted payoпђА Y as a function of
S(0). If we пђБx any S(0) > 0, then Y is diпђАerentiable at S(0) with probability
1, because for each S(0) the event {S(T ) = K} has probability 1. However,
the probability that Y is a diпђАerentiable function of S(0) throughout (0, вИЮ) is
zero: for each value of Z, there is some S(0) at which Y fails to be diпђАerentiable
вАФ namely, the S(0) that makes S(T ) = K for the given value of Z.

Example 7.2.6 Digital options and gamma. Consider a digital option with
discounted payoпђА
                       Y = eвИТrT 1{S(T ) > K},
and for concreteness let S be modeled by geometric Brownian motion. Viewed
as a function of S(T ), the discounted payoпђА Y is diпђАerentiable except at
S(T ) = K, which implies that Y is diпђАerentiable with probability 1. But
because Y is piecewise constant in S(T ), this derivative is 0 wherever it exists,
and the same is true of Y viewed as a function of S(0). Thus,
                                      
                                  dY          d
                         0=E             =        E[Y ].
                                 dS(0)     dS(0)
This is an example in which the pathwise derivative exists with probability
1 but is entirely uninformative. The change in E[Y ] with a change in S(0)
is driven by the possibility that a change in S(0) will cause S(T ) to cross
the strike K, but this possibility is missed by the pathwise derivative. The
pathwise derivative sees only the local insensitivity of Y to S(0). This also
explains the qualitatively diпђАerent behavior as h вЖТ 0 for the standard call
and the digital option in Figure 7.2.
                                       7.2 Pathwise Derivative Estimates     393

    For much the same reason, the pathwise method is generally inapplicable to
barrier options. On a пђБxed path, a suпђГciently small change in the underlying
asset will neither create nor eliminate a barrier crossing, so the eпђАect of the
barrier is missed by the pathwise derivative.
    The example of a digital option also indicates that the pathwise method,
at least in its simplest form, is generally inapplicable to estimating second
derivatives. If, for example, Y is the discounted payoпђА of an ordinary call op-
tion, then its пђБrst derivative (in S(T )) has exactly the form of a digital payoпђА
вАФ see (7.19). The pathwise method faces the same diпђГculty in estimating the
gamma of a standard call option as it does in estimating the delta of a digital
option. 2

7.2.2 Conditions for Unbiasedness

Example 7.2.6 points to a limitation in the scope of the pathwise method: it
is generally inapplicable to discontinuous payoпђАs. The possible failure of the
interchange of derivative and expectation in (7.16) is a practical as well as
theoretical issue. We therefore turn now to a discussion of conditions ensuring
the validity of (7.16) and thus the unbiasedness of the pathwise method.
    We continue to use ќЄ to denote a generic parameter and assume the ex-
istence of a random function {Y (ќЄ), ќЄ вИИ ќШ} with ќШ an interval of the real
line. As explained at the beginning of Section 7.2.1, this random function
represents the output of a simulation algorithm as a function of ќЄ with the
simulationвАЩs random numbers held пђБxed. We consider settings in which the
derivative Y (ќЄ) exists with probability 1 at each ќЄ вИИ ќШ. As the examples of
Section 7.2.1 should make evident, this is by no means restrictive.
    Given the existence of Y (ќЄ), the most important question is the validity
of (7.16), which is a matter of interchanging a limit and an expectation to
ensure                                                           
                    Y (ќЄ + h) вИТ Y (ќЄ)             Y (ќЄ + h) вИТ Y (ќЄ)
            E lim                       = lim E                       .
                hвЖТ0         h             hвЖТ0             h
A necessary and suпђГcient condition for this is uniform integrability (see Ap-
pendix A) of the diпђАerence quotients hвИТ1 [Y (ќЄ + h) вИТ Y (ќЄ)]. Our objective is
to provide suпђГcient conditions that are more readily veriпђБed in practice.
    Inspection of the examples in Section 7.2.1 reveals a pattern in the deriva-
tion of the estimators: we apply the chain rule to express Y (ќЄ) as a product
of terms, the пђБrst relating the discounted payoпђА to the path of the underlying
asset, the second relating the path to the parameter. Because this is a natural
and convenient way to derive estimators, we formulate conditions to пђБt this
approach, following Broadie and Glasserman [64].
    We restrict attention to discounted payoпђАs that depend on the value of
an underlying asset or assets at a пђБnite number of пђБxed dates. Rather than
distinguish between multiple assets and values of a single asset at multiple
dates, we simply suppose that the discounted payoпђА is a function of a random
394     7 Estimating Sensitivities

vector X(ќЄ) = (X1 (ќЄ), . . . , Xm (ќЄ)), which is itself a function of the parameter
ќЄ. Thus,
                          Y (ќЄ) = f (X1 (ќЄ), . . . , Xm (ќЄ)),
for some function f : m вЖТ  depending on the speciпђБc derivative security.
We require
(A1) At each ќЄ вИИ ќШ, Xi (ќЄ) exists with probability 1, for all i = 1, . . . , m.
     If f is diпђАerentiable, then Y inherits diпђАerentiability from X under condi-
tion (A1). As illustrated in the examples of Section 7.2.1, option payoпђАs often
fail to be diпђАerentiable, but the points at which diпђАerentiability fails can often
be ignored because they occur with probability 0. To make this precise, we
let Df вКЖ m denote the set of points at which f is diпђАerentiable and require
(A2) P (X(ќЄ) вИИ Df ) = 1 for all ќЄ вИИ ќШ.
This then implies that Y (ќЄ) exists with probability 1 and is given by

                                     
                                     m
                                       вИВf
                           Y (ќЄ) =               (X(ќЄ))Xi (ќЄ).
                                     i=1
                                           вИВxi

    Comparison of Examples 7.2.1 and 7.2.6 indicates that even if points of
nondiпђАerentiability of f occur with probability 0, the behavior of f at these
points is important. For both the standard call and the digital option, diпђАer-
entiability fails on the zero-probability event {S(T ) = K}; but whereas the
payoпђА of the call is continuous as S(T ) crosses the strike, the payoпђА of the
digital option is not. This distinction leads to an unbiased estimator in the
пђБrst case and not the second. In fact we need a bit more than continuity; a
convenient condition is
(A3) There exists a constant kf such that for all x, y вИИ m ,

                                |f (x) вИТ f (y)| вЙ§ kf x вИТ y;

       i.e., f is Lipschitz.
   The standard call, Asian option, lookback, spread option, and max option
in Section 7.2.1 all satisfy this condition (as does any composition of linear
transformations and the functions min and max); the digital payoпђА does not.
We impose a related condition on X(ќЄ):
(A4) There exist random variables ќЇi , i = 1, . . . , m, such that for all ќЄ1 , ќЄ2 вИИ
     ќШ,
                       |Xi (ќЄ2 ) вИТ Xi (ќЄ1 )| вЙ§ ќЇi |ќЄ2 вИТ ќЄ1 |,
       and E[ќЇi ] < вИЮ, i = 1, . . . , m.
                                           7.2 Pathwise Derivative Estimates     395

    Conditions (A3) and (A4) together imply that Y is almost surely Lipschitz
in ќЄ because the Lipschitz property is preserved by composition. Thus,

and for ќЇY we may take

we can now apply the dominated convergence theorem to interchange expec-

derivative is an unbiased estimator.
    Condition (A4) is satisпђБed in all the examples of Section 7.2.1, at least if

on the parameter is linear as in, e.g., the mapping from S(0) to S(T ) for
geometric Brownian motion. In the case of the mapping from ѕГ to S(T ), (A4)
holds if we take ќШ to be a bounded interval; this is harmless because for the
purposes of estimating a derivative at ѕГ we may take an arbitrarily small
neighborhood of ѕГ. In Example 7.2.5, for the mapping from X(0) to X(t) to
be Lipschitz, we need to restrict X(0) to a set of values bounded away from
zero.

                        E[(Y (ќЄ + h) вИТ Y (ќЄ))2 ] вЙ§ E[ќЇ2Y ]h2 ,

from which it follows that Var[Y (ќЄ + h) вИТ Y (ќЄ)] = O(h2 ), as in Case (iii) of

E[Y (ќЄ)] is diпђАerentiable, then

E[(Y (ќЄ + h) вИТ Y (ќЄ))2 ] = Var[Y (ќЄ + h) вИТ Y (ќЄ)] + (E[Y (ќЄ + h) вИТ Y (ќЄ)])2 = O(h2 ).

Hence,                                                        
                                                           2

                                            h
remains bounded as h вЖТ 0 and [Y (ќЄ + h) вИТ Y (ќЄ)]/h is uniformly integrable.
Thus, the scope of the pathwise method is essentially the same as the scope
of Case (iii) in (7.8).
   Of conditions (A1)вАУ(A4), the one that poses a practical limitation is (A3).
As we noted previously, the payoпђАs of digital options and barrier options are
396    7 Estimating Sensitivities

not even continuous. Indeed, discontinuities in a payoпђА are the main obstacle
to the applicability of the pathwise method. A simple rule of thumb states that
the pathwise method applies when the payoпђА is continuous in the parameter
of interest. Though this is not a precise guarantee (of the type provided by
the conditions above), it provides sound guidance in most practical problems.



7.2.3 Approximations and Related Methods

This section develops further topics in sensitivity estimation using pathwise
derivatives. Through various approximations and extensions, we can improve
the eпђГciency or expand the scope of the method.

General DiпђАusion Processes

Consider a process X described by a stochastic diпђАerential equation

                     dX(t) = a(X(t)) dt + b(X(t)) dW (t)                  (7.23)

with пђБxed initial condition X(0). Suppose, for now, that this is a scalar process
driven by a scalar Brownian motion. We might simulate the process using an
Euler scheme with step size h; using the notation of Chapter 6, we write the
discretization as
                                               вИЪ
       XћВ(i + 1) = XћВ(i) + a(XћВ(i))h + b(XћВ(i)) hZi+1 , XћВ(0) = X(0),

with XћВ(i) denoting the discretized approximation to X(ih) and Z1 , Z2 , . . .
denoting independent N (0, 1) random variables.
   Let
                               ЋЖ      dXћВ(i)

                                         7.2 Pathwise Derivative Estimates     397

    Under relatively minor conditions on the coeпђГcient functions a and b, the

      dвИЖ(t) = a (X(t))вИЖ(t) dt + b (X(t))вИЖ(t) dW (t),             вИЖ(0) = 1.   (7.25)

See Section 4.7 of Kunita [217] or Section 5.7 of Protter [300] for precise
results of this type. It follows that (7.24) вАФ derived by diпђАerentiating the

to derivatives of X(t) with respect to parameters of the coeпђГcient functions
a and b.
    In light of these results, there is no real theoretical obstacle to developing
pathwise estimators for general diпђАusions; through (7.25), the problem reduces
to one of discretizing stochastic diпђАerential equations. There may, however, be
practical obstacles to this approach. The additional eпђАort required to simulate
(7.25) may be roughly the same as the eпђАort required to simulate a second
path of X from a diпђАerent initial state. (Contrast this with examples in Sec-
tion 7.2.1 for which dS(T )/dS(0) reduces to S(T )/S(0) and is thus available
at no additional cost.) Also, in the case of a d-dimensional diпђАusion X, we
need to replace (7.25) with a d √Ч d system of equations for
                                  вИВXi (t)
                      вИЖij (t) =           ,   i, j = 1, . . . , d.
                                  вИВXj (0)
Simulating a system of SDEs for this matrix of derivatives may be quite time-
consuming.
    These considerations motivate the use of approximations in simulating the
derivatives of X(t). One strategy is to use coarser time steps in simulating the
derivatives than in simulating X. There are many ways of implementing this
strategy; we discuss just one and for simplicity we consider only the scalar
case.
    Freezing a (X(t)) and b (X(t)) at their time-zero values transforms (7.24)
into                                                     вИЪ
              вИЖ(i) = ЋЖ
               ЋЖ      вИЖ(i вИТ 1) 1 + a (X(0))h + b (X(0)) hZi .
This is potentially much faster to simulate than (7.24), especially if evaluation
of a and b is time-consuming. To improve the accuracy of the approximation,
we might update the coeпђГcients after every k steps. In this case, we would
use the coeпђГciencts a (X(0)) and b (X(0)) for i = 1, . . . , k, then use
                                                           вИЪ
              вИЖ(i) = ЋЖ
              ЋЖ      вИЖ(i вИТ 1) 1 + a (XћВ(k))h + b (XћВ(k)) hZi

for i = k + 1, . . . , 2k, and so on.
    If, for purposes of diпђАerentiation, we entirely ignored the dependence of
a and b on the current state X, we would get simply вИЖ(i) ЋЖ    вЙ° 1. For S(t) =
S(0) exp(X(t)), this is equivalent to making the approximation dS(t)/dS(0) вЙИ
S(t)/S(0), which we know is exact in several cases.

Smoothing

We noted in Section 7.2.2 that discontinuities in a discounted payoпђА generally
make the pathwise method inapplicable. In some cases, this obstacle can be
overcome by smoothing discontinuities through conditional expectations. We
illustrate this idea with two examples.
    Our пђБrst example is the discounted digital payoпђА Y = eвИТrT 1{S(T ) > K},
which also arises in the derivative of a standard call option; see Example 7.2.6.
If S вИЉ GBM(¬µ, ѕГ 2 ) and we пђБx 0 <  < T , then

                                     log(S(T вИТ )/K) + (¬µ вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 )
         E[Y |S(T вИТ )] = eвИТrT ќ¶                  вИЪ                        .
                                                 ѕГ 
DiпђАerentiation yields

    dE[Y |S(T вИТ )]      eвИТrT             log(S(T вИТ )/K) + (¬µ вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 )
                    =          вИЪ ѕЖ                     вИЪ                        ,
      dS(T вИТ )       S(T вИТ )ѕГ                      ѕГ 
                                                                               (7.29)
400     7 Estimating Sensitivities

and then, through the chain rule, the unbiased delta estimator

        dE[Y |S(T вИТ )]    eвИТrT              log(S(T вИТ )/K) + (¬µ вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 )
                        =      вИЪ ѕЖ                        вИЪ                        .
            dS(0)         S(0)ѕГ                         ѕГ 

    This calculation illustrates that by conditioning on the underlying asset 
time units before expiration, we can smooth the discontinuity in the digital
payoпђА and then, by diпђАerentiating, arrive at an unbiased derivative estimator.
Of course, the same argument allows exact calculation of the derivative of
E[Y ] in this example. The value of this derivation lies not so much in pro-
viding an exact expression for geometric Brownian motion as in providing an
approximation for more general processes. Equation (7.29) could, for exam-
ple, be applied to a stochastic volatility model by further conditioning on the
volatility at T вИТ  and using this value in place of ѕГ. This expression should
then be multiplied by an exact or approximate expression for dS(T вИТ)/dS(0).
If the underlying asset is simulated using a log-Euler scheme with time step
, then (7.29) does not introduce any error beyond that already inherent to
the discretization method.
    Our second example of smoothing applies to barrier options. We again
introduce it through geometric Brownian motion though we intend it as an
approximation for more general processes. Consider a discretely monitored
knock-out option with discounted payoпђА

Expressions of this form are derived in Glasserman and Staum [146] through
the observation that switching from S to SћГ can be formulated as a change of
measure and that the product of the survival probabilities is the appropriate
likelihood ratio for this transformation. Because the conditioned process never
crosses the barrier, we can omit the indicator from the expression on the right.
The product of the survival probabilities smooths the indicator.
    We could now diпђАerentiate with respect to S(0) inside the expectation
on the right side of (7.30) to get an estimator of the barrier option delta.
Though straightforward in principle, this is rather involved in practice. The
price we pay for switching from S to SћГ is the loss of linearity in S(0). Each
Zi depends on SћГ(tiвИТ1 ) and thus contributes a term when we diпђАerentiate
SћГ(ti ). The practical value of this approach is therefore questionable, but it
nevertheless serves to illustrate the пђВexibility we have to modify a problem
before diпђАerentiating.


7.3 The Likelihood Ratio Method
As explained in several places in Section 7.2, the scope of the pathwise method
is limited primarily by the requirement of continuity in the discounted payoпђА
as a function of the parameter of diпђАerentiation. The likelihood ratio method
provides an alternative approach to derivative estimation requiring no smooth-
ness at all in the discounted payoпђА and thus complementing the pathwise
method. It accomplishes this by diпђАerentiating probabilities rather than pay-
oпђАs.

7.3.1 Method and Examples

As in Section 7.2.2, we consider a discounted payoпђА Y expressed as a function f
of a random vector X = (X1 , . . . , Xm ). The components of X could represent
diпђАerent underlying assets or values of a single asset at multiple dates. In our
discussion of the pathwise method, we assumed the existence of a functional
dependence of X (and then Y ) on a parameter ќЄ. In the likelihood ratio
method, we instead suppose that X has a probability density g and that ќЄ
is a parameter of this density. We therefore write gќЄ for the density, and to
emphasize that an expectation is computed with respect to gќЄ , we sometimes
write it as EќЄ .
    In this formulation, the expected discounted payoпђА is given by
402    7 Estimating Sensitivities
                                                       
               EќЄ [Y ] = EќЄ [f (X1 , . . . , Xm )] =            f (x)gќЄ (x) dx.
                                                           m

To derive a derivative estimator, we suppose that the order of diпђАerentiation
and integration can be interchanged to get
                                    
                        d                    d
                          EќЄ [Y ] =     f (x) gќЄ (x) dx.               (7.31)
                       dќЄ            m      dќЄ

If this indeed holds, then multiplying and dividing the integrand by gќЄ yields
                                                                      
             d                     gћЗќЄ (x)                      gћЗќЄ (X)
               EќЄ [Y ] =     f (x)         gќЄ (x) dx = EќЄ f (X)           ,
            dќЄ            m       gќЄ (x)                       gќЄ (X)

where we have written gћЗќЄ for dgќЄ /dќЄ. It now follows from this equation that
the expression
                                       gћЗќЄ (X)
                                f (X)                                  (7.32)
                                       gќЄ (X)
is an unbiased estimator of the derivative of EќЄ [Y ]. This is a likelihood ratio
method (LRM) estimator.
    Three issues merit comment:
вЧ¶ As with the pathwise method, the validity of this approach relies on an
  interchange of diпђАerentation and integration. In practice, however, the in-
  terchange in (7.31) is relatively benign in comparison to (7.16), because
  probability densities are typically smooth functions of their parameters but
  option payoпђАs are not. Whereas the interchange (7.16) imposes practical
  limits on the use of the pathwise method, the validity of (7.31) is seldom
  an obstacle to the use of the likelihood ratio method.
вЧ¶ Whether we view ќЄ as a parameter of the path X or of its density is largely a
  matter a choice. Suppose, for example, that X is a normal random variable
  with distribution N (ќЄ, 1) and Y = f (X) for some function f . The density
  of X is gќЄ (x) = ѕЖ(x вИТ ќЄ) with ѕЖ the standard normal density. By following
  the steps above, we arrive at the estimator

                                  ѕЖ (X вИТ ќЄ)
                         вИТf (X)             = f (X)(X вИТ ќЄ)
                                  ѕЖ(X вИТ ќЄ)

  for the derivative of EќЄ [Y ]. But we could also write X(ќЄ) = ќЄ + Z with
  Z вИЉ N (0, 1) and apply the pathwise method to get the estimator

                                               dX
                             Y (ќЄ) = f (X)        = f (X).
                                               dќЄ
  This illustrates the пђВexibility we have in representing the dependence on
  a parameter through the path X or its density (or both); this пђВexibility
  should be exploited in developing derivative estimators.
                                         7.3 The Likelihood Ratio Method      403

вЧ¶ In the statistical literature, an expression of the form gћЗќЄ /gќЄ , often written
  d log gќЄ /dќЄ, is called a score function. In the estimator (7.32), the score
  function is evaluated at the outcome X. We refer to the random variable
  gћЗќЄ (X)/gќЄ (X) as the score. This term is short for вАЬthe diпђАerentiated log den-
  sity evaluated at the simulated outcomeвАЭ and also for вАЬthe expression that
  multiplies the discounted payoпђА in a likelihood ratio method estimator.вАЭ
   We illustrate the likelihood ratio method through examples. This approach
has been developed primarily in the discrete-event simulation literature; im-
portant early references include Glynn [152], Reiman and Weiss [305], and
Rubinstein [311]. It is sometimes called the score function method, as in Ru-
binstein and Shapiro [313]. Broadie and Glasserman [64] and Glasserman and
Zhao [150] develop applications in пђБnance, and we include some of their ex-
amples here.
Example 7.3.1 Black-Scholes delta. To estimate the Black-Scholes delta us-
ing the likelihood ratio method, we need to view S(0) as a parameter of the
density of S(T ). Using (3.23), we пђБnd that the lognormal density of S(T ) is
given by

                 1                       log(x/S(0)) вИТ (r вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 )T
      g(x) =     вИЪ ѕЖ(ќґ(x)),     ќґ(x) =              вИЪ                 ,    (7.33)
               xѕГ T                                ѕГ T
with ѕЖ the standard normal density. Some algebra now shows that

          dg(x)/dS(0)         dќґ(x)   log(x/S(0)) вИТ (r вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 )T
                      = вИТќґ(x)       =                              .
              g(x)            dS(0)           S(0)ѕГ 2 T
We get the score by evaluating this expression at S(T ) and an unbiased esti-
mator of delta by multiplying by the discounted payoпђА of the option:
                                  log(S(T )/S(0)) вИТ (r вИТ 12 ѕГ 2 )T
            eвИТrT (S(T ) вИТ K)+                                         .

Example 7.3.5 Square-root diпђАusion. We use the notation of Example 7.2.5.

a score function by diпђАerentiating the log density (available from (3.67)) with
respect to the noncentrality parameter. But the noncentral chi-square density
is cumbersome and its score function still more cumbersome, so this is not a
practical solution.
    An alternative uses the decomposition (7.22). Any function of X(t) can be
represented as a function of a normal random variable with mean c2 X(0)
and an independent chi-square random variable. The expected value of the
function is then an integral with respect to the bivariate density of these two
random variables. Because the variables are independent, their joint density is
just the product of their marginal normal and chi-square densities. Only the
пђБrst of these densities depends on X(0), and X(0) enters through the mean.
