n вЖТ вИЮ.
    Our main interest in measure transformations lies in changes of measure
that have the eпђАect of adding a drift to a Brownian motion. This may be
viewed as an extension of (B.25) and (B.26). In discussing measure trans-
formations for continuous-time processes we restrict ourselves to пђБnite time
intervals, just as the transformation in (B.25) is feasible only for пђБnite n.

GirsanovвАЩs Theorem

We now generalize the basic transformation in (B.24). For the пђБltration {Ft }
of (вД¶, F , P ), let Pt denote the restriction of P to Ft . Let {X(t), t вИИ [0, T ]}
be a nonnegative martingale with respect to {Ft } and suppose E[X(T )] = 1.
DeпђБne a probability measure Qt on Ft by setting

                  Qt (A) = EP [1A X(t)] = EPt [1A X(t)],            A вИИ Ft ;

i.e., for each t вИИ [0, T ],
                                        dQt
                                            = X(t).
                                        dPt
Then Qt is the restriction of QT to Ft because for any A вИИ Ft ,

    QT (A) = EP [1A X(T )] = EP [1A EP [X(T )|Ft ]] = EP [1A X(t)] = Qt (A).

In this sense, the measures {Qt , t вИИ [0, T ]} are consistent. If X is strictly
positive, then Qt and Pt are equivalent for all t. To replace [0, T ] with [0, вИЮ)
556    B Appendix: Results from Stochastic Calculus

and still have all Qt consistent, we would need to add the requirement that
the martingale X be uniformly integrable (see (A.3).
   Suppose, now, that P and Q are equivalent probability measures on (вД¶, F )
with Radon-Nikodym derivative dQ/dP . DeпђБne
                                           
                        dQ            dQ
                              = EP       |Ft , t вИИ [0, T ].
                        dP t          dP

We claim that (dQ/dP )t is a martingale and equals the Radon-Nikodym deriv-
ative of the restriction of Q to Ft with respect to the restriction of P to Ft .
The martingale property is immediate from the deпђБnition. For the second
claim, observe that for any A вИИ Ft ,
                                                        
                 dQ                    dQ                  dQ
      E P 1A             = E P E P 1A     |Ft = EP 1A            = Q(A).
                 dP t                  dP                  dP

    We may summarize this discussion by saying that a nonnegative, unit-
mean martingale deпђБnes a consistent (with respect to {Ft , t вИИ [0, T ]}) family
of probability measures, and, conversely, the Radon-Nikodym derivatives for
such a family deпђБne a unit-mean, nonnegative martingale.
    The following simple rule for applying a change of measure to conditional
expectations arises frequently in mathematical пђБnance, most notably in ap-
plying a change of numeraire:
Proposition B.4.1 If P and Q are equivalent and EQ [|X|] < вИЮ, then
                                         вИТ1             
                                    dQ             dQ
                    EQ [X|Ft ] =              EP X    |Ft .
                                    dP   t         dP

    This follows immediately from the deпђБnition of (dQ/dP )t ; for an explicit
proof, see Musiela and Rutkowski [275], p.458.
    We can now state GirsanovвАЩs Theorem. In the following, let {W (t), t вИИ
[0, T ]} denote a standard k-dimensional Brownian motion on (вД¶, F , P ) and
let {FtW , t вИИ [0, T ]} denote the пђБltration generated by W augmented to include
all subsets of sets having P -probability 0.
Theorem B.4.1 (Girsanov Theorem). (i) Let ќ≥ be an k -valued process
adapted to {FtW } satisfying (B.19) for t вИИ [0, T ] and let
                               t                 t
            X(t) = exp вИТ 21       ќ≥(u)2 du +       ќ≥(u) dW (u) . (B.27)
                                0                 0

If EP [X(T )] = 1, then {X(t), t вИИ [0, T ]} is a martingale and the measure Q
on (вД¶, FTW ) deпђБned by
                                 dQ
                                      = X(T )
                                 dP
is equivalent to P . Under Q, the process
                                                            B.4 Change of Measure   557
                                            t
                   W Q (t) = W (t) вИТ             ќ≥(u) du,     t вИИ [0, T ]
                                            0

is a standard Brownian motion with respect to {FtW }. (ii) Conversely, if Q
is a probability measure on (вД¶, FTW ) equivalent to the restriction of P to FTW ,
then (dQ/dP )t admits the representation in (B.27) for some ќ≥ and W Q is a
standard Brownian motion under Q.
    Thus, the change of measure associated with a change of вАЬdriftвАЭ in a
Brownian motion over a пђБnite horizon is an absolutely continuous change of
measure, and every absolutely continuous change of measure for Brownian
motion is of this type.
    There are several diпђАerent results, applicable at diпђАerent levels of gen-
erality, known as GirsanovвАЩs Theorem. The formulation given here is more
precisely GirsanovвАЩs Theorem for Brownian motion and is closest to the one
proved in Section 5.2.2 of Hunt and Kennedy [191]. See Revuz and Yor [306]
for historical remarks as well as a more general formulation. Usually, only part
(i) of this result is called GirsanovвАЩs Theorem. Part (ii) is a consequence of
the Martingale Representation Theorem: as a positive unit-mean martingale,
(dQ/dP )t must be of the form (B.27) and then Q and P must be related
as in part (i). We have included the converse because of its importance in
the theory of derivative pricing. It assures us that when we change from the
objective probability measure to an equivalent martingale measure, the drifts
in asset prices may change but their volatilities may not.
    The requirement that EP [X(T )] = 1 in part (i) of Theorem B.4.1 is needed
to ensure that X is a martingale and not merely a local martingale; see Theo-
rem B.3.1. The Novikov condition is a widely cited suпђГcient condition for this
requirement (see, e.g., Section 3.5 of Karatzas and Shreve [207] for a proof):
Proposition B.4.2 (Novikov condition). If
                                                      
                                       T
                      E exp    1
                               2           ќ≥(u)2 du         < вИЮ,
                                   0


then X in (B.27) is a martingale on [0, T ].
C
Appendix: The Term Structure of Interest
Rates




The term structure of interest rates refers to the dependence of interest rates
on maturity. There are several equivalent ways of recording this relationship.
This appendix reviews terminology used for this purpose and describes some
of the most important interest rate derivative securities.


C.1 Term Structure Terminology
A unit of account (e.g., a dollar) invested at a continuously compounded rate
R grows to a value of eRT over the interval from time 0 to T . If instead the
investment earns simple interest over [0, T ], the value grows to 1 + RT over
this interval. The discount factors associated with continuous compounding
and simple interest are thus eвИТRT and 1/(1 + RT ), respectively.
    Many пђБxed income securities (including US Treasury bonds) follow an
intermediate convention in determining how interest accrues: an interest rate
is quoted on an annual basis with semi-annual compounding. In this case, an
initial investment of 1 grows to a value of 1+(R/2) at the end of half a year, to
a value of (1 + (R/2))2 at the end of one year, and so on. A bit more generally,
if we let ќі denote the fraction of a year over which interest is compounded
(with ќі = 1/2 and ќі = 1/4 the most important cases), the interest accrued
over nќі years is (1 + ќіR)n вИТ 1. Depending on what day-count convention is
used, the exact lengths of nominally equal six-month or three-month intervals
may vary; if we therefore generalize to allow unequal fractions ќі1 , ќі2 , . . ., then
at the end of n periods, an initial investment of 1 grows to a value of
                                   ,
                                   n
                                       (1 + ќіi R).
                                   i=1

The associated discount factor is the reciprocal of this expression.

Bonds, Yields, and Forward Rates

Let B(t, T ) denote the price at time t of a security making a single payment
of 1 at time T , T вЙ• t. This is a zero-coupon (or pure-discount) bond with
maturity T . A coupon bond makes payments at multiple dates and may be
viewed as a portfolio of zero-coupon bonds. A coupon bond paying ci at Ti ,
i = 1, . . . , n, and a principal payment of 1 at Tn has a value prior to maturity
of
                                               
                                               n
                          Bc (t) = B(t, Tn ) +     ci B(t, Ti ),             (C.1)
                                                i= (t)

with (t) the index of the next coupon date, deпђБned by

                                    T (t)вИТ1 < t вЙ§ T (t) .

    In a world with a constant continuously compounded interest rate, an
investor could replicate a zero-coupon bond with maturity T by investing
eвИТRT in an interest bearing account at time 0 and letting it grow to a value
of 1 at time T . It follows that B(0, T ) = eвИТRT in this setting.
    More generally, if the continuously compounded rate at time t (the short
rate) is given by a stochastic process r(t), an investment of 1 at time 0 grows
to a value of                              t

at time t. As explained in Chapter 1, the price of a bond is given by

                                                    0

the expectation taken under the risk-neutral measure. This is the only identity
in this section that involves probability.
    The yield of a bond may be interpreted as the interest rate implied by the
price of the bond; the yield therefore depends on the compounding convention
assumed for the implied interest rate. The continuously compounded yield
Y (t, T ) for a zero-coupon bond maturing at T is deпђБned by
                                                                  1

Yields are more commonly quoted on a semi-annual basis. The yield Yќі (t, T )

                                              C.1 Term Structure Terminology       561

                                                1
                           B(t, T ) =                       ,
                                         (1 + ќіYќі (t, T ))n

when n = (T вИТ t)/ќі is an integer. This extends to arbitrary t вИИ (0, T ) through
the convention that interest accrues linearly between compounding dates. The
yield of a coupon bond is similarly deпђБned by discounting the coupons as well
as the principal payment.
    A forward rate is an interest rate set today for borrowing or lending at
some date in the future. Consider, пђБrst, the case of a forward rate based on
simple interest, and let F (t, T1 , T2 ) denote the forward rate пђБxed at time t for
the interval [T1 , T2 ], with t < T1 < T2 . An investor entering into an agreement
at time t to borrow 1 at time T1 and repay the loan at time T2 pays interest
at rate F (t, T1 , T2 ). More explicitly, the investor receives 1 at T1 and pays
1 + F (t, T1 , T2 )(T2 вИТ T1 ) at T2 ; no other payments are exchanged.
    An arbitrage argument shows that forward rates are determined by bond
prices. At time t, an investor could buy a zero-coupon bond maturing at T1 ,
funding the purchase by issuing bonds maturing at T2 . If the number of bonds
k is chosen to satisfy
                                 kB(t, T2 ) = B(t, T1 ),
there is no net cashпђВow at time t. The investor will receive 1 at T1 and pay k
at T2 . To preclude arbitrage, the amount paid at T2 in this transaction must
be the same as the amount paid in the forward rate transaction, so

                          k = 1 + F (t, T1 , T2 )(T2 вИТ T1 ).

But k = B(t, T1 )/B(t, T2 ), so we conclude that

                                       1          B(t, T1 ) вИТ B(t, T2 )
                F (t, T1 , T2 ) =                                         .       (C.4)
                                    T2 вИТ T1             B(t, T2 )

    For much of the пђБnancial industry, the most important benchmark interest
rates are the London Inter-Bank OпђАered Rates or LIBOR. LIBOR is calcu-
lated daily through an average of rates oпђАered by select banks in London.
Separate rates are quoted for diпђАerent maturities (e.g., three months and six
months) and diпђАerent currencies. LIBOR is quoted as a simple annualized
interest rate. A forward LIBOR rate is a special case of (C.4) with a пђБxed
length ќі = T2 вИТ T1 for the accrual period, typically with ќі = 1/2 or ќі = 1/4.
Thus, the ќі-year forward LIBOR rate at time t with maturity T is

                                              1    B(t, T ) вИТ B(t, T + ќі)
            L(t, T ) = F (t, T, T + ќі) =                                      .   (C.5)
                                              ќі         B(t, T + ќі)

   In taking forward LIBOR rates as a special case of (C.4), we are ignoring
credit risk. The discussion leading to (C.4) assumes that bonds always make
their scheduled payments, that issuers never default. But the banks whose
rates set LIBOR may indeed default and this risk is presumably reпђВected in
562    C Appendix: The Term Structure of Interest Rates

the rates they oпђАer. Equation (C.5) ignores this feature. It is a convenient
simpliпђБcation often used in practice.
    In deriving (C.4) we assumed a simple forward rate, but we could just
as well have used a continuously compounded forward rate f (t, T1 , T2 ). The
interest paid must be the same regardless of the compounding convention, so
we must have

            exp (f (t, T1 , T2 )(T2 вИТ T1 )) вИТ 1 = F (t, T1 , T2 )(T2 вИТ T1 ).

With (C.4), this implies

                                        log B(t, T1 ) вИТ log B(t, T2 )
                    f (t, T1 , T2 ) =
                                                  T2 вИТ T1
for the continuously compounded forward rate for the accrual interval [T1 , T2 ].
    Now deпђБne f (t, T ) to be the continuously compounded forward rate пђБxed
at t for the instant T . This is the limit (assuming it exists) of f (t, T, T + h)
as h approaches 0, and is thus given by
                                             вИВ
                            f (t, T ) = вИТ      log B(t, T ).
                                            вИВT
Inverting this relationship and using B(T, T ) = 1, we get
                                                     
                                                     T
                       B(t, T ) = exp вИТ                  f (t, u) du .
                                                 t


Thus, the forward curve f (t, ¬Ј) is characterized by the property that discount-
ing along this curve reproduces time-t bond prices.
    Comparison with (C.3) reveals that
                                                T
                                       1
                         Y (t, T ) =                     f (t, u) du;
                                     T вИТt        t

i.e., that yields are averages over forward rates. This suggests that forward
rates are more fundamental quantities than yields and thus potentially a more
attractive starting point in building models of term structure dynamics.

Swaps and Swap Rates

In a standard interest rate swap, two parties agree to exchange payments tied
to a notional principal, one party paying interest at a пђВoating rate, the other
at a пђБxed rate. The principal is notional in the sense that it is never paid by
either party; it is merely used to determine the magnitudes of the payments.
    Fix a period ќі (e.g., half a year) and a set of dates Tn = nќі, n =
0, 1, . . . , M + 1. Consider a swap with payment dates T1 , . . . , TM+1 on a no-
tional principal of 100. At each Tn , the пђБxed-rate payer pays 100Rќі: this is the
                                          C.1 Term Structure Terminology       563

simple interest accrued on a principal of 100 over an interval of length ќі at an
annual rate of R. Denote by LnвИТ1 (TnвИТ1 ) the simple annualized interest rate
пђБxed at TnвИТ1 for the interval [TnвИТ1 , Tn ]. (Thus, LnвИТ1 (TnвИТ1 ) = L(TnвИТ1 , TnвИТ1 )
in (C.5), the ќі-year LIBOR rate пђБxed at TnвИТ1 .) The пђВoating-rate payer pays
100LnвИТ1(TnвИТ1 )ќі at each Tn . The exchange of payments terminates at TM+1 .
    Consider the value of the swap from the perspective of the party paying
пђВoating to receive пђБxed; the value to the other party has the same magnitude
but opposite sign. Although no principal is ever exchanged in the swap, valu-
ation is simpliпђБed if we pretend that each party pays the other 100 at TM+1 .
These two пђБctitious payments cancel each other and thus have no eпђАect on
the value of the swap. With this modiпђБcation, each partyвАЩs payments look like
those of a bond with a face value of 100, one bond having a пђБxed coupon
rate of R, the other having a пђВoating coupon. The value of the swap is the
diпђАerence between the values of the two bonds.
    At time T0 = 0, the value of the пђБxed rate bond is (see (C.1))

                             
                             M+1
                     100Rќі         B(0, Ti ) + 100B(0, TM+1 ).
                             i=1

To value the пђВoating rate bond, we argue that it can be replicated with an
initial investment of 100. Over the interval [0, T1 ], the initial investment earns
100ќіL0(0) in interest, precisely enough to pay the пђБrst coupon of the пђВoating
rate bond. The remaining 100 can then be invested at rate L1 (T1 ) until T2
to fund the next coupon while preserving the original 100. This reinvestment
process can be repeated until TM+1 when the 100 is used to pay the bondвАЩs
principal. Because the cashпђВows of the пђВoating rate bond can be replicated
with an initial investment of 100, we conclude that the value of the пђВoating
rate bond must itself be 100. From the perspective of the пђВoating-for-пђБxed
party, the value of the swap is the diпђАerence
                          
                          M+1
                  100Rќі         B(0, Ti ) + 100B(0, TM+1 ) вИТ 100             (C.6)
                          i=1

between the пђБxed and пђВoating rate bonds.
   By deпђБnition, the swap rate at time 0 (for payments at T1 , . . . , TM+1 ) is
the пђБxed rate R that makes the value of the swap (C.6) equal to zero. Both
parties would willingly enter into a swap at this rate without either party
having to make an additional payment to the otherвАФthe swap is costless.
From (C.6), we пђБnd that the swap rate is
                                       1 вИТ B(0, TM+1 )
                           S0 (0) =     $M+1           .

We have subscripted the swap rate by 0 to indicate that this is the rate for a
swap beginning at time 0 with payments at T1 , . . . , TM+1 . The same derivation
shows that the rate at Tn for a swap with payments at Tn+1 , . . . , TM+1 is

                                      1 вИТ B(Tn , TM+1 )

   The forward swap rate at time t < Tn for a swap with payment dates
Tn+1 , . . . , TM+1 is
                                B(t, Tn ) вИТ B(t, TM+1 )
                       Sn (t) =    $M+1                 .       (C.7)
                                 ќі i=n+1 B(t, Ti )
An extension of the argument leading to (C.6) shows that this is the rate
that makes a swap with payment dates Tn+1 , . . . , TM+1 costless at time t.
The forward swap rate for a single period (M = n) coincides with the forward
LIBOR rate L(t, Tn ); see (C.5).


C.2 Interest Rate Derivatives
The relationships among forward rates, swap rates, and bonds in Section C.1
are purely algebraic and independent of any stochastic assumptions about
interest rates. These relationships follow from static no-arbitrage arguments
that must hold at each valuation date t irrespective of the dynamics of the
term structure. This is not generally true of the value of interest rate futures
and options. The relationship between these interest rate derivatives and the
underlying term structure variables ordinarily depends on how one models
term structure dynamics.

Futures

We describe a slightly simpliпђБed version of Eurodollar futures, which are
among the most actively traded contracts in any market. The futures con-
tract has a settlement value of

                              100 ¬Ј (1 вИТ L(T, T ))

at the expiration date T , where L(T, T ) is the ќі-year LIBOR rate in (C.5).
Through an argument detailed in Section 8.D of DuпђГe [98], the time-t fu-
tures price associated with a futures contract is the risk-neutral conditional
expectation of the settlement value. DeпђБne
                                                                
                                          1      1
           LћВT (t) = E[L(T, T )|Ft] = E                 вИТ 1 |Ft ,        (C.8)
                                          ќі B(T, T + ќі)

with Ft the history of market prices up to time t, and the expectation taken
under the risk-neutral measure. The Eurodollar futures price at time t (for
settlement at T ) is then 100(1 вИТ LћВT (t)). The futures contract commits the
holder to making or receiving payments as the futures price пђВuctuates through
a process called resettlement or marking to market . In the idealized case of
                                                     C.2 Interest Rate Derivatives     565

continuous resettlement, each increment dLћВT triggers a payment of 100dLћВT by
the holder of the contract, with a negative payment interpreted as a dividend.
    The relationship (C.8) between the futures rate LћВT and bond prices de-
pends on stochastic elements of a model of the dynamics of the term structure,
whereas the relationship between forward rates and bond prices is essentially
algebraic and independent of the choice of model. The futures rate LћВT (t) is a
martingale under the risk-neutral measure; the forward rate L(t, T ) is a mar-
tingale under the forward measure for date T . The two processes coincide at
t = T.

Caps and Floors
An interest rate cap is a portfolio of options that serve to limit the interest paid
on a пђВoating rate liability over a set of consecutive periods. Each individual
option in the cap applies to a single period and is called a caplet. Because
the value of a cap is simply the sum of the values of its component caplets, it
suпђГces to discuss valuation of caplets.
   Consider, then, a caplet for the interval [T, T + ќі]. A party with a пђВoating
rate liability over this interval would pay interest equal to ќіL(T, T ) times the
principal at the end of the interval, with L(T, T ) the ќі-year rate in (C.5). A
security designed to limit the interest rate paid to some пђБxed level K refunds
the diпђАerence ќі(L(T, T )вИТK) (per unit of principal) if this diпђАerence is positive
and pays nothing otherwise. Thus, the payoпђА of a caplet is
                                      ќі(L(T, T ) вИТ K)+ ,                              (C.9)
and this payment is made at T + ќі. This can also be written as
                                                 +
                                  ќі
                      exp             f (T, T + u)     вИТ 1 вИТ ќіK       ,
                              0

using the curve f (T, ¬Ј) of instantaneous forward rates at time T .
    A пђВoor similarly sets a lower limit on interest payments. A single-period
пђВoor (a пђВoorlet) for the interval [T, T + ќі] pays ќі(K вИТ L(T, T ))+ at T + ќі.
    The caplet payoпђА (C.9) is received at time T + ќі but пђБxed at time T ; there
is no uncertainty in the payoпђА over the interval [T, T + ќі]. Hence, a security
paying (C.9) at time T + ќі is equivalent to one paying
          ќі
                   (L(T, T ) вИТ K)+ = ќіB(T, T + ќі)(L(T, T ) вИТ K)+                     (C.10)
     1 + ќіL(T, T )
at time T .
    The payoпђА of a caplet (or пђВoorlet) can be replicated by trading in two un-
derlying assets, the bonds maturing at T and T + ќі. Valuing a caplet entails
determining the initial cost of this trading strategy or, more directly, comput-
ing the expected present value of the capletвАЩs payoпђА. This requires specifying
a model of the dynamics of the term structure.
566    C Appendix: The Term Structure of Interest Rates

    By market convention, caplet prices are quoted through BlackвАЩs formula,
after Black [49]. This formula equates the time-t price of the caplet to

                                 log(L(t, T )/K) + ѕГ 2 (T вИТ t)/2
        ќіB(t, T + ќі) L(t, T )ќ¶                 вИЪ
                                             ѕГ T вИТt
                                   log(L(t, T )/K) вИТ ѕГ 2 (T вИТ t)/2
                             вИТKќ¶                вИЪ                        ,
                                               ѕГ T вИТt
with ќ¶ the standard cumulative normal distribution. This expression is what
one would obtain for the expectation of ќіB(t, T + ќі)(L(T, T ) вИТ K)+ if L(¬Ј, T )
satisпђБed
                            dL(t, T )
                                      = ѕГ dW (t),
                             L(t, T )
though this does not necessarily correspond to a price in the sense of the
theory of derivatives valuation. In practice, the Black formula is typically used
in reverse, to extract the вАЬimplied volatilityвАЭ parameter ѕГ from the market
prices of caps. An obvious modiпђБcation of the formula above produces the
Black formula for a пђВoor.

Swaptions

A swaption is an option to enter into a swap. A вАЬ2 √Ч 5вАЭ or вАЬ2-into-5вАЭ swaption
is a two-year option to enter into a пђБve-year swap. A bit more generically,
consider an option expiring at Tn to enter into a swap with payment dates
Tn+1 , . . . , TM+1 . Suppose the option grants the holder the right to pay пђБxed
and receive пђВoating on a notional principal of 1. Denote by R the пђБxed rate
speciпђБed in the underlying swap. At the expiration date Tn , the value of the
underlying swap is then

                                  
                                  M+1
               V (Tn ) = 1 вИТ Rќі           B(Tn , Ti ) вИТ B(Tn , TM+1 ),
                                  i=n+1

by the argument used to derive (C.6). The holder of the option exercises if
the swap has positive value and otherwise lets the option expire worthless.
We may therefore think of the swaption as an instrument that pays [V (Tn )]+
at Tn .
   Using (C.7), we пђБnd that

                                  
                                  M+1
                         +
                 [V (Tn )] = ќі           B(Tn , Ti )(Sn (Tn ) вИТ R)+ .        (C.11)
                                 i=n+1

Hence, the swaption looks like a call option on a swap rate. This formulation
is convenient because modeling the dynamics of forward swap rates is more
natural than modeling the dynamics of swap values, just as modeling the
                                                   C.2 Interest Rate Derivatives   567

dynamics of forward interest rates is more natural than modeling bond prices.
Furthermore, this representation makes it evident that a caplet is a special
case of a swaption by taking M = n and comparing with (C.10).
       A swaption can be replicated by trading in bonds maturing at Tn , Tn+1 ,
. . . , TM+1 . Valuing a swaption entails determining the initial cost of this
trading strategy or, more directly, computing the expected present value of
the swaption payoпђА. This requires specifying a model of the dynamics of the
term structure.
